iso-27701-a1-2.title: Identify and document purpose
iso-27701-a1-2.objective: Identify, document, and justify purpose for processing PII.
iso-27701-a1-2.applies_rationale: PB acts as controller for own corporate, telemetry, and lead data.
iso-27701-a1-2.pb_interpretation: On-prem reduces purposes (no analytics-on-customer-content) but PB still has multiple controller-side purposes: telemetry, support metadata, leads, marketing comms. Each must have a documented purpose.
iso-27701-a1-2.implementation: Documented purposes register; per data category list purpose, lawful basis, retention.
iso-27701-a1-2.evidence: Purposes register v1.x; auditor samples 5 purposes and traces to controls.

good_vs_audit_ready.audit_ready: Purposes tied to controls; reviewed annually; each entry traceable to lawful basis and retention rule.

iso-27701-a1-2.external_safe_phrasing: Each processing activity has a documented purpose and lawful basis.

source_refs.note: Controller privacy notice supports POPIA s 17-18 openness and collection-notice obligations.
Privacy programme operationalises POPIA accountability.

iso-27701-a1-3.title: Identify lawful basis
iso-27701-a1-3.objective: Determine and document the lawful basis for each PII processing activity.
iso-27701-a1-3.applies_rationale: Required for each controller-side processing activity.
iso-27701-a1-3.pb_interpretation: Customer's consent often inapplicable to vendor-side analytics; PB relies on contract performance or legitimate interest for support metadata, telemetry. LIA documented where legitimate interest used.
iso-27701-a1-3.implementation: Lawful-basis register; per processing entry; LIA documented where applicable.
iso-27701-a1-3.evidence: Register; sample entries; auditor cross-checks vs purpose register.
iso-27701-a1-3.external_safe_phrasing: Lawful basis is determined and documented for each activity.

source_refs.note: Controller role assignment maps to POPIA Information Officer duties.

iso-27701-a1-4.title: Determine when and how consent is obtained
iso-27701-a1-4.objective: Where consent is the lawful basis, document procedures for obtaining and recording consent.
iso-27701-a1-4.applies_rationale: Conditional on processing activities relying on consent.
iso-27701-a1-4.pb_interpretation: Marketing communications and voluntary telemetry rely on consent. Consent must be granular, evidenced, and withdrawable.
iso-27701-a1-4.implementation: Consent procedure; consent records system supporting withdrawal.
iso-27701-a1-4.evidence: Consent records sample; verify withdrawal works.
iso-27701-a1-4.external_safe_phrasing: Consent is captured, recorded and revocable.

iso-27701-a1-5.title: Privacy impact assessment (PIA/DPIA)
iso-27701-a1-5.objective: Conduct PIA/DPIA for high-risk processing activities.
iso-27701-a1-5.applies_rationale: High-risk processing across multiple PB activities.
iso-27701-a1-5.pb_interpretation: AI + sensitive client data + new technology implies DPIA likely required for most PB processing activities. PB conducts own DPIA per material activity AND provides DPIA template to assist customer.

role_applicability.processor_note: PB assists customer DPIA where customer is controller.

iso-27701-a1-5.implementation: DPIA process and register; per-activity DPIAs.
iso-27701-a1-5.evidence: DPIAs sampled; living documents updated on change.
iso-27701-a1-5.pitfall: One-off generic DPIA used for all activities.

good_vs_audit_ready.good: DPIA template exists; DPIAs done for major activities.
good_vs_audit_ready.audit_ready: Living DPIAs updated on change; tied to risk register; deployer-side DPIA aid template provided.

iso-27701-a1-5.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox conducts DPIAs for high-risk processing.

iso-27701-a1-6.title: Contracts with PII processors
iso-27701-a1-6.objective: Have contracts with processors meeting GDPR Art. 28 / POPIA s.21 equivalents.
iso-27701-a1-6.applies_rationale: Required for each processor PB engages.
iso-27701-a1-6.pb_interpretation: PB's own subprocessors (cloud, monitoring vendors, support tooling) governed by DPAs. Subprocessor register links to per-vendor DPAs.
iso-27701-a1-6.implementation: Subprocessor register; signed DPAs aligned with Article 28(3) requirements.
iso-27701-a1-6.evidence: DPA library; register; sample DPA terms.
iso-27701-a1-6.pitfall: Missing or stale DPAs; weak flow-down clauses.
iso-27701-a1-6.external_safe_phrasing: All processors are governed by data-protection agreements.

crosswalk.note: Supplier relationships — security baseline; privacy adds Art 28 / s.21 specifics
Addressing security in supplier agreements — DPA is privacy-specific instance

iso-27701-a1-7.title: Joint controllership
iso-27701-a1-7.objective: Determine and document joint controller arrangements where applicable.
iso-27701-a1-7.applies_rationale: Conditional on existence of joint controller arrangements.
iso-27701-a1-7.na_justification_template: N/A where no joint-controller relationships exist after review.
iso-27701-a1-7.pb_interpretation: Rarely PB; possible in specific integrations. Document if discovered.
iso-27701-a1-7.pitfall: Unrecognised joint controllership in product integrations.
iso-27701-a1-7.external_safe_phrasing: Joint controller arrangements documented when applicable.

iso-27701-a1-8.title: Records related to processing PII (ROPA)
iso-27701-a1-8.objective: Maintain records of processing activities equivalent to GDPR Art. 30.
iso-27701-a1-8.applies_rationale: Required for any processing of PII.
iso-27701-a1-8.pb_interpretation: ROPA covers controller-side activities; updated quarterly.
iso-27701-a1-8.evidence: ROPA sample; auditor traces an activity.
iso-27701-a1-8.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox maintains records of processing activities.

iso-27701-a1-9.title: Determining and fulfilling obligations to PII principals
iso-27701-a1-9.objective: Identify and meet obligations to PII principals.
iso-27701-a1-9.pb_interpretation: Tied to data subject rights process; obligations register.
iso-27701-a1-9.implementation: Obligations register tied to rights process.
iso-27701-a1-9.pitfall: Obligations identified but rights process not exercised.

iso-27701-a1-10.title: Determining information to be provided to PII principals
iso-27701-a1-10.objective: Provide privacy notice meeting transparency obligations (GDPR Art 12-14 etc.).
iso-27701-a1-10.applies_rationale: Required for transparency to data subjects.
iso-27701-a1-10.pb_interpretation: Public privacy notice should clearly distinguish PB-as-controller scope vs PB-as-processor scope. Reviewed annually.
iso-27701-a1-10.implementation: Public privacy notice published; layered (summary + detail).
iso-27701-a1-10.pitfall: Misleading notice that conflates controller and processor scopes.
iso-27701-a1-10.external_safe_phrasing: Provides a privacy notice covering controller-role processing.

iso-27701-a1-11.title: Providing information; modifying purpose; access; correction; erasure; objection; portability; automated decisions
iso-27701-a1-11.objective: End-to-end DSAR fulfilment and data subject rights.
iso-27701-a1-11.applies_rationale: Statutory rights apply across processing activities.
iso-27701-a1-11.pb_interpretation: Critical for credibility. DSARs against PB-controller-side data go to PB; DSARs against client data go to customer (PB assists per A.2.8).
iso-27701-a1-11.pitfall: No tooling; manual chase; missed timelines.
iso-27701-a1-11.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox operates a documented DSAR fulfilment process.

iso-27701-a1-12.title: Privacy by design and by default; collection limitation; minimisation; de-identification; deletion at end; temp files
iso-27701-a1-12.pb_interpretation: On-prem helps significantly; reduces vendor exposure. Privacy-by-default still must be evidenced (default settings, telemetry minimisation, temp file handling, deletion at end of processing).
iso-27701-a1-12.implementation: PbD design records; default settings docs; deletion runbook.
iso-27701-a1-12.pitfall: PbD claimed but not evidenced in design records.

good_vs_audit_ready.audit_ready: PbD documented across SDLC; default settings evidenced; deletion runbook tested.

iso-27701-a1-12.external_safe_phrasing: Privacy by design and default is evidenced in product design records.

crosswalk.note: Secure development lifecycle — PbD is the privacy lens of secure design
Secure system architecture and engineering principles

source_refs.note: PIA records cover POPIA s 71 automated-decision documentation.
PIA evidences POPIA accountability principle.

iso-27701-a1-13.title: Identify basis for transfer of PII between jurisdictions
iso-27701-a1-13.objective: Cross-border transfer basis (GDPR Ch. V; POPIA s.72).
iso-27701-a1-13.applies_rationale: Live for SA clients touching global model APIs; for any cross-border telemetry.
iso-27701-a1-13.pb_interpretation: On-prem in client country = no transfer. Teraco-SA = no transfer for SA-domiciled data. Outbound to non-adequate jurisdictions = SCCs/POPIA s.72 basis required.
iso-27701-a1-13.implementation: Transfer register; SCCs/IDTAs/POPIA s.72 evidence per transfer.
iso-27701-a1-13.external_safe_phrasing: Cross-border transfers are governed by documented legal bases.

crosswalk.note: Information transfer rules — privacy adds jurisdictional/legal basis layer

iso-27701-a1-14.title: Countries / international organisations to which PII can be transferred
iso-27701-a1-14.pb_interpretation: Recipient list annexed to privacy notice.
iso-27701-a1-14.pitfall: Hidden recipients (e.g., new sub-processor not added).
iso-27701-a1-14.external_safe_phrasing: Recipients of PII transfers are disclosed.

iso-27701-a1-15.title: Records of transfers
iso-27701-a1-15.applies_rationale: Required for accountability of cross-border transfers.
iso-27701-a1-15.pb_interpretation: Live log of transfers with destination, basis, date.

iso-27701-a1-16.title: Records of disclosures to third parties
iso-27701-a1-16.objective: Maintain a disclosure log including law-enforcement requests.
iso-27701-a1-16.pb_interpretation: Important for confidential firms. Law-enforcement requests reaching PB: narrow processor role limits scope, but disclosures still must be logged. Privileged-data escalation path critical.

role_applicability.processor_note: PB assists customer with disclosures of customer data per A.2.14.

iso-27701-a1-16.implementation: Disclosure log; law-enforcement-request SOP with counsel-review step.

crosswalk.note: Privacy and protection of PII — disclosure logging is a privacy-specific evidence

iso-27701-a2-2.title: Customer agreement
iso-27701-a2-2.objective: Process PII only on customer's documented instructions.
iso-27701-a2-2.pb_interpretation: Cornerstone of processor relationship. Even though customer 'operates' the box, PB acts on customer instruction during support, telemetry, and any vendor-touch activity. Master DPA + per-engagement annexes covers this.
iso-27701-a2-2.implementation: DPA / Customer Agreement defining instructions; annexes for specific engagements (deployment, support, RAG setup).
iso-27701-a2-2.evidence: Signed DPA library; sampled DPAs verified.
iso-27701-a2-2.pitfall: DPA missing or silent on AI specifics (no-train, RAG, support access).

good_vs_audit_ready.good: DPA template exists and is used.
good_vs_audit_ready.audit_ready: Master DPA + per-engagement annexes for all material processing flows; AI-specific clauses included.

iso-27701-a2-2.external_safe_phrasing: Processes client PII only on documented customer instructions.

source_refs.note: Processor controls map to POPIA s 20-21 operator confidentiality and security.

iso-27701-a2-3.title: Organization's purposes
iso-27701-a2-3.objective: Processor does not process PII for its own purposes beyond the contract.
iso-27701-a2-3.pb_interpretation: Critical for AI vendor: no model training on customer data. Telemetry must be designed to NOT capture customer content. The 'no-train' commitment must be engineering-true and contractually anchored.
iso-27701-a2-3.implementation: Engineering attestation: no customer-data training; telemetry minimisation specification.
iso-27701-a2-3.evidence: Telemetry spec; design doc; engineering attestations.
iso-27701-a2-3.pitfall: Implicit/ambiguous re training; customer expected to trust without technical evidence.
iso-27701-a2-3.external_safe_phrasing: Customer content is not used for model training.

source_refs.note: Processor role assignment supports POPIA operator-side accountability.

iso-27701-a2-4.title: Marketing and advertising use
iso-27701-a2-4.objective: No marketing or advertising use of PII unless explicit consent.
iso-27701-a2-4.implementation: Policy prohibiting; technical controls preventing use of customer data for marketing.
iso-27701-a2-4.pitfall: Hidden marketing flags or inferred uses.
iso-27701-a2-4.external_safe_phrasing: Customer PII is not used for marketing.

iso-27701-a2-5.title: Infringing instructions
iso-27701-a2-5.objective: Notify controller if instruction may infringe law.
iso-27701-a2-5.pb_interpretation: Customers may request data export PB cannot legally action; documented escalation procedure.
iso-27701-a2-5.implementation: Procedure to flag illegal instructions; counsel review step.
iso-27701-a2-5.pitfall: No procedure; engineers comply without review.
iso-27701-a2-5.external_safe_phrasing: Identifies and notifies customers of potentially infringing instructions.

iso-27701-a2-6.title: Confidentiality undertakings
iso-27701-a2-6.objective: Persons authorised to process PII committed to confidentiality.
iso-27701-a2-6.applies_rationale: Required for any persons accessing customer PII.
iso-27701-a2-6.pb_interpretation: All support staff and contractors; NDAs and HR records evidence coverage.
iso-27701-a2-6.external_safe_phrasing: All personnel processing customer data are under confidentiality.

crosswalk.note: Confidentiality or non-disclosure agreements — same control with privacy emphasis

iso-27701-a2-7.title: Records related to processing on behalf of customer (Art 30(2))
iso-27701-a2-7.pb_interpretation: Per-customer activity log; available to auditor.

iso-27701-a2-8.title: Obligations to PII principals (assist controller)
iso-27701-a2-8.objective: Assist customer with DSARs, breach notifications, DPIA cooperation.
iso-27701-a2-8.applies_rationale: Required to support customer's controller-side obligations.
iso-27701-a2-8.pb_interpretation: Critical. On-prem may give customer direct access for many DSARs, but PB still must assist when telemetry/support involved. SLA defined in DPA.
iso-27701-a2-8.pitfall: Customer left to fend off DSARs alone.
iso-27701-a2-8.external_safe_phrasing: Assists customers in fulfilling data-subject rights requests.

crosswalk.note: Privacy and protection of PII — assist obligations are operational instance

iso-27701-a2-9.title: Temporary files
iso-27701-a2-9.objective: Manage and delete temporary files containing PII.
iso-27701-a2-9.applies_rationale: Required to prevent retention beyond purpose.
iso-27701-a2-9.pb_interpretation: Telemetry, debug bundles, support session captures are common temp-file sources. Audit support-bundle / temp-file lifecycle.
iso-27701-a2-9.external_safe_phrasing: Temporary files are subject to documented deletion procedures.

iso-27701-a2-10.title: Return, transfer, disposal of PII
iso-27701-a2-10.objective: At end of contract: delete or return PII; certify deletion.
iso-27701-a2-10.applies_rationale: Required at end of every processor engagement.
iso-27701-a2-10.pb_interpretation: On-prem: most data is already with customer. Vendor-side artefacts (telemetry, support bundles, backups, RAG corpora when PB-managed) must be deleted/returned with certificate.
iso-27701-a2-10.pitfall: 'Yeah we deleted it' verbally without certificate.
iso-27701-a2-10.external_safe_phrasing: Provides return/deletion of customer PII at end of service.

iso-27701-a2-11.title: Basis for transfer of PII between jurisdictions (processor scope)
iso-27701-a2-11.objective: Document lawful basis for processor-side cross-border transfers.
iso-27701-a2-11.implementation: Annex with locations; per customer instruction.
iso-27701-a2-11.external_safe_phrasing: Processor-side transfers are governed and disclosed.

iso-27701-a2-12.title: Countries to which PII may be transferred (disclose to customer)
iso-27701-a2-12.objective: Disclose to customer the countries where PII may be processed.
iso-27701-a2-12.pb_interpretation: List of countries annexed to DPA.
iso-27701-a2-12.implementation: List of countries; updated as sub-processors change.

iso-27701-a2-13.title: Records of disclosures (processor)
iso-27701-a2-13.objective: Same as controller side: maintain disclosure log.

iso-27701-a2-14.title: Notification of PII disclosure requests
iso-27701-a2-14.objective: Notify controller of law-enforcement disclosure requests where lawful.
iso-27701-a2-14.applies_rationale: Required to allow controller to take appropriate action.
iso-27701-a2-14.pb_interpretation: LE-request SOP with counsel review; customer notified per DPA terms.
iso-27701-a2-14.external_safe_phrasing: Customers are notified of disclosure requests where lawful.

iso-27701-a2-15.title: Legally binding PII disclosures
iso-27701-a2-15.objective: Comply only when legally binding; counsel review.
iso-27701-a2-15.pb_interpretation: Legal-review step before any compelled disclosure.
iso-27701-a2-15.external_safe_phrasing: Legally binding disclosure requests are reviewed by counsel.

iso-27701-a2-16.title: Disclosure of subcontractors
iso-27701-a2-16.objective: Disclose sub-processors before use; allow customer to consent or object.
iso-27701-a2-16.pb_interpretation: Critical for AI vendor (model API providers, monitoring vendors). Pure on-prem with no remote support → minimal sub-processors. Once telemetry/support added → register grows. Public sub-processor register with change-notice channel.
iso-27701-a2-16.external_safe_phrasing: Maintains a published subprocessor register with change notifications.

crosswalk.note: Supplier relationships — sub-processor register is privacy operationalisation
Managing security in the ICT supply chain

iso-27701-a2-17.title: Engagement of a subcontractor (flow-down obligations)
iso-27701-a2-17.objective: Same data-protection obligations flow down to sub-processors.
iso-27701-a2-17.pb_interpretation: Flow-down clauses in sub-processor DPAs are equivalent to customer DPAs.
iso-27701-a2-17.pitfall: Weak flow-down (e.g., processor accepts customer DPA but flows down only generic clauses).
iso-27701-a2-17.external_safe_phrasing: Subprocessor obligations equivalent to customer DPAs.

crosswalk.note: Addressing security in supplier agreements — DPA flow-down is the privacy form

iso-27701-a2-18.title: Change of subcontractor (notice and objection)
iso-27701-a2-18.objective: Notify customer of sub-processor changes; allow objection per DPA.
iso-27701-a2-18.pb_interpretation: Defined notice window in DPA; objection mechanism.
iso-27701-a2-18.implementation: Change-notice procedure; defined window (e.g., 30 days advance notice).
iso-27701-a2-18.external_safe_phrasing: Subprocessor changes are notified in advance.

iso-27701-a3-5.7.title: Threat intelligence
iso-27701-a3-5.7.objective: Collect and use threat intelligence to inform privacy and security risk.
iso-27701-a3-5.7.pb_interpretation: Privacy-aware threat intel feed; integrated with privacy risk register.
iso-27701-a3-5.7.implementation: Threat-intel feed; integration with risk register.

crosswalk.note: ISO/IEC 27701:2025 A.3 controls explicitly align to ISO/IEC 27002:2022 themes — same numbering, privacy lens.

iso-27701-a3-5.16-8.5.title: Identity management & privileged access
iso-27701-a3-5.16-8.5.objective: Strong authentication, privileged-access management for support roles.
iso-27701-a3-5.16-8.5.applies_rationale: Required for any vendor-side access to customer environments.
iso-27701-a3-5.16-8.5.pb_interpretation: Cornerstone for support-access risk. Support engineers' access to client environments must be MFA + JIT + recorded. PAM logs evidence each session.
iso-27701-a3-5.16-8.5.implementation: JIT/PAM for support; SSO; MFA; session recording.

good_vs_audit_ready.audit_ready: JIT credential issuance; per-session approval; session recording; quarterly access review.

iso-27701-a3-5.16-8.5.external_safe_phrasing: Vendor support access is governed by privileged-access management.

crosswalk.note: ISO/IEC 27701:2025 A.3 controls explicitly align to ISO/IEC 27002:2022 themes — same numbering, privacy lens.

iso-27701-a3-5.14-5.15.title: Information transfer security
iso-27701-a3-5.14-5.15.applies_rationale: Required for any vendor↔customer data transfer.
iso-27701-a3-5.14-5.15.pb_interpretation: Backup support, log shipping, telemetry — all must use encrypted channels with allow- listed destinations. Customer-controllable.
iso-27701-a3-5.14-5.15.implementation: Encrypted channels; signed packages; allow-listed destinations.
iso-27701-a3-5.14-5.15.pitfall: Telemetry over plain HTTP; unsigned updates.
iso-27701-a3-5.14-5.15.external_safe_phrasing: Vendor↔customer transfers are encrypted and integrity-protected.

crosswalk.note: ISO/IEC 27701:2025 A.3 controls explicitly align to ISO/IEC 27002:2022 themes — same numbering, privacy lens.

iso-27701-a3-8.15-8.17.title: Logging & monitoring
iso-27701-a3-8.15-8.17.applies_rationale: Required for accountability and incident response.
iso-27701-a3-8.15-8.17.pb_interpretation: Critical for support-access auditability. Customer keeps primary logs on-prem; PB-side logs minimised and PII-redacted by default.
iso-27701-a3-8.15-8.17.implementation: Logging specification including PII minimisation and redaction defaults.
iso-27701-a3-8.15-8.17.pitfall: Logs include raw client content (e.g., prompts, outputs).
iso-27701-a3-8.15-8.17.external_safe_phrasing: Logs are designed to minimise PII while retaining auditability.

crosswalk.note: ISO/IEC 27701:2025 A.3 controls explicitly align to ISO/IEC 27002:2022 themes — same numbering, privacy lens.

iso-27701-a3-8.24.title: Cryptography (incl. KMS)
iso-27701-a3-8.24.applies_rationale: Required for any PII at rest or in transit.
iso-27701-a3-8.24.pb_interpretation: Essential for confidential firms. Customer-managed keys preferred; PB documents key management options (CMK vs vendor-managed) in deployment guide.
iso-27701-a3-8.24.implementation: Crypto policy; KMS architecture; customer-managed keys offered.
iso-27701-a3-8.24.pitfall: Vendor-controlled keys forced; no CMK option.

crosswalk.note: ISO/IEC 27701:2025 A.3 controls explicitly align to ISO/IEC 27002:2022 themes — same numbering, privacy lens.

iso-27701-a3-8.25-8.30.title: Secure development (SDL incl. PbD)
iso-27701-a3-8.25-8.30.objective: Privacy by design embedded in SDLC.
iso-27701-a3-8.25-8.30.applies_rationale: Required to evidence PbD architectural posture.
iso-27701-a3-8.25-8.30.pb_interpretation: Tied to A.1.12. PbD checklist in PR template; privacy review per material change.
iso-27701-a3-8.25-8.30.implementation: PbD checklist in PR template; SDLC documentation.
iso-27701-a3-8.25-8.30.external_safe_phrasing: Privacy by design is embedded in the SDLC.

crosswalk.note: ISO/IEC 27701:2025 A.3 controls explicitly align to ISO/IEC 27002:2022 themes — same numbering, privacy lens.

iso-27701-a3-8.13.title: Backup integrity & access
iso-27701-a3-8.13.pb_interpretation: When PB performs backups (optional service), full A.2 processor obligations attach. Encrypted backups; tested restore.
iso-27701-a3-8.13.cadence: Per backup cycle + annual restore test
iso-27701-a3-8.13.pitfall: Untested backups; backups offshore without basis.
iso-27701-a3-8.13.external_safe_phrasing: Backup operations follow documented privacy-preserving procedures.

crosswalk.note: ISO/IEC 27701:2025 A.3 controls explicitly align to ISO/IEC 27002:2022 themes — same numbering, privacy lens.

iso-27701-a3-5.24-5.27.title: Privacy incident management
iso-27701-a3-5.24-5.27.applies_rationale: Required for accountability and regulatory compliance.
iso-27701-a3-5.24-5.27.pb_interpretation: Privacy track within IR plan; notification SLA in DPAs (e.g., <24h customer, <72h regulator); coordination with customer.
iso-27701-a3-5.24-5.27.implementation: IR plan with privacy track; notification SLAs in DPAs.
iso-27701-a3-5.24-5.27.external_safe_phrasing: Privacy incidents trigger documented notification SLAs.

crosswalk.note: ISO/IEC 27701:2025 A.3 controls explicitly align to ISO/IEC 27002:2022 themes — same numbering, privacy lens.

iso-27701-a3-5.19-5.23.title: Supplier / cloud security
iso-27701-a3-5.19-5.23.objective: Third-party / cloud security with privacy lens.
iso-27701-a3-5.19-5.23.pb_interpretation: All optional support layers introduce supplier dependencies; subject to privacy-aware due diligence.
iso-27701-a3-5.19-5.23.pitfall: Stale supplier register; no privacy review on renewal.
iso-27701-a3-5.19-5.23.external_safe_phrasing: Suppliers are subject to privacy-aware due diligence.

crosswalk.note: ISO/IEC 27701:2025 A.3 controls explicitly align to ISO/IEC 27002:2022 themes — same numbering, privacy lens.

iso-27701-a3-7.x.title: Physical security
iso-27701-a3-7.x.objective: Physical security of PII processing facilities.
iso-27701-a3-7.x.applies_rationale: Customer-owned in on-prem default; inherited from host in Teraco scenario.
iso-27701-a3-7.x.pb_interpretation: On-prem default: less directly applicable (customer-owned facility). Teraco scenario: inherits Teraco's ISAE 3402 / PCI DSS attestations; PB documents the inheritance.
iso-27701-a3-7.x.implementation: Physical-security mapping document; Teraco compliance pack inheritance for hosted scenario.
iso-27701-a3-7.x.pitfall: Asserted but not documented; claiming Teraco's certifications as PB's own.
iso-27701-a3-7.x.external_safe_phrasing: Physical security relies on customer-owned facility or Teraco-attested colocation.

crosswalk.note: ISO/IEC 27701:2025 A.3 controls explicitly align to ISO/IEC 27002:2022 themes — same numbering, privacy lens.

iso-27701-a3-5.31-5.34.title: Compliance / records (legal compliance)
iso-27701-a3-5.31-5.34.objective: Track legal and regulatory compliance obligations.
iso-27701-a3-5.31-5.34.pb_interpretation: Compliance register tracking GDPR, POPIA, AI Act, sectoral regulations.

crosswalk.note: ISO/IEC 27701:2025 A.3 controls explicitly align to ISO/IEC 27002:2022 themes — same numbering, privacy lens.

effective_dates.notes: Legacy GPAI models (placed on market before this date) have until 2 Aug 2027.

effective_dates.milestone: High-risk Annex III + transparency + most provider/deployer duties
effective_dates.notes: Digital Omnibus (Nov 2025 proposal) may delay this; plan to original date.
Legacy GPAI deadline. Most product-embedded high-risk obligations.

pb_application.why_chosen: EU customers in regulated sectors (legal, financial, healthcare, public sector) will require AI Act alignment as a procurement gate from mid-2026 onwards. On-prem reduces some risks but does not remove provider duties, prohibited-practice screening, AI literacy obligations, or transparency duties. Non-compliance penalties are the highest of any AI regulation globally (€35M / 7% turnover for Art 5 breaches).

on_prem.rationale: Customer hardware in EU customer environments = putting into service in the EU. Outputs used in EU = extraterritorial reach (Art 2(1)(c)). PB role: typically provider of AI system. Customer role: deployer. Customer-driven Annex III use triggers role-flip (Art 25(1)(c)) where customer becomes provider of high-risk system.

ai_factory.rationale: Partner DC may or may not be in EU jurisdiction. If non-EU PB serves EU customers, authorised representative required for high-risk variants (Art 22). PB acts as provider; customer as deployer; partner DC as supplier governed contractually.

top_risks: Sales rep saying 'yes you can use it for HR ranking' triggers Art 25(1)(c) role-flip exposure for both PB and customer
Skipping AI literacy programme (Art 4) — already binding since Feb 2025
Failing to screen prohibited practices (Art 5) — €35M / 7% penalty tier
Stripping upstream GPAI documentation when integrating third-party models (Art 53 pass-through)
Telemetry/support access undermining the on-prem story without contractual scoping
Treating on-prem as 'exempt' — Act applies regardless of hosting topology
Missing Art 50(2) machine-readable marking for generative outputs from Aug 2026

open_questions.question: Do we have or actively pursue EU customers?
candidate_answers: Yes — EU is a primary target market
open_questions.notes: Determines applicability scope. Even one EU customer brings entire framework into scope. Outputs used in EU also count under Art 2(1)(c) extraterritorial reach.

open_questions.question: Will we appoint an EU Authorised Representative?
candidate_answers: Yes — for any high-risk variant (mandatory under Art 22)
Yes — for GPAI provider role (mandatory under Art 54 if triggered)
Voluntary EU contact for non-high-risk customer expectation
open_questions.notes: Required for non-EU providers shipping high-risk systems or GPAI models to EU. Even where not legally required, EU customers expect an EU contact.

open_questions.question: Will we offer a high-risk-ready SKU/variant?
candidate_answers: Yes — productise a high-risk-ready configuration with full Art 8-15 compliance
No — explicitly out of scope; route customers to refusal or contractual carve-out
open_questions.notes: Determines whether Articles 8-17 + 49 are in scope as binding obligations or as aspirational scaffolding. Strong commercial decision; impacts AUP, MSA, and product roadmap.

open_questions.question: Will we sign the GPAI Code of Practice if we trigger GPAI provider status?
candidate_answers: Yes — sign at first GPAI provider trigger
No — demonstrate compliance by other adequate means
Decide per case based on upstream supplier signatures
open_questions.notes: GPAI CoP is voluntary but creates presumption of compliance. Non-signatories must demonstrate compliance 'by other adequate means' — heavier evidence burden.

open_questions.question: Will we ship fine-tuned models, and if so, who owns the GPAI provider duties?
candidate_answers: We do not fine-tune; only ship third-party models unmodified
We fine-tune below the one-third compute threshold (no GPAI provider role)
We fine-tune above threshold and assume GPAI provider duties
Customer fine-tunes, customer owns GPAI provider role for modifications
open_questions.notes: One-third compute threshold from Commission GPAI Guidelines (Jul 2025). Determines whether PB triggers GPAI provider role for modifications.

open_questions.question: What technical approach for Art 50(2) machine-readable output marking?
candidate_answers: Wait for final Code of Practice (June 2026) and adopt then
open_questions.notes: Art 50(2) binding from Aug 2026. Code of Practice on Marking and Labelling — first draft Dec 2025, second draft Mar 2026, final June 2026.

open_questions.question: What is the AUP template for EU deployments?
candidate_answers: Single global AUP with EU-specific addendum
open_questions.notes: AUP is the primary contractual mechanism for excluding Annex III use, preventing Art 25(1)(c) role-flip surprises, and screening Art 5 prohibited practices.

open_questions.question: Which DC partner(s) for AI Factory deployments?
open_questions.notes: If AI Factory is hosted at EU-based DC partner, AR requirement may be relaxed. If non-EU partner, full Art 22 AR mandate applies for high-risk variants.

open_questions.question: Which legal jurisdiction(s) for AI Factory hosting?
open_questions.notes: EU jurisdiction simplifies Art 2 scope; non-EU triggers extraterritorial reach for EU customers/outputs.

evidence_families.name: AI Act Scope Memo
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Anchor for whether the framework applies at all.

evidence_families.name: Role Allocation Memo
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Establishes who carries which obligations under the Act.

evidence_families.name: Risk Classification Register
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Determines whether Art 8-15 obligations apply.

evidence_families.name: Prohibited-Use Screening Checklist
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Article 5 carries highest penalty tier; screening is the defence.

evidence_families.name: AI Literacy Programme
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Art 4 binding since Feb 2025. Already in scope.

evidence_families.name: Model Inventory (per-SKU, per-version)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Foundation for GPAI documentation pass-through and Art 53 compliance.

evidence_families.name: Intended Purpose Register & Instructions for Use
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Defines the scope against which Art 25 role-flip is measured.

evidence_families.name: Technical Documentation Skeleton (Annex IV)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Required for high-risk; readiness baseline for non-high-risk.

evidence_families.name: Human-Oversight Design Note
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Art 14 high-risk requirement; good practice for all systems.

evidence_families.name: Data Governance Standard
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Art 10 high-risk requirement; defensible posture for all systems.

evidence_families.name: Output-Marking / Labelling Specification (Article 50(2))
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Binding from Aug 2026 for all generative AI providers.

evidence_families.name: Logging & Traceability Spec
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Art 12 high-risk + Art 26(6) deployer 6-month minimum.

evidence_families.name: Post-Market Monitoring Plan
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Art 72 obligation for high-risk; good practice for all.

evidence_families.name: Incident Response & Serious-Incident Decision Tree
examples: Severity matrix with timing buckets (2/10/15 days)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Art 73 reporting timing rules; failure = visible non-compliance.

evidence_families.name: Quality Management Materials
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Art 17 high-risk requirement; ISO 42001 alignment.

evidence_families.name: Supplier / Model-Provider Due Diligence Pack
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Art 53 documentation chain; prevents inheriting upstream gaps.

evidence_families.name: Change-Control Records
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Art 18 record-keeping; substantial-modification triggers re-conformity.

evidence_families.name: Customer Deployment Guidance / Deployer Pack
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Enables customer to discharge Art 26-27 deployer duties.

evidence_families.name: Trust-Pack Language Guide
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Prevents marketing overclaim that triggers regulatory exposure.

evidence_families.name: Acceptable Use Policy (AUP)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Primary contractual defence against Art 25(1)(c) role-flip.

scenarios.name: Pure on-prem enterprise chat assistant, customer-supplied data only
scenarios.description: PrivateBox runs locally inside customer's environment. Customer's employees use it for general drafting, Q&A, summarisation. No third-party model invoked over the internet at runtime. No customer data leaves the customer environment.
scenarios.legal_basis: Art 2(1)(a)–(b); Art 3(1) AI system definition; Commission Guidelines on AI System Definition (Feb 2025).
scenarios.on_prem_note: On-prem deployment does not change 'placing on the market' / 'putting into service'. Act applies.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: Art 4 literacy, Art 5 prohibited-use screening, AI system documentation, Art 50 transparency by design. Customer: deployer-side Art 4 literacy, lawful use, workplace consultation if applicable.
scenarios.common_misclassification: Believing 'on-prem means out of scope'. Wrong.
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: PrivateBox is an AI system within the meaning of Article 3(1) of the EU AI Act and is placed on the market in the EU when delivered to EU customers, regardless of the on-prem deployment topology.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Putting into service in the EU.
Both PB and customer have AI literacy duty.
Prohibited-use screening at customer onboarding.
Persistent disclosure that user is interacting with AI.

scenarios.name: On-prem enterprise search / RAG over customer documents
scenarios.description: Same as A but with retrieval-augmented generation over customer's own documents. Customer corpus stays local; no internet retrieval.
scenarios.on_prem_note: Same as A — RAG over local corpus does not change scope or topology analysis.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: same as A plus document-source citation, retrieval guardrails. Customer: same as A plus corpus quality (Art 16 deployer's input-data appropriateness duty applies if high-risk variant).
scenarios.common_misclassification: Treating RAG as 'just search' — outputs are still AI generation under Art 50(2).
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: PrivateBox supports retrieval-augmented generation over customer-supplied documents within the customer environment, with citations preserved at point of use.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Same as A.
Same as A.
Same as A.
Same as A; output marking applies to generated summaries.

scenarios.name: PrivateBox embeds a third-party GPAI model unmodified
scenarios.description: PrivateBox bundles an unmodified third-party GPAI model (e.g., Llama, Mistral) as the runtime LLM. Model is shipped to customer environment via the PrivateBox installer.
scenarios.legal_basis: Art 2; Art 3(63) GPAI model definition; Art 53 GPAI provider documentation; Art 25(4) supplier agreements.
scenarios.on_prem_note: Topology irrelevant — GPAI obligations attach to model layer.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: due diligence on upstream GPAI provider, preserve Annex XII downstream documentation, pass through to customer, do not strip provenance. Upstream GPAI provider: Art 53 documentation duties (their problem, not PB's). Customer: deployer duties.
scenarios.common_misclassification: Confusing GPAI **model** obligations with GPAI **system** obligations. PB ships a system; the model is upstream.
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: PrivateBox documents the third-party general-purpose AI models it integrates and preserves the downstream documentation those providers supply under Article 53.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Confirm model is GPAI under Art 3(63) (≥10²³ FLOP + generative).
Pass-through duty: preserve and forward Annex XII docs.
Art 25(4) written-agreement template for upstream supplier.

scenarios.name: PrivateBox fine-tunes / substantially modifies a model and ships modified weights
scenarios.description: PrivateBox takes a base GPAI model, fine-tunes or substantially modifies it, and ships the modified model as part of the platform.
scenarios.legal_basis: Art 25(1)(b) substantial modification; Art 53 (own GPAI duties for modifications); Annex XI, XII; Commission GPAI Guidelines (Jul 2025) one-third compute threshold.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: documentation of fine-tune dataset, copyright policy for fine-tune data, training-data summary covering the fine-tune, evaluation results, intended purpose; decide whether to sign GPAI Code of Practice. Customer: deployer obligations.
scenarios.common_misclassification: Assuming 'we just fine-tuned' means 'we have no GPAI duties'. Threshold is one-third of compute used to train base.
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: PrivateBox-modified models are accompanied by documentation describing the modifications, the data used for fine-tuning, and a copyright policy applicable to those modifications.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Art 25(1)(b) substantial modification triggers GPAI provider role.
Annex XI internal docs + Annex XII downstream pack + copyright policy + training-data summary, all specific to the fine-tune.
AR required if PB is non-EU and triggers GPAI provider role.
Decide whether to sign GPAI CoP.

scenarios.name: Customer uses PrivateBox to build an HR screening / ranking tool
scenarios.description: Customer uses PrivateBox to ingest CVs and produce candidate rankings, sift, scoring, or interview questions personalised to candidates.
scenarios.legal_basis: Annex III(4)(a)–(b) recruitment/selection; Art 6(2); Art 6(3) profiling carve-out unavailable; Art 25(1)(c) role-flip.
scenarios.on_prem_note: On-prem does not lower the risk class.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: clear instructions stating the system is **not intended** for high-risk Annex III use without further deployer assessment; technical preconditions (logging, oversight) for high-risk operation; pass-through GPAI documentation. Customer: full Article 9-15 compliance, Art 49 registration, Art 27 FRIA, Art 26(7) worker information.
scenarios.common_misclassification: Letting the customer slip into Annex III use without acknowledging it; PB pulled into Art 25(1)(c) provider status because a salesperson said 'yes you can use it for that'.
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: PrivateBox is not intended to be used as a high-risk AI system under Annex III of the EU AI Act, including for HR screening, ranking, or selection. Customers who choose to operate PrivateBox in such a use case become providers of a high-risk AI system under Article 25(1)(c) and must independently meet the obligations in Articles 9-15, 16, 26, 27, and 49.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Annex III(4) classification.
Art 25(1)(c) role-flip: customer becomes provider of high-risk system.
Customer-side deployer duties.
FRIA required.
Customer registers in EU database.

scenarios.name: Customer uses PrivateBox for credit scoring / insurance pricing decisions
scenarios.description: Customer (bank or insurer) uses PrivateBox to support creditworthiness decisions or pricing of life/health insurance for natural persons.
scenarios.legal_basis: Annex III(5)(b)/(c) creditworthiness, life/health insurance pricing; Art 25(1)(c); DORA in parallel.
scenarios.on_prem_note: Reduces sub-processor and DORA-third-party risks but does not lower AI Act class.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: instructions excluding such use without sign-off; logging hooks; deployer oversight tooling. Customer: full provider obligations, FRIA (financial actors listed in Art 27), registration.
scenarios.common_misclassification: Same as E. Customer may try to claim 'fraud detection' carve-out incorrectly — narrow.
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: PrivateBox is not intended to be used to evaluate the creditworthiness of natural persons or to assess risk and price life or health insurance. Customers who use PrivateBox for those purposes assume provider obligations for a high-risk AI system.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Annex III(5)(b)/(c).
Role-flip same as E.
FRIA required for financial actors.

scenarios.name: Healthcare / medical decision support
scenarios.description: Customer (hospital, clinic, pharma) uses PrivateBox for clinical decision support, triage, or patient-facing chat.
scenarios.legal_basis: Art 6(1) + Annex I (MDR/IVDR safety components); Annex III(5)(d) eligibility for essential public services.
scenarios.on_prem_note: Reduces patient-data exposure. Does not by itself lower risk class.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: written instructions for use, AUP excluding medical-device incorporation absent contractual route, logging hooks. Customer: clinical validation, MDR/IVDR conformity if applicable. Note Art 25(3): if customer integrates into a regulated medical device, the device manufacturer becomes the provider of the AI system.
scenarios.common_misclassification: Underestimating MDR/IVDR overlap. Treating 'administrative' hospital tools as not high-risk when in fact they touch eligibility decisions.
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: PrivateBox is not a medical device and is not intended to be a safety component of any product subject to EU medical-device legislation. Use cases requiring conformity under MDR/IVDR or under Annex III(5)(d) require a separate scoping discussion.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Annex I + Annex III(5)(d).
Art 25(3) device-manufacturer-as-provider rule.
FRIA where public-sector patient services.

scenarios.name: Public-facing chatbot / customer-service assistant
scenarios.description: PrivateBox is exposed to end-users (customers, citizens) as a chatbot or assistant.
scenarios.legal_basis: Art 50(1) provider duty; Art 50(2) synthetic-output marking; Art 50(4) deepfake/public-interest text (deployer side).
scenarios.on_prem_note: Irrelevant to Art 50: duty travels with system and deployer's use, not hosting model.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: in-product disclosure UI; machine-readable marking of generated outputs; persistent disclosure surface; technical guidance for deployer. Customer: deepfake labelling (Art 50(4)); ensuring users informed at first interaction (Art 50(5)).
scenarios.common_misclassification: Treating 'obvious it's AI' as universal carve-out — burden of proof is on the provider.
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: PrivateBox surfaces a clear and persistent indication that users are interacting with an AI system, and where it generates synthetic content, marks outputs in a machine-readable format. The product is designed in alignment with Article 50, applicable from 2 August 2026.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Art 50(1) provider duty + Art 50(2) marking.

scenarios.name: Content generation / synthetic media features
scenarios.description: PrivateBox produces drafts, summaries, images, audio, or video.
scenarios.legal_basis: Art 50(2) machine-readable marking; Art 50(4) deepfakes/public-interest text; Code of Practice on Marking and Labelling (voluntary).
scenarios.on_prem_note: Marking duty applies regardless of hosting topology.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: marking pipeline (metadata, watermark, fingerprint depending on modality); provenance preservation; configurable export-time options. Customer: deepfake labelling at publication; editorial review for public-interest text.
scenarios.common_misclassification: Stripping upstream provenance metadata. Skipping marking for 'low-risk' modalities. Misusing the assistive-editing carve-out.
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: PrivateBox marks AI-generated outputs in a machine-readable manner, in line with Article 50(2) of the EU AI Act and the technical approach reflected in the European Commission's draft Code of Practice on marking and labelling of AI-generated content (currently voluntary).

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Art 50(2) provider duty for synthetic outputs.

scenarios.name: PrivateBox-hosted model packaging / update delivery
scenarios.description: PrivateBox ships periodic updates: model upgrades, fine-tunes, security patches, prompt-template changes. Updates are pushed (with customer consent) over a managed channel.
scenarios.legal_basis: Art 16 provider obligations; Art 18 record-keeping; Art 20 corrective actions; Art 72 post-market monitoring.
scenarios.on_prem_note: On-prem update channels often use signed packages and outbound-only fetch — *reduces* attack surface but creates evidence requirement: integrity, provenance, change-control traceability.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: change-control pipeline, signed releases, release notes describing AI-functional changes, regression tests, rollback. Customer: receive, test, deploy, log.
scenarios.common_misclassification: Silent 'improvement' pushes that change behaviour without notice. Treating prompt-template changes as not material.
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: PrivateBox uses a controlled, signed update channel. Each release includes versioned release notes that describe AI-functional changes, supporting deployer-side change-control and post-market obligations under Article 72 of the AI Act where applicable.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Update channel is part of post-market monitoring.

scenarios.name: Remote support / temporary access by PrivateBox personnel
scenarios.description: A PrivateBox engineer remotely accesses a customer's environment to diagnose. Access may include logs, prompts, outputs, system parameters.
scenarios.legal_basis: Art 4 (literacy for support staff); Art 20 (corrective actions); Art 21 (cooperation with authorities); Art 72 (post-market monitoring); GDPR Art 28.
scenarios.on_prem_note: Strongest selling point of on-prem is undermined if support access is broad and routine. Build narrow, audited, time-limited, customer-initiated access.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: support access is opt-in, just-in-time, logged, scoped, masked-by-default for prompts/outputs; AI literacy training for support staff; DPA with customer. Customer: approves each session; reviews logs.
scenarios.common_misclassification: 'Support staff are background contractors so AI literacy doesn't apply' — wrong; Art 4 covers persons dealing with operation/use on behalf. Allowing standing access.
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: Remote support access to a PrivateBox deployment is opt-in, time-bounded, customer-initiated, and audit-logged. PrivateBox personnel involved in supporting the system receive AI literacy training in line with Article 4.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Support staff in scope of literacy duty.
Support data feeds PMM; preserve evidence.

scenarios.name: Optional cloud telemetry / monitoring
scenarios.description: Customer enables anonymous telemetry / health monitoring that streams metadata to PrivateBox-hosted services.
scenarios.legal_basis: Art 72 post-market monitoring; Art 20 corrective-action duty (non-high-risk); GDPR.
scenarios.on_prem_note: Default-off telemetry preserves the on-prem promise.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: telemetry inventory, schema, retention, off-by-default switch. Customer: opt-in.
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: Telemetry is off by default. When enabled by the customer, telemetry is limited to system-health metadata; prompts and outputs are not transmitted.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Telemetry feeds PMM.

scenarios.name: Optional web search / external retrieval (RAG over the live web)
scenarios.description: Customer enables an optional connector that lets the assistant fetch live web content.
scenarios.legal_basis: Art 13 transparency (high-risk); Art 50 transparency duties; Art 53(1)(c) copyright (GPAI providers, not runtime web fetch — but customers will ask).
scenarios.on_prem_note: Web access is the **biggest single dilution** of the on-prem story. Treat as opt-in, with explicit purpose.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: domain allow-list, citation surfacing, robots.txt observance, no scraping bypass, audit logs. Customer: opt-in, lawful use.
scenarios.common_misclassification: Treating web RAG as harmless because outputs are 'summaries'. Stripping citations.
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: Web retrieval is an opt-in, explicitly enabled connector. When enabled, retrieved content is cited at the point of use to preserve provenance, and access is restricted by allow-list.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Citations surface output provenance.

scenarios.name: Cross-border support and EU market placement (PrivateBox vendor outside the EU)
scenarios.description: PrivateBox is a non-EU entity. EU customers buy and deploy. Support is delivered from outside the EU.
scenarios.legal_basis: Art 2(1)(a) provider placing on EU market; Art 2(1)(c) outputs used in EU; Art 22 AR for high-risk; Art 54 AR for GPAI.
scenarios.shared_responsibility: PrivateBox: appoint AR; preserve technical documentation; cooperate with market surveillance authorities. Customer: deployer obligations.
scenarios.common_misclassification: Believing EU rules don't apply because PB is HQ'd elsewhere.
scenarios.sales_safe_wording: PrivateBox treats EU customer deployments as in scope of the EU AI Act regardless of where PrivateBox itself is established. Where a high-risk variant is delivered, PrivateBox appoints an authorised representative under Article 22.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Extraterritorial reach explicit.
AR mandate required if any high-risk variant.
AR for GPAI provider role if triggered.

requirements.title: Scope
requirements.requirement: The Regulation applies to providers placing AI systems on the EU market or putting them into service in the EU; deployers established or located in the EU; providers and deployers in third countries where output is used in the EU; importers, distributors, product manufacturers under their name; authorised representatives of non-EU providers; affected persons in the EU.
requirements.pb_interpretation: EU geography is not what matters; EU presence of the system, the deployer, or the output is what matters. Being headquartered outside the EU is not a defence. PB almost certainly has EU customers, EU outputs, or both. Extraterritorial reach is presumed.
requirements.applies_rationale: Treats EU as in scope by default; verifies per customer.
requirements.operational_evidence: EU customer list maintained current; AR mandate text reachable in EU.
requirements.failure_modes: 'We're not EU; we're not in scope.' Excluding EU output cases.
requirements.pitfall: Treating non-EU HQ as exempting status.
requirements.remediation: Customer register exists, current, reviewed at every significant product change.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox treats EU deployments and EU-bound outputs as fully in scope of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689.

requirements.title: Definitions
requirements.requirement: Defined terms include: AI system (Art 3(1)) — software with autonomy and adaptiveness producing outputs from inputs; GPAI model (Art 3(63)) — model with significant generality, capable of competently performing a wide range of distinct tasks (Commission threshold ≥10²³ FLOP plus generative output for text/image/video); provider (Art 3(3)) — places on market under own name; deployer (Art 3(4)) — uses on its own responsibility; intended purpose (Art 3(12)); substantial modification (Art 3(23)).
requirements.pb_interpretation: Almost every other duty under the Act flips on these definitions. Maintain a Definition Register that maps every PB SKU and every customer scenario to the relevant defined terms. Each model packaged classified as GPAI / not-GPAI. Each scenario tagged with intended-purpose. Substantial-modification policy documents what counts as 'substantial'.
requirements.applies_rationale: Foundational classification work for all subsequent articles.
requirements.operational_evidence: Register reviewed quarterly; tied to release process.
requirements.pitfall: Loosely calling everything 'AI' without checking the definition; treating the GPAI threshold as a compliance threshold rather than a classification threshold.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox classifies its components as AI systems and (where applicable) general-purpose AI models in line with the definitions in Article 3 of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 and the European Commission's guidelines on the AI system definition (February 2025) and on GPAI scope (July 2025).
source_refs: Commission AI System Definition Guidelines Feb 2025 [G]

requirements.title: AI Literacy
requirements.requirement: Providers and deployers shall take measures to ensure, to their best extent, a sufficient level of AI literacy of their staff and other persons dealing with the operation and use of AI systems on their behalf, taking into account technical knowledge, experience, education, training, the context of use, and the persons or groups on whom the systems are used.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Already binding since 2 February 2025. People using or running AI on PB's behalf must be educated about it. 'On behalf' includes contractors, support staff, integration partners. Tier the training by role. Easy to fail because it requires PB's own programme, not just customer-facing materials.
requirements.applies_rationale: Provider duty (PB staff) AND deployer duty (customer staff). Each side owns its own.

role_applicability.deployer_note: Customer's literacy programme; PB provides supporting materials.

requirements.operational_evidence: Pick a random PB employee in scope; verify they completed the appropriate tier within 12 months.
requirements.pitfall: Treating Art 4 as customer duty only; one-off training without refresh; ignoring contractors and support engineers.
requirements.remediation: Curriculum integrated into onboarding; refresh tracked; deployer-facing version maintained.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox operates an AI literacy programme for its own personnel and provides training resources to customers, in line with Article 4 of the EU AI Act, applicable since 2 February 2025.

crosswalk.note: Competence — AI literacy is the AI-specific instance of competence requirement
Awareness — AI literacy programme is the AI awareness instance
Competence — AI literacy programme parallels privacy competence
Awareness — AI literacy and privacy awareness share form

source_refs: AI Office Q&A on AI Literacy [G]
Commission Living Repository of AI Literacy Practices [G]

requirements.title: Prohibited AI Practices
requirements.requirement: Article 5 prohibits, inter alia: (a) subliminal/manipulative/deceptive techniques materially distorting behaviour and causing significant harm; (b) exploitation of vulnerabilities; (c) social-scoring by public authorities; (d) predictive policing based solely on profiling; (e) untargeted scraping of facial images for facial-recognition databases; (f) emotion- recognition in workplace and education contexts (with narrow safety/medical exceptions); (g) biometric categorisation inferring sensitive attributes; (h) real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces for law enforcement (with strict carve-outs).
requirements.pb_interpretation: Already binding since 2 February 2025. Highest penalty tier (€35M / 7%). Even an apparently benign assistant can be configured by a customer to support a prohibited practice (e.g., emotion-recognition in workplace setting through 'sentiment analysis' of e-mails). Must be screened in design AND in deployer guidance.
requirements.applies_rationale: Provider duty (do not place such a system on market) and deployer duty (do not use).
requirements.operational_evidence: Pick a recent feature release and trace the Article 5 review. Pick a customer and check the onboarding screening.
requirements.pitfall: Treating Art 5 as solely a 'biometrics' issue. Failing to flag features that *enable* a prohibited use even if PB doesn't itself perform it. Missing the workplace + education emotion-recognition prohibition.
requirements.remediation: Article 5 review is a stage gate, not a checkbox.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox's Acceptable Use Policy and onboarding screening expressly exclude the prohibited AI practices defined in Article 5 of the EU AI Act, applicable since 2 February 2025.
source_refs: Commission Guidelines on Prohibited AI Practices Feb 2025 [G]

requirements.title: Classification of high-risk AI systems
requirements.requirement: A system is high-risk if (1) it is a safety component of a product covered by Annex I Union harmonisation legislation that is required to undergo third-party conformity assessment; or (2) it falls within Annex III, unless the Article 6(3) carve-out applies (narrow procedural task; improves prior human activity; detects patterns/deviations not meant to replace human assessment; preparatory task) — and the system does not perform profiling, in which case it is always high-risk.
requirements.pb_interpretation: High-risk = product-safety route (Annex I) OR listed use-case (Annex III). The narrow Article 6(3) exit door exists, but profiling kills it. PB does not naturally land in Annex I. Annex III risk is driven by the customer's intended purpose, not PB's design. The role-flip mechanism in Article 25(1)(c) is the dominant scenario.
requirements.applies_rationale: Conditional — high-risk classification typically attaches to customer-driven use, with role-flip consequences.
requirements.na_justification_template: N/A only where intended purpose is provably non-Annex-III and AUP enforces this contractually.
requirements.operational_evidence: Audit a sample of customer deployments. Are any inadvertently in Annex III categories without contractual acknowledgement?
requirements.pitfall: 'Article 6(3) means we're never high-risk.' Ignoring profiling. Letting customers assume Annex III use without flipping role.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox is not intended for use as a high-risk AI system under Annex III of the EU AI Act. Customers proposing such use will be routed through a separate contract and configuration; otherwise such use is contractually out of scope and subject to the role-flip rules in Art 25(1)(c).

requirements.title: Authorised Representatives of Providers (high-risk)
requirements.requirement: Non-EU providers of high-risk systems must, prior to placing on the market, appoint an authorised representative in the EU by written mandate. AR keeps documentation, declaration of conformity, conformity-assessment certificate; cooperates with authorities; can terminate if provider non-compliant.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Required only if/when PB provides a high-risk system from outside the EU. Note: GPAI providers also need an AR under Article 54.
requirements.applies_rationale: Triggers if (and only if) PB ships a high-risk variant from outside the EU.
requirements.pitfall: Forgetting the GPAI-specific AR under Art 54 if PB triggers GPAI provider status.

crosswalk.note: Contracts with processors — AR mandate is contractual instrument

business_models.note: AI Act Art. 22 automated processing aligns directly with POPIA s 71 automated decision-making.

requirements.title: Importers and Distributors
requirements.requirement: Importers verify CE marking, conformity declaration, technical documentation, AR appointment, instructions for use, before placing on EU market. Distributors verify CE marking and that provider/importer have done their job. Both must keep records and act on non-conformities.
requirements.pb_interpretation: PB can become an 'importer' if it imports an upstream third-party model packaged as a separate AI product. Or PB's resellers/MSPs become distributors of PB.
documented_evidence: Reseller agreements with distributor verification clauses

Addressing security in supplier agreements
Supplier / cloud security — channel partners as suppliers

requirements.title: Responsibilities along the AI value chain (role-flips)
requirements.requirement: A distributor, importer, deployer, or other third party becomes a provider of a high-risk system if it (a) puts its name/trademark on it, (b) makes a substantial modification, or (c) modifies the intended purpose of an AI system (including a GPAI system) into a high-risk Annex III use. The original provider is then relieved of obligations for that new system but must cooperate. For Annex I safety-component cases, the product manufacturer is the provider. Providers and third parties must agree in writing on the information and technical access necessary for compliance.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Single most important article for PB's commercial posture. Whenever a customer turns PB into a high-risk Annex III tool, Art 25(1)(c) flips the customer to provider — but only IF PB has been clear in instructions/contract that its system is not for that use. If PB is loose, both PB and customer can wind up co-liable. One sentence from a salesperson can flip the analysis.
requirements.applies_rationale: Rule applies to PB's contractual posture even when no high-risk use is happening.
requirements.operational_evidence: Contract review; legal sign-off; sample customer file.
requirements.pitfall: Sales saying 'yes, you can use this for HR ranking' — that single sentence can flip role allocation.
requirements.remediation: Standard contract language; sales staff trained on Article 25.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox is not placed on the market as a high-risk AI system. Customers who change the intended purpose of the system to a high-risk use case as defined in Annex III assume the provider obligations under Article 25(1)(c) of the EU AI Act, and PrivateBox provides reasonable cooperation in line with Article 25(2).

requirements.title: Risk Management System (high-risk)
requirements.requirement: Establish, implement, document, and maintain a risk-management system as a continuous, iterative process across the lifecycle: identify and analyse known and reasonably foreseeable risks; estimate and evaluate risks emerging from intended use and reasonably foreseeable misuse; evaluate other potentially arising risks based on post-market monitoring data; adopt risk-management measures; eliminate/reduce risks where possible; give clear information to the deployer.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Required only when high-risk; strongly recommended as scaffolding regardless. LLM-based products face known risk classes (prompt injection, hallucination, data leakage, model drift) that customers will ask about. PB maintains a living risk register tied to the release cycle, even outside high-risk scope.
requirements.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk variants; good practice for all SKUs.
requirements.operational_evidence: Pick a recent release; verify risk-review record; trace one risk from identification to mitigation to deployer disclosure.
requirements.pitfall: RMS that is a one-off; missing foreseeable-misuse coverage; no link to post-market monitoring.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox maintains a documented risk-management process across the AI lifecycle, covering identified risks, foreseeable misuse, mitigation measures, and deployer-facing residual-risk information, in alignment with Article 9 of the EU AI Act for any high-risk variant.

crosswalk.note: ISMS risk planning — Art 9 RMS is the AI-specific risk discipline

business_models.note: EU AI Act risk-management for high-risk AI overlaps with POPIA s 19 security safeguards when personal information is processed.

requirements.title: Data and Data Governance (high-risk)
requirements.requirement: Training, validation, and testing data sets shall be subject to data-governance and management practices: design choices; data-collection processes and data origin; relevant data-preparation (annotation, labelling, cleaning, updating, enrichment, aggregation); assumptions; assessment of availability, quantity, suitability; bias examination; mitigation; gap analysis. Datasets must be relevant, sufficiently representative, free of errors, complete, and have appropriate statistical properties.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Article 10 is high-risk-only on its face, but PB's posture toward fine-tuning data and customer RAG corpora needs to be defensible. If PB does not train on customer data, that is a powerful, evidenceable claim — subject to highest penalty tier (€35M / 7%) for breaches.
requirements.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk; defensible posture for all SKUs.
documented_evidence: 'No training on customer data' technical/contractual assurance
requirements.operational_evidence: Walk through dataset-card index; trace bias-examination methodology; check controls preventing customer-data leakage into training.
requirements.pitfall: Inheriting a third-party model whose training data is opaque without flagging the dependency. Vague 'no training' claims without contractual or technical anchors.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox documents the provenance, processing, and bias examination of any training or fine-tuning data it controls, and provides technical and contractual assurances that customer data is not used for model training, in alignment with Article 10 of the EU AI Act.

Data masking — supports data minimisation
Data leakage prevention — protects training data integrity
Identify and document purpose — training data must have documented purpose
Identify lawful basis — applies to training data collection

requirements.title: Technical Documentation (high-risk)
requirements.requirement: Technical documentation shall be drawn up before placing on the market and kept up to date; it shall demonstrate compliance with Articles 9-15. Annex IV specifies contents: general description, detailed description (development methods, design choices, system architecture, computational resources), description of monitoring/control/oversight, validation and testing procedures, risk-management documentation, list of harmonised standards/common specifications applied, EU declaration of conformity, post-market monitoring plan.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Required for high-risk; an Annex IV-aligned skeleton is best practice for non-high-risk too — speeds customer due-diligence and any future high-risk SKU.
requirements.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk; recommended for all SKUs.
requirements.operational_evidence: Annex IV checklist walk-through with assessor; show a sample technical-documentation file.
requirements.pitfall: 'It's all in the wiki' — unstructured, inconsistent, not version-controlled.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox maintains technical documentation aligned with Annex IV of the EU AI Act for any high-risk variant, and an Annex-IV-aligned skeleton for the standard product to support customer due diligence.

crosswalk.note: Documented information — Annex IV file is structured technical documentation
Documented information — Annex IV file structure

requirements.title: Record-Keeping / Logs (high-risk)
requirements.requirement: High-risk systems shall technically allow for the automatic recording of events ('logs') over their lifetime. Logging capabilities shall enable identification of situations that may result in the system presenting a risk or causing a substantial modification, facilitate post-market monitoring, and (for biometric ID) record specified parameters. Providers keep logs under their control. Deployers keep logs at least 6 months (Art 26(6)).
requirements.pb_interpretation: PB ships with comprehensive, configurable logging by default — even outside high-risk: prompt timestamps, model identifiers, response identifiers, session metadata, retrieval citations. On-prem means logs sit with deployer, which is privacy-positive AND satisfies Art 26(6) deployer duty.
requirements.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk; default-on for all SKUs.
requirements.operational_evidence: Inspect a deployment's log schema; verify ≥6-month retention configurable; review log-export interface.
requirements.pitfall: Logs that auto-rotate at 30 days. Log volume so high that retention becomes infeasible.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox provides default-on, structured logging with configurable retention of at least six months, and a documented log schema, in alignment with Articles 12 and 26(6) of the EU AI Act.

crosswalk.note: Logging — Art 12 logs build on the security logging baseline
Monitoring activities — runtime monitoring overlap

Logging & monitoring — Art 12 builds on privacy-aware logging baseline

business_models.note: AI Act record-keeping aligns with POPIA s 14 retention rules.

requirements.title: Transparency and Provision of Information to Deployers (high-risk)
requirements.requirement: High-risk systems shall be designed and developed in such a way as to ensure their operation is sufficiently transparent to enable deployers to interpret the system's output and use it appropriately. Instructions for use must accompany the system, providing concise, complete, correct, clear information including: provider identity; characteristics, capabilities, and limitations; foreseeable misuse; technical capabilities; specifications of input data; performance metrics; risks; circumstances that may affect output reliability; human oversight measures; computational and hardware resources; expected lifetime; maintenance and care; logs.
requirements.pb_interpretation: 'Instructions for use' is the central deployer-facing artifact. PB ships an Instructions for Use pack covering Art 13(3) topics. Even non-high-risk deployments benefit from this shape — it doubles as customer onboarding material.
requirements.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk; recommended structure for all SKUs.
requirements.operational_evidence: Pick a recent release; verify Instructions for Use cover all Art 13(3) topics; trace deployer-facing changes since prior release.
requirements.pitfall: Instructions written for compliance, not for the deployer's actual operational reality.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox ships with Instructions for Use that cover the topics required under Article 13(3) of the EU AI Act, and that enable deployers to interpret the system's output and use it appropriately.

business_models.note: AI Act transparency obligations align with POPIA s 17-18 openness.

requirements.title: Human Oversight (high-risk)
requirements.requirement: High-risk systems shall be designed and developed in such a way that they can be effectively overseen by natural persons during the period in which they are in use. Human oversight measures shall enable understanding of the system's capacities and limitations; awareness of automation bias; correct interpretation of output; ability to decide not to use the system or to override output; ability to intervene or interrupt operation. For remote biometric ID systems, two-person verification.
requirements.pb_interpretation: PB designs oversight controls (sampling, override, escalation, refusal) into the product regardless of risk class. For high-risk variants, these are formalised; for standard SKUs, they support good operational hygiene and give deployers somewhere to plug in.
requirements.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk; built-in for all SKUs.
requirements.operational_evidence: Walk through a deployment's oversight UI; verify override paths function; review escalation playbook.
requirements.pitfall: Treating oversight as a UX afterthought rather than a core design requirement. Oversight that depends on monitoring volumes a human cannot realistically sustain.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox provides human-oversight controls — including override paths, intervention, and interruption — designed into the product to support Article 14 of the EU AI Act.

business_models.note: AI Act human-oversight obligation supports POPIA s 71 substantial-effect safeguards.

requirements.title: Accuracy, Robustness, and Cybersecurity (high-risk)
requirements.requirement: High-risk systems shall be designed and developed to achieve appropriate level of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity, and to perform consistently throughout their lifecycle. Accuracy metrics declared in Instructions for Use. Resilience to errors, faults, inconsistencies. Resilience against unauthorised attempts to alter use, output, or performance, exploiting system vulnerabilities (data poisoning, model poisoning, adversarial examples, model evasion, confidentiality attacks).
requirements.pb_interpretation: For high-risk variants, accuracy/robustness/cyber are formal commitments declared in Instructions for Use. For all SKUs, PB maintains evaluation harness covering hallucination, prompt-injection resilience, jailbreak resistance, and data-leakage probes.
requirements.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk; engineered baseline for all SKUs.
documented_evidence: Cyber posture (security controls inventory crosswalked to ISO 27001)
requirements.operational_evidence: Inspect evaluation harness; verify recent adversarial test results; cross-reference cyber controls to ISO 27001.
requirements.cadence: Per release / per significant model change
requirements.pitfall: Declaring accuracy metrics that the system does not actually meet under real deployment conditions. Confusing 'we use TLS' with cybersecurity for an AI system.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox declares accuracy and robustness metrics for any high-risk variant in its Instructions for Use, and maintains an adversarial-testing programme covering prompt injection, jailbreak, and data-leakage resistance, in alignment with Article 15 of the EU AI Act.

requirements.title: Obligations of Providers of high-risk AI systems
requirements.requirement: A high-risk provider must: ensure compliance with Articles 9-15; have a quality-management system (Art 17); keep documentation (Art 18); keep automatic logs (Art 19); take corrective actions (Art 20); cooperate with authorities (Art 21); appoint AR if non-EU (Art 22); affix CE marking and draw up EU declaration of conformity (Art 47, 48); register in EU database (Art 49).
requirements.pb_interpretation: Triggers only if PB knowingly provides a high-risk system. Right now: not the default product. But the Article 16 regime is the destination for any future PB-Annex-III SKU. Design QMS scaffolding now to ease the transition.
requirements.applies_rationale: Conditional — full applicability only on high-risk variant launch.
requirements.operational_evidence: Walk an external assessor through the QMS; show sample technical documentation; show DoC template.
requirements.pitfall: Believing CE marking is always self-declared — true for Annex III by default (internal control under Annex VI), but biometrics require notified body under Annex VII.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox maintains internal documentation and quality-management practices that support Article 16 obligations should a high-risk variant be required for a specific deployment.

requirements.title: Quality Management System
requirements.requirement: Providers of high-risk systems must operate a documented QMS covering at least: regulatory strategy; design and development controls; quality control; testing and validation; technical documentation; data governance; record-keeping; risk management; post-market monitoring; serious-incident reporting; communication with authorities; human resources / accountability; resource management.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Even non-high-risk operation benefits from a QMS that mirrors Article 17. ISO/IEC 42001 is strong scaffolding. PB targets ISO 42001 alignment as the QMS reference architecture, which gives Article 17 coverage by construction.
requirements.operational_evidence: ISO 42001 internal audit covers the Article 17 areas; management-review records exist.
requirements.pitfall: Mistaking ISO 27001 alone for a sufficient QMS — the AI lifecycle pieces are missing.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox operates a quality-management system aligned with Article 17 of the EU AI Act and structured around ISO/IEC 42001 principles.
