requirements.title: Deployer Obligations (high-risk)
requirements.requirement: Deployers must: (1) use the system per instructions; (2) assign human oversight to competent natural persons; (3) ensure relevance/appropriateness of input data they control; (4) monitor operation and notify provider/authorities of risks or serious incidents; (5) keep automatically generated logs at least 6 months; (6) inform workers' representatives and affected workers before workplace use; (7) public authorities register in EU database; (8) use Art 13 information for DPIAs; (9) inform affected natural persons subject to a high-risk AI system; (10) cooperate with authorities. Article 27 layers a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment for specific deployer categories.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Customer obligation, but PB must enable it — Instructions for Use, oversight tooling, log retention, worker-information templates. PB ships a Deployer Pack that gives the customer everything they need to discharge Art 26 with documented support.
requirements.applies_rationale: Customer is the addressee; PB enables.

role_applicability.provider_note: PB enables but is not the addressee.
role_applicability.deployer_note: Customer is the legal addressee for high-risk variants.

requirements.operational_evidence: Sample customer can produce all artifacts on request; deployer pack version-controlled and updated per release.
requirements.pitfall: Logs that auto-rotate at 30 days. Forgetting workers'-information duty (Art 26(7)).
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox supplies an Instructions for Use pack and configurable logging, oversight, and notification controls that enable deployers of high-risk variants to meet their obligations under Article 26 of the EU AI Act.

crosswalk.note: Customer agreement / instructions — deployer-as-controller direction-giving

business_models.note: AI Act deployer obligations require POPIA-style accountability where personal information is processed.
Designated humans (Art. 26.2) overlap with POPIA Information Officer duties.

requirements.title: Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (deployer)
requirements.requirement: Before deploying certain high-risk systems, deployers that are bodies governed by public law, private operators providing public services, or operators of certain Annex III systems (incl. credit/insurance for natural persons) must perform a FRIA covering: deployer's processes, period and frequency of use, categories of natural persons affected, specific risks of harm, human oversight measures, internal governance, complaint procedures.
requirements.pb_interpretation: PB does not perform a FRIA (it is not the deployer). But PB provides a deployer-FRIA aid template pre-filled with PB's stated intended purpose, foreseeable risks, oversight controls, so customers can adapt rather than start from zero.

role_applicability.deployer_note: Customer carries out and signs FRIA.

documented_evidence: Example FRIA for a fictional deployer
PB-side risk and oversight description (input to FRIA)
requirements.pitfall: Providing only a generic template that ignores the AI-specific risks customers actually face.

crosswalk.note: PIA/DPIA — FRIA extends DPIA with fundamental-rights layer

business_models.note: FRIA covers fundamental-rights impacts of automated decisions; POPIA s 71 analysis integrates.

requirements.title: Registration in the EU database
requirements.requirement: Providers (and certain public-authority deployers) of Annex III high-risk systems must register the system in the EU database before placing on the market or putting into service. Information per Annex VIII.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Triggers only if a high-risk variant is shipped. Database registration is a public-facing artifact that must be kept current — non-trivial post-launch obligation.
documented_evidence: Annex VIII content per registered system
requirements.pitfall: Registering once and not maintaining the entry as the system evolves.

requirements.title: Transparency Obligations (chat, synthetic content, deepfakes, public-interest text)
requirements.requirement: (1) Providers must inform persons interacting with the AI system that they are interacting with AI, unless this is obvious or use is for permitted criminal-law purposes. (2) Providers of generative AI systems must mark synthetic outputs in a machine-readable format and detectable as artificially generated, with technical solutions effective, interoperable, robust, reliable as far as technically feasible — except where the system performs only assistive editing or does not substantially alter input. (3) Deployers of emotion-recognition or biometric-categorisation systems must inform exposed natural persons. (4) Deployers of deepfake-generating systems must disclose; deployers of AI-generated text published with the purpose of informing the public on matters of public interest must disclose, with editorial-review carve-out. (5) Information must be provided in a clear and distinguishable manner at the latest at first interaction.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Article 50 is the obligation that DOES apply broadly — including to PB's typical office assistant. Persistent in-product disclosure, machine-readable marking of generated content, and deployer guidance for deepfake and public-interest text are all PB-side workstreams. Binding from 2 August 2026.
requirements.applies_rationale: Provider duties under 50(1) and 50(2); customer-side under 50(3)/(4)/(5).

role_applicability.provider_note: 50(1) AI disclosure + 50(2) machine-readable marking are provider duties.
role_applicability.deployer_note: 50(3) emotion/biometric disclosure + 50(4) deepfake/public-interest text labelling are deployer duties.

requirements.operational_evidence: Live demonstration of marking and verification; UX review against accessibility standards.
requirements.pitfall: Treating disclosure as a one-time pop-up. Stripping upstream provenance metadata in pipelines. Skipping marking for 'assistive' features that actually do generate substantial content. Confusing the provider duty (50(2)) with the deployer duty (50(4)).
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox surfaces a persistent indication that users are interacting with AI and marks generative outputs in a machine-readable format, in alignment with Article 50(1)-(2) of the EU AI Act, applicable from 2 August 2026, and the technical approaches reflected in the European Commission's voluntary draft Code of Practice on marking and labelling of AI-generated content.

crosswalk.note: Privacy notice — Art 50 disclosure parallels privacy transparency principle

source_refs: Code of Practice on Marking and Labelling of AI-generated Content (1st draft Dec 2025; 2nd draft Mar 2026; final June 2026) [C]

business_models.note: AI Act Art. 50 disclosure aligns with POPIA s 18 collection-notice obligations.

requirements.title: GPAI models with systemic risk — classification, notification, additional duties
requirements.requirement: Article 51 classifies GPAI models with 'systemic risk' (presumption when training compute exceeds 10²⁵ FLOP or by Commission designation under Annex XIII). Article 52: notification procedure; provider can rebut presumption. Article 55: GPAI-with-systemic-risk additional duties — model evaluations including adversarial testing, mitigation of systemic risks, serious-incident reporting to AI Office, cybersecurity protection.
requirements.pb_interpretation: PB extremely unlikely to train a model crossing 10²⁵ FLOP. Systemic-risk regime applies to frontier-lab providers. Relevant to PB only as upstream-supplier diligence — verify whether any GPAI model PB integrates is itself a systemic-risk model, since systemic-risk models carry their own transparency and reporting cascade downstream.
requirements.applies_rationale: PB is downstream integrator; systemic-risk obligations attach to upstream providers. Upstream-provider diligence remains.
documented_evidence: Per-model upstream supplier file flagging systemic-risk status
requirements.operational_evidence: For each integrated GPAI model: capture systemic-risk flag, link to upstream provider's compliance posture.
requirements.pitfall: Misreading the threshold as compliance-obligating rather than classification-triggering.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox tracks whether the third-party general-purpose AI models it integrates fall under the systemic-risk regime in Articles 51-52 and 55 of the EU AI Act and preserves the upstream provider's compliance documentation accordingly.

requirements.title: GPAI Provider Obligations — documentation, copyright, training summary
requirements.requirement: Every GPAI provider must keep technical documentation (Annex XI), provide downstream-provider documentation (Annex XII), have a copyright-compliance policy under EU copyright law, and publish a sufficiently detailed summary of training content using the AI Office's published template. Free-and-open-licence models are partially exempt (only copyright + summary), unless they are systemic-risk.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Two sub-cases for PB: (i) downstream integrator of unmodified third-party GPAI (Scenarios A, B, C) — pass-through duty plus diligence on upstream provider; (ii) PB crosses the modification threshold (Scenario D) and becomes GPAI provider in respect of modifications — limited but real duties (Annex XI internal docs + Annex XII downstream pack + copyright policy + training-data summary, all specific to the fine-tune). Binding since 2 August 2025 for new GPAI models.
requirements.applies_rationale: Pass-through duty in all GPAI-touching scenarios; full GPAI-provider duty only in Scenario D.
documented_evidence: Per-model upstream supplier file (Annex XII pass-through)
Training-data summary (where PB triggers GPAI provider role)
Annex XI internal documentation (where PB triggers GPAI provider role)
requirements.operational_evidence: Pick a model; trace the chain: upstream provider → PB pass-through → customer deployer pack.
requirements.pitfall: Confusing GPAI **model** obligations (Articles 51-55) with AI **system** obligations. Over-claiming open-source exemption (does not apply if systemic-risk).
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox documents the third-party GPAI models it integrates, preserves the downstream documentation those providers supply under Article 53 of the EU AI Act, and where it materially modifies a model, prepares its own GPAI documentation in respect of the modifications.

crosswalk.note: Transfer basis — Annex XII downstream documentation

requirements.title: Authorised Representatives for non-EU GPAI Providers
requirements.requirement: Non-EU GPAI providers must, prior to placing a GPAI model on the EU market, appoint an authorised representative in the EU by written mandate. AR keeps the technical documentation, cooperates with the AI Office and competent authorities, can terminate if provider non-compliant.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Triggers only if PB itself becomes a GPAI provider (Scenario D — fine-tunes above threshold) AND PB is non-EU. Distinct from Article 22 (which is for high-risk-system AR). Both ARs needed if both roles trigger.
requirements.applies_rationale: Conditional on Scenario D + non-EU PB establishment.
requirements.pitfall: Forgetting that high-risk AR (Art 22) and GPAI AR (Art 54) are separate; one AR may satisfy both, by design.

requirements.title: Codes of Practice (legal status)
requirements.requirement: The AI Office facilitates codes of practice. The Commission may approve a code by implementing act. Adherence is voluntary. Non-signatories must demonstrate compliance by other adequate means.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Codes are a route to compliance, not the compliance itself. PB can choose to align with codes (GPAI CoP if Scenario D triggers; Marking and Labelling CoP for Article 50(2)/(4)). Not legally required. Trade-off: signing creates presumption of compliance but binds PB to the code's terms; non-signing keeps flexibility but puts evidence burden on 'demonstrate by other means'.
requirements.applies_rationale: Governance article; signing decisions are PB-strategic.

requirements.title: Post-Market Monitoring (high-risk)
requirements.requirement: Providers of high-risk systems must operate a post-market monitoring system, with a documented plan, that actively and systematically collects, documents, and analyses data on system performance throughout its lifetime. Evidence used to update the risk-management system, the system itself, and to enable corrective actions.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Conditional on high-risk status. Even non-high-risk products need a de-facto incident response process covering AI-specific failure modes (model degradation, prompt injection, hallucinated outputs leading to harm, data leakage). PB designs PMM as default operational discipline.
requirements.applies_rationale: Binding for high-risk; recommended as default operational discipline.
documented_evidence: PMM plan template (per Art 72(3); Commission template forthcoming under Art 96/98)
Telemetry and customer-feedback channels feeding PMM
requirements.operational_evidence: Inspect PMM plan; verify telemetry feeds reach risk register; review last-quarter PMM cycle.
requirements.pitfall: Treating PMM as a 'v1.1 feature' rather than a day-1 process.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox operates a post-market monitoring process with documented telemetry and feedback channels feeding a living risk register, in alignment with Article 72 of the EU AI Act for any high-risk variant.

requirements.title: Serious-Incident Reporting (high-risk)
requirements.requirement: Providers of high-risk systems must report serious incidents to market surveillance authorities of the Member State where the incident occurred. Timing: immediately and at most 15 days after the provider becomes aware of the serious incident or of the causal link; 2 days for widespread infringement or serious malfunction of critical infrastructure; 10 days for incidents resulting in death.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Conditional on high-risk status. Decision tree with the four timing buckets is a small but critical artifact; failure of timing is visible non-compliance. PB drills the decision tree annually even outside high-risk scope.
requirements.applies_rationale: Binding for high-risk; deployer also reports under Art 26(5).
documented_evidence: Liaison list per Member State market surveillance authority
requirements.operational_evidence: Run a tabletop exercise; verify timing decisions match the four buckets (2/10/15 days, plus 'immediately').
requirements.pitfall: Confusing the timing buckets. Treating 'serious malfunction' too narrowly.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox maintains a serious-incident decision tree and reporting playbook aligned with the timing requirements of Article 73 of the EU AI Act for any high-risk variant.

crosswalk.note: Incident management planning — Art 73 has stricter timing
Assessment and decision on information security events
Response to information security incidents
Learning from incidents
Privacy incident management — Art 73 has stricter timing for high-risk AI

business_models.note: AI Act Art. 73 serious-incident reporting aligns with POPIA s 22 breach-notification timing.

eu-ai-act-art-2.title: Scope
eu-ai-act-art-2.requirement: The Regulation applies to providers placing AI systems on the EU market or putting them into service in the EU; deployers established or located in the EU; providers and deployers in third countries where output is used in the EU; importers, distributors, product manufacturers under their name; authorised representatives of non-EU providers; affected persons in the EU.
eu-ai-act-art-2.pb_interpretation: EU geography is not what matters; EU presence of the system, the deployer, or the output is what matters. Being headquartered outside the EU is not a defence. PB almost certainly has EU customers, EU outputs, or both. Extraterritorial reach is presumed.
eu-ai-act-art-2.applies_rationale: Treats EU as in scope by default; verifies per customer.
eu-ai-act-art-2.operational_evidence: EU customer list maintained current; AR mandate text reachable in EU.
eu-ai-act-art-2.failure_modes: 'We're not EU; we're not in scope.' Excluding EU output cases.
eu-ai-act-art-2.pitfall: Treating non-EU HQ as exempting status.
eu-ai-act-art-2.remediation: Customer register exists, current, reviewed at every significant product change.
eu-ai-act-art-2.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox treats EU deployments and EU-bound outputs as fully in scope of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689.

eu-ai-act-art-3.title: Definitions
eu-ai-act-art-3.requirement: Defined terms include: AI system (Art 3(1)) — software with autonomy and adaptiveness producing outputs from inputs; GPAI model (Art 3(63)) — model with significant generality, capable of competently performing a wide range of distinct tasks (Commission threshold ≥10²³ FLOP plus generative output for text/image/video); provider (Art 3(3)) — places on market under own name; deployer (Art 3(4)) — uses on its own responsibility; intended purpose (Art 3(12)); substantial modification (Art 3(23)).
eu-ai-act-art-3.pb_interpretation: Almost every other duty under the Act flips on these definitions. Maintain a Definition Register that maps every PB SKU and every customer scenario to the relevant defined terms. Each model packaged classified as GPAI / not-GPAI. Each scenario tagged with intended-purpose. Substantial-modification policy documents what counts as 'substantial'.
eu-ai-act-art-3.applies_rationale: Foundational classification work for all subsequent articles.
eu-ai-act-art-3.operational_evidence: Register reviewed quarterly; tied to release process.
eu-ai-act-art-3.pitfall: Loosely calling everything 'AI' without checking the definition; treating the GPAI threshold as a compliance threshold rather than a classification threshold.
eu-ai-act-art-3.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox classifies its components as AI systems and (where applicable) general-purpose AI models in line with the definitions in Article 3 of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 and the European Commission's guidelines on the AI system definition (February 2025) and on GPAI scope (July 2025).
source_refs: Commission AI System Definition Guidelines Feb 2025 [G]

eu-ai-act-art-4.title: AI Literacy
eu-ai-act-art-4.requirement: Providers and deployers shall take measures to ensure, to their best extent, a sufficient level of AI literacy of their staff and other persons dealing with the operation and use of AI systems on their behalf, taking into account technical knowledge, experience, education, training, the context of use, and the persons or groups on whom the systems are used.
eu-ai-act-art-4.pb_interpretation: Already binding since 2 February 2025. People using or running AI on PB's behalf must be educated about it. 'On behalf' includes contractors, support staff, integration partners. Tier the training by role. Easy to fail because it requires PB's own programme, not just customer-facing materials.
eu-ai-act-art-4.applies_rationale: Provider duty (PB staff) AND deployer duty (customer staff). Each side owns its own.

role_applicability.deployer_note: Customer's literacy programme; PB provides supporting materials.

eu-ai-act-art-4.operational_evidence: Pick a random PB employee in scope; verify they completed the appropriate tier within 12 months.
eu-ai-act-art-4.pitfall: Treating Art 4 as customer duty only; one-off training without refresh; ignoring contractors and support engineers.
eu-ai-act-art-4.remediation: Curriculum integrated into onboarding; refresh tracked; deployer-facing version maintained.
eu-ai-act-art-4.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox operates an AI literacy programme for its own personnel and provides training resources to customers, in line with Article 4 of the EU AI Act, applicable since 2 February 2025.

crosswalk.note: Competence — AI literacy is the AI-specific instance of competence requirement
Awareness — AI literacy programme is the AI awareness instance
Competence — AI literacy programme parallels privacy competence
Awareness — AI literacy and privacy awareness share form

source_refs: AI Office Q&A on AI Literacy [G]
Commission Living Repository of AI Literacy Practices [G]

eu-ai-act-art-5.title: Prohibited AI Practices
eu-ai-act-art-5.requirement: Article 5 prohibits, inter alia: (a) subliminal/manipulative/deceptive techniques materially distorting behaviour and causing significant harm; (b) exploitation of vulnerabilities; (c) social-scoring by public authorities; (d) predictive policing based solely on profiling; (e) untargeted scraping of facial images for facial-recognition databases; (f) emotion- recognition in workplace and education contexts (with narrow safety/medical exceptions); (g) biometric categorisation inferring sensitive attributes; (h) real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces for law enforcement (with strict carve-outs).
eu-ai-act-art-5.pb_interpretation: Already binding since 2 February 2025. Highest penalty tier (€35M / 7%). Even an apparently benign assistant can be configured by a customer to support a prohibited practice (e.g., emotion-recognition in workplace setting through 'sentiment analysis' of e-mails). Must be screened in design AND in deployer guidance.
eu-ai-act-art-5.applies_rationale: Provider duty (do not place such a system on market) and deployer duty (do not use).
eu-ai-act-art-5.operational_evidence: Pick a recent feature release and trace the Article 5 review. Pick a customer and check the onboarding screening.
eu-ai-act-art-5.pitfall: Treating Art 5 as solely a 'biometrics' issue. Failing to flag features that *enable* a prohibited use even if PB doesn't itself perform it. Missing the workplace + education emotion-recognition prohibition.
eu-ai-act-art-5.remediation: Article 5 review is a stage gate, not a checkbox.
eu-ai-act-art-5.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox's Acceptable Use Policy and onboarding screening expressly exclude the prohibited AI practices defined in Article 5 of the EU AI Act, applicable since 2 February 2025.
source_refs: Commission Guidelines on Prohibited AI Practices Feb 2025 [G]

eu-ai-act-art-6.title: Classification of high-risk AI systems
eu-ai-act-art-6.requirement: A system is high-risk if (1) it is a safety component of a product covered by Annex I Union harmonisation legislation that is required to undergo third-party conformity assessment; or (2) it falls within Annex III, unless the Article 6(3) carve-out applies (narrow procedural task; improves prior human activity; detects patterns/deviations not meant to replace human assessment; preparatory task) — and the system does not perform profiling, in which case it is always high-risk.
eu-ai-act-art-6.pb_interpretation: High-risk = product-safety route (Annex I) OR listed use-case (Annex III). The narrow Article 6(3) exit door exists, but profiling kills it. PB does not naturally land in Annex I. Annex III risk is driven by the customer's intended purpose, not PB's design. The role-flip mechanism in Article 25(1)(c) is the dominant scenario.
eu-ai-act-art-6.applies_rationale: Conditional — high-risk classification typically attaches to customer-driven use, with role-flip consequences.
eu-ai-act-art-6.na_justification_template: N/A only where intended purpose is provably non-Annex-III and AUP enforces this contractually.
eu-ai-act-art-6.operational_evidence: Audit a sample of customer deployments. Are any inadvertently in Annex III categories without contractual acknowledgement?
eu-ai-act-art-6.pitfall: 'Article 6(3) means we're never high-risk.' Ignoring profiling. Letting customers assume Annex III use without flipping role.
eu-ai-act-art-6.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox is not intended for use as a high-risk AI system under Annex III of the EU AI Act. Customers proposing such use will be routed through a separate contract and configuration; otherwise such use is contractually out of scope and subject to the role-flip rules in Art 25(1)(c).

eu-ai-act-art-22.title: Authorised Representatives of Providers (high-risk)
eu-ai-act-art-22.requirement: Non-EU providers of high-risk systems must, prior to placing on the market, appoint an authorised representative in the EU by written mandate. AR keeps documentation, declaration of conformity, conformity-assessment certificate; cooperates with authorities; can terminate if provider non-compliant.
eu-ai-act-art-22.pb_interpretation: Required only if/when PB provides a high-risk system from outside the EU. Note: GPAI providers also need an AR under Article 54.
eu-ai-act-art-22.applies_rationale: Triggers if (and only if) PB ships a high-risk variant from outside the EU.
eu-ai-act-art-22.pitfall: Forgetting the GPAI-specific AR under Art 54 if PB triggers GPAI provider status.

crosswalk.note: Contracts with processors — AR mandate is contractual instrument

business_models.note: AI Act Art. 22 automated processing aligns directly with POPIA s 71 automated decision-making.

eu-ai-act-art-23-24.title: Importers and Distributors
eu-ai-act-art-23-24.requirement: Importers verify CE marking, conformity declaration, technical documentation, AR appointment, instructions for use, before placing on EU market. Distributors verify CE marking and that provider/importer have done their job. Both must keep records and act on non-conformities.
eu-ai-act-art-23-24.pb_interpretation: PB can become an 'importer' if it imports an upstream third-party model packaged as a separate AI product. Or PB's resellers/MSPs become distributors of PB.
documented_evidence: Reseller agreements with distributor verification clauses

crosswalk.note: Supplier relationships — channel partners are suppliers
Addressing security in supplier agreements
Supplier / cloud security — channel partners as suppliers

eu-ai-act-art-25.title: Responsibilities along the AI value chain (role-flips)
eu-ai-act-art-25.requirement: A distributor, importer, deployer, or other third party becomes a provider of a high-risk system if it (a) puts its name/trademark on it, (b) makes a substantial modification, or (c) modifies the intended purpose of an AI system (including a GPAI system) into a high-risk Annex III use. The original provider is then relieved of obligations for that new system but must cooperate. For Annex I safety-component cases, the product manufacturer is the provider. Providers and third parties must agree in writing on the information and technical access necessary for compliance.
eu-ai-act-art-25.pb_interpretation: Single most important article for PB's commercial posture. Whenever a customer turns PB into a high-risk Annex III tool, Art 25(1)(c) flips the customer to provider — but only IF PB has been clear in instructions/contract that its system is not for that use. If PB is loose, both PB and customer can wind up co-liable. One sentence from a salesperson can flip the analysis.
eu-ai-act-art-25.applies_rationale: Rule applies to PB's contractual posture even when no high-risk use is happening.
eu-ai-act-art-25.operational_evidence: Contract review; legal sign-off; sample customer file.
eu-ai-act-art-25.pitfall: Sales saying 'yes, you can use this for HR ranking' — that single sentence can flip role allocation.
eu-ai-act-art-25.remediation: Standard contract language; sales staff trained on Article 25.
eu-ai-act-art-25.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox is not placed on the market as a high-risk AI system. Customers who change the intended purpose of the system to a high-risk use case as defined in Annex III assume the provider obligations under Article 25(1)(c) of the EU AI Act, and PrivateBox provides reasonable cooperation in line with Article 25(2).

eu-ai-act-art-9.title: Risk Management System (high-risk)
eu-ai-act-art-9.requirement: Establish, implement, document, and maintain a risk-management system as a continuous, iterative process across the lifecycle: identify and analyse known and reasonably foreseeable risks; estimate and evaluate risks emerging from intended use and reasonably foreseeable misuse; evaluate other potentially arising risks based on post-market monitoring data; adopt risk-management measures; eliminate/reduce risks where possible; give clear information to the deployer.
eu-ai-act-art-9.pb_interpretation: Required only when high-risk; strongly recommended as scaffolding regardless. LLM-based products face known risk classes (prompt injection, hallucination, data leakage, model drift) that customers will ask about. PB maintains a living risk register tied to the release cycle, even outside high-risk scope.
eu-ai-act-art-9.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk variants; good practice for all SKUs.
eu-ai-act-art-9.operational_evidence: Pick a recent release; verify risk-review record; trace one risk from identification to mitigation to deployer disclosure.
eu-ai-act-art-9.pitfall: RMS that is a one-off; missing foreseeable-misuse coverage; no link to post-market monitoring.
eu-ai-act-art-9.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox maintains a documented risk-management process across the AI lifecycle, covering identified risks, foreseeable misuse, mitigation measures, and deployer-facing residual-risk information, in alignment with Article 9 of the EU AI Act for any high-risk variant.

crosswalk.note: ISMS risk planning — Art 9 RMS is the AI-specific risk discipline
Threat intelligence — feeds AI risk identification
Privacy + security risk methodology — Art 9 RMS aligns to integrated risk discipline
Operational privacy risk assessment

business_models.note: EU AI Act risk-management for high-risk AI overlaps with POPIA s 19 security safeguards when personal information is processed.

eu-ai-act-art-10.title: Data and Data Governance (high-risk)
eu-ai-act-art-10.requirement: Training, validation, and testing data sets shall be subject to data-governance and management practices: design choices; data-collection processes and data origin; relevant data-preparation (annotation, labelling, cleaning, updating, enrichment, aggregation); assumptions; assessment of availability, quantity, suitability; bias examination; mitigation; gap analysis. Datasets must be relevant, sufficiently representative, free of errors, complete, and have appropriate statistical properties.
eu-ai-act-art-10.pb_interpretation: Article 10 is high-risk-only on its face, but PB's posture toward fine-tuning data and customer RAG corpora needs to be defensible. If PB does not train on customer data, that is a powerful, evidenceable claim — subject to highest penalty tier (€35M / 7%) for breaches.
eu-ai-act-art-10.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk; defensible posture for all SKUs.
documented_evidence: 'No training on customer data' technical/contractual assurance
eu-ai-act-art-10.operational_evidence: Walk through dataset-card index; trace bias-examination methodology; check controls preventing customer-data leakage into training.
eu-ai-act-art-10.pitfall: Inheriting a third-party model whose training data is opaque without flagging the dependency. Vague 'no training' claims without contractual or technical anchors.
eu-ai-act-art-10.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox documents the provenance, processing, and bias examination of any training or fine-tuning data it controls, and provides technical and contractual assurances that customer data is not used for model training, in alignment with Article 10 of the EU AI Act.

crosswalk.note: Information deletion — supports data lifecycle obligations
Data masking — supports data minimisation
Data leakage prevention — protects training data integrity
Identify and document purpose — training data must have documented purpose
Identify lawful basis — applies to training data collection
Privacy by design — data governance is core PbD principle
No re-purposing — 'no training on customer data' is the operational form

eu-ai-act-art-11.title: Technical Documentation (high-risk)
eu-ai-act-art-11.requirement: Technical documentation shall be drawn up before placing on the market and kept up to date; it shall demonstrate compliance with Articles 9-15. Annex IV specifies contents: general description, detailed description (development methods, design choices, system architecture, computational resources), description of monitoring/control/oversight, validation and testing procedures, risk-management documentation, list of harmonised standards/common specifications applied, EU declaration of conformity, post-market monitoring plan.
eu-ai-act-art-11.pb_interpretation: Required for high-risk; an Annex IV-aligned skeleton is best practice for non-high-risk too — speeds customer due-diligence and any future high-risk SKU.
eu-ai-act-art-11.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk; recommended for all SKUs.
eu-ai-act-art-11.operational_evidence: Annex IV checklist walk-through with assessor; show a sample technical-documentation file.
eu-ai-act-art-11.pitfall: 'It's all in the wiki' — unstructured, inconsistent, not version-controlled.
eu-ai-act-art-11.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox maintains technical documentation aligned with Annex IV of the EU AI Act for any high-risk variant, and an Annex-IV-aligned skeleton for the standard product to support customer due diligence.

crosswalk.note: Documented information — Annex IV file is structured technical documentation
Documented information — Annex IV file structure

eu-ai-act-art-12.title: Record-Keeping / Logs (high-risk)
eu-ai-act-art-12.requirement: High-risk systems shall technically allow for the automatic recording of events ('logs') over their lifetime. Logging capabilities shall enable identification of situations that may result in the system presenting a risk or causing a substantial modification, facilitate post-market monitoring, and (for biometric ID) record specified parameters. Providers keep logs under their control. Deployers keep logs at least 6 months (Art 26(6)).
eu-ai-act-art-12.pb_interpretation: PB ships with comprehensive, configurable logging by default — even outside high-risk: prompt timestamps, model identifiers, response identifiers, session metadata, retrieval citations. On-prem means logs sit with deployer, which is privacy-positive AND satisfies Art 26(6) deployer duty.
eu-ai-act-art-12.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk; default-on for all SKUs.
eu-ai-act-art-12.operational_evidence: Inspect a deployment's log schema; verify ≥6-month retention configurable; review log-export interface.
eu-ai-act-art-12.pitfall: Logs that auto-rotate at 30 days. Log volume so high that retention becomes infeasible.
eu-ai-act-art-12.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox provides default-on, structured logging with configurable retention of at least six months, and a documented log schema, in alignment with Articles 12 and 26(6) of the EU AI Act.

crosswalk.note: Logging — Art 12 logs build on the security logging baseline
Monitoring activities — runtime monitoring overlap
Clock synchronisation — log integrity dependency
Logging & monitoring — Art 12 builds on privacy-aware logging baseline

business_models.note: AI Act record-keeping aligns with POPIA s 14 retention rules.

eu-ai-act-art-13.title: Transparency and Provision of Information to Deployers (high-risk)
eu-ai-act-art-13.requirement: High-risk systems shall be designed and developed in such a way as to ensure their operation is sufficiently transparent to enable deployers to interpret the system's output and use it appropriately. Instructions for use must accompany the system, providing concise, complete, correct, clear information including: provider identity; characteristics, capabilities, and limitations; foreseeable misuse; technical capabilities; specifications of input data; performance metrics; risks; circumstances that may affect output reliability; human oversight measures; computational and hardware resources; expected lifetime; maintenance and care; logs.
eu-ai-act-art-13.pb_interpretation: 'Instructions for use' is the central deployer-facing artifact. PB ships an Instructions for Use pack covering Art 13(3) topics. Even non-high-risk deployments benefit from this shape — it doubles as customer onboarding material.
eu-ai-act-art-13.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk; recommended structure for all SKUs.
eu-ai-act-art-13.operational_evidence: Pick a recent release; verify Instructions for Use cover all Art 13(3) topics; trace deployer-facing changes since prior release.
eu-ai-act-art-13.pitfall: Instructions written for compliance, not for the deployer's actual operational reality.
eu-ai-act-art-13.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox ships with Instructions for Use that cover the topics required under Article 13(3) of the EU AI Act, and that enable deployers to interpret the system's output and use it appropriately.

business_models.note: AI Act transparency obligations align with POPIA s 17-18 openness.

eu-ai-act-art-14.title: Human Oversight (high-risk)
eu-ai-act-art-14.requirement: High-risk systems shall be designed and developed in such a way that they can be effectively overseen by natural persons during the period in which they are in use. Human oversight measures shall enable understanding of the system's capacities and limitations; awareness of automation bias; correct interpretation of output; ability to decide not to use the system or to override output; ability to intervene or interrupt operation. For remote biometric ID systems, two-person verification.
eu-ai-act-art-14.pb_interpretation: PB designs oversight controls (sampling, override, escalation, refusal) into the product regardless of risk class. For high-risk variants, these are formalised; for standard SKUs, they support good operational hygiene and give deployers somewhere to plug in.
eu-ai-act-art-14.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk; built-in for all SKUs.
eu-ai-act-art-14.operational_evidence: Walk through a deployment's oversight UI; verify override paths function; review escalation playbook.
eu-ai-act-art-14.pitfall: Treating oversight as a UX afterthought rather than a core design requirement. Oversight that depends on monitoring volumes a human cannot realistically sustain.
eu-ai-act-art-14.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox provides human-oversight controls — including override paths, intervention, and interruption — designed into the product to support Article 14 of the EU AI Act.

business_models.note: AI Act human-oversight obligation supports POPIA s 71 substantial-effect safeguards.

eu-ai-act-art-15.title: Accuracy, Robustness, and Cybersecurity (high-risk)
eu-ai-act-art-15.requirement: High-risk systems shall be designed and developed to achieve appropriate level of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity, and to perform consistently throughout their lifecycle. Accuracy metrics declared in Instructions for Use. Resilience to errors, faults, inconsistencies. Resilience against unauthorised attempts to alter use, output, or performance, exploiting system vulnerabilities (data poisoning, model poisoning, adversarial examples, model evasion, confidentiality attacks).
eu-ai-act-art-15.pb_interpretation: For high-risk variants, accuracy/robustness/cyber are formal commitments declared in Instructions for Use. For all SKUs, PB maintains evaluation harness covering hallucination, prompt-injection resilience, jailbreak resistance, and data-leakage probes.
eu-ai-act-art-15.applies_rationale: Conditional (binding) for high-risk; engineered baseline for all SKUs.
documented_evidence: Cyber posture (security controls inventory crosswalked to ISO 27001)
eu-ai-act-art-15.operational_evidence: Inspect evaluation harness; verify recent adversarial test results; cross-reference cyber controls to ISO 27001.
eu-ai-act-art-15.cadence: Per release / per significant model change
eu-ai-act-art-15.pitfall: Declaring accuracy metrics that the system does not actually meet under real deployment conditions. Confusing 'we use TLS' with cybersecurity for an AI system.
eu-ai-act-art-15.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox declares accuracy and robustness metrics for any high-risk variant in its Instructions for Use, and maintains an adversarial-testing programme covering prompt injection, jailbreak, and data-leakage resistance, in alignment with Article 15 of the EU AI Act.

Protection against malware — model-poisoning analog

eu-ai-act-art-16.title: Obligations of Providers of high-risk AI systems
eu-ai-act-art-16.requirement: A high-risk provider must: ensure compliance with Articles 9-15; have a quality-management system (Art 17); keep documentation (Art 18); keep automatic logs (Art 19); take corrective actions (Art 20); cooperate with authorities (Art 21); appoint AR if non-EU (Art 22); affix CE marking and draw up EU declaration of conformity (Art 47, 48); register in EU database (Art 49).
eu-ai-act-art-16.pb_interpretation: Triggers only if PB knowingly provides a high-risk system. Right now: not the default product. But the Article 16 regime is the destination for any future PB-Annex-III SKU. Design QMS scaffolding now to ease the transition.
eu-ai-act-art-16.applies_rationale: Conditional — full applicability only on high-risk variant launch.
eu-ai-act-art-16.operational_evidence: Walk an external assessor through the QMS; show sample technical documentation; show DoC template.
eu-ai-act-art-16.pitfall: Believing CE marking is always self-declared — true for Annex III by default (internal control under Annex VI), but biometrics require notified body under Annex VII.
eu-ai-act-art-16.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox maintains internal documentation and quality-management practices that support Article 16 obligations should a high-risk variant be required for a specific deployment.

crosswalk.note: Internal audit — Art 16 quality discipline mirrors ISMS audit
Management review — Art 16 management oversight

eu-ai-act-art-17.title: Quality Management System
eu-ai-act-art-17.requirement: Providers of high-risk systems must operate a documented QMS covering at least: regulatory strategy; design and development controls; quality control; testing and validation; technical documentation; data governance; record-keeping; risk management; post-market monitoring; serious-incident reporting; communication with authorities; human resources / accountability; resource management.
eu-ai-act-art-17.pb_interpretation: Even non-high-risk operation benefits from a QMS that mirrors Article 17. ISO/IEC 42001 is strong scaffolding. PB targets ISO 42001 alignment as the QMS reference architecture, which gives Article 17 coverage by construction.
eu-ai-act-art-17.operational_evidence: ISO 42001 internal audit covers the Article 17 areas; management-review records exist.
eu-ai-act-art-17.pitfall: Mistaking ISO 27001 alone for a sufficient QMS — the AI lifecycle pieces are missing.
eu-ai-act-art-17.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox operates a quality-management system aligned with Article 17 of the EU AI Act and structured around ISO/IEC 42001 principles.

eu-ai-act-art-26.title: Deployer Obligations (high-risk)
eu-ai-act-art-26.requirement: Deployers must: (1) use the system per instructions; (2) assign human oversight to competent natural persons; (3) ensure relevance/appropriateness of input data they control; (4) monitor operation and notify provider/authorities of risks or serious incidents; (5) keep automatically generated logs at least 6 months; (6) inform workers' representatives and affected workers before workplace use; (7) public authorities register in EU database; (8) use Art 13 information for DPIAs; (9) inform affected natural persons subject to a high-risk AI system; (10) cooperate with authorities. Article 27 layers a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment for specific deployer categories.
eu-ai-act-art-26.pb_interpretation: Customer obligation, but PB must enable it — Instructions for Use, oversight tooling, log retention, worker-information templates. PB ships a Deployer Pack that gives the customer everything they need to discharge Art 26 with documented support.
eu-ai-act-art-26.applies_rationale: Customer is the addressee; PB enables.

role_applicability.provider_note: PB enables but is not the addressee.
role_applicability.deployer_note: Customer is the legal addressee for high-risk variants.

eu-ai-act-art-26.operational_evidence: Sample customer can produce all artifacts on request; deployer pack version-controlled and updated per release.
eu-ai-act-art-26.pitfall: Logs that auto-rotate at 30 days. Forgetting workers'-information duty (Art 26(7)).
eu-ai-act-art-26.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox supplies an Instructions for Use pack and configurable logging, oversight, and notification controls that enable deployers of high-risk variants to meet their obligations under Article 26 of the EU AI Act.

crosswalk.note: Customer agreement / instructions — deployer-as-controller direction-giving
Assist controller — analogous to deployer support obligations

business_models.note: AI Act deployer obligations require POPIA-style accountability where personal information is processed.
Designated humans (Art. 26.2) overlap with POPIA Information Officer duties.

eu-ai-act-art-27.title: Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (deployer)
eu-ai-act-art-27.requirement: Before deploying certain high-risk systems, deployers that are bodies governed by public law, private operators providing public services, or operators of certain Annex III systems (incl. credit/insurance for natural persons) must perform a FRIA covering: deployer's processes, period and frequency of use, categories of natural persons affected, specific risks of harm, human oversight measures, internal governance, complaint procedures.
eu-ai-act-art-27.pb_interpretation: PB does not perform a FRIA (it is not the deployer). But PB provides a deployer-FRIA aid template pre-filled with PB's stated intended purpose, foreseeable risks, oversight controls, so customers can adapt rather than start from zero.

role_applicability.deployer_note: Customer carries out and signs FRIA.

documented_evidence: Example FRIA for a fictional deployer
PB-side risk and oversight description (input to FRIA)
eu-ai-act-art-27.pitfall: Providing only a generic template that ignores the AI-specific risks customers actually face.

crosswalk.note: PIA/DPIA — FRIA extends DPIA with fundamental-rights layer

business_models.note: FRIA covers fundamental-rights impacts of automated decisions; POPIA s 71 analysis integrates.

eu-ai-act-art-49.title: Registration in the EU database
eu-ai-act-art-49.requirement: Providers (and certain public-authority deployers) of Annex III high-risk systems must register the system in the EU database before placing on the market or putting into service. Information per Annex VIII.
eu-ai-act-art-49.pb_interpretation: Triggers only if a high-risk variant is shipped. Database registration is a public-facing artifact that must be kept current — non-trivial post-launch obligation.
documented_evidence: Annex VIII content per registered system
eu-ai-act-art-49.pitfall: Registering once and not maintaining the entry as the system evolves.

eu-ai-act-art-50.title: Transparency Obligations (chat, synthetic content, deepfakes, public-interest text)
eu-ai-act-art-50.requirement: (1) Providers must inform persons interacting with the AI system that they are interacting with AI, unless this is obvious or use is for permitted criminal-law purposes. (2) Providers of generative AI systems must mark synthetic outputs in a machine-readable format and detectable as artificially generated, with technical solutions effective, interoperable, robust, reliable as far as technically feasible — except where the system performs only assistive editing or does not substantially alter input. (3) Deployers of emotion-recognition or biometric-categorisation systems must inform exposed natural persons. (4) Deployers of deepfake-generating systems must disclose; deployers of AI-generated text published with the purpose of informing the public on matters of public interest must disclose, with editorial-review carve-out. (5) Information must be provided in a clear and distinguishable manner at the latest at first interaction.
eu-ai-act-art-50.pb_interpretation: Article 50 is the obligation that DOES apply broadly — including to PB's typical office assistant. Persistent in-product disclosure, machine-readable marking of generated content, and deployer guidance for deepfake and public-interest text are all PB-side workstreams. Binding from 2 August 2026.
eu-ai-act-art-50.applies_rationale: Provider duties under 50(1) and 50(2); customer-side under 50(3)/(4)/(5).

role_applicability.provider_note: 50(1) AI disclosure + 50(2) machine-readable marking are provider duties.
role_applicability.deployer_note: 50(3) emotion/biometric disclosure + 50(4) deepfake/public-interest text labelling are deployer duties.

eu-ai-act-art-50.operational_evidence: Live demonstration of marking and verification; UX review against accessibility standards.
eu-ai-act-art-50.pitfall: Treating disclosure as a one-time pop-up. Stripping upstream provenance metadata in pipelines. Skipping marking for 'assistive' features that actually do generate substantial content. Confusing the provider duty (50(2)) with the deployer duty (50(4)).
eu-ai-act-art-50.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox surfaces a persistent indication that users are interacting with AI and marks generative outputs in a machine-readable format, in alignment with Article 50(1)-(2) of the EU AI Act, applicable from 2 August 2026, and the technical approaches reflected in the European Commission's voluntary draft Code of Practice on marking and labelling of AI-generated content.

crosswalk.note: Privacy notice — Art 50 disclosure parallels privacy transparency principle

source_refs: Code of Practice on Marking and Labelling of AI-generated Content (1st draft Dec 2025; 2nd draft Mar 2026; final June 2026) [C]

business_models.note: AI Act Art. 50 disclosure aligns with POPIA s 18 collection-notice obligations.

eu-ai-act-art-51-52-55.title: GPAI models with systemic risk — classification, notification, additional duties
eu-ai-act-art-51-52-55.requirement: Article 51 classifies GPAI models with 'systemic risk' (presumption when training compute exceeds 10²⁵ FLOP or by Commission designation under Annex XIII). Article 52: notification procedure; provider can rebut presumption. Article 55: GPAI-with-systemic-risk additional duties — model evaluations including adversarial testing, mitigation of systemic risks, serious-incident reporting to AI Office, cybersecurity protection.
eu-ai-act-art-51-52-55.pb_interpretation: PB extremely unlikely to train a model crossing 10²⁵ FLOP. Systemic-risk regime applies to frontier-lab providers. Relevant to PB only as upstream-supplier diligence — verify whether any GPAI model PB integrates is itself a systemic-risk model, since systemic-risk models carry their own transparency and reporting cascade downstream.
eu-ai-act-art-51-52-55.applies_rationale: PB is downstream integrator; systemic-risk obligations attach to upstream providers. Upstream-provider diligence remains.
documented_evidence: Per-model upstream supplier file flagging systemic-risk status
eu-ai-act-art-51-52-55.operational_evidence: For each integrated GPAI model: capture systemic-risk flag, link to upstream provider's compliance posture.
eu-ai-act-art-51-52-55.pitfall: Misreading the threshold as compliance-obligating rather than classification-triggering.
eu-ai-act-art-51-52-55.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox tracks whether the third-party general-purpose AI models it integrates fall under the systemic-risk regime in Articles 51-52 and 55 of the EU AI Act and preserves the upstream provider's compliance documentation accordingly.

eu-ai-act-art-53.title: GPAI Provider Obligations — documentation, copyright, training summary
eu-ai-act-art-53.requirement: Every GPAI provider must keep technical documentation (Annex XI), provide downstream-provider documentation (Annex XII), have a copyright-compliance policy under EU copyright law, and publish a sufficiently detailed summary of training content using the AI Office's published template. Free-and-open-licence models are partially exempt (only copyright + summary), unless they are systemic-risk.
eu-ai-act-art-53.pb_interpretation: Two sub-cases for PB: (i) downstream integrator of unmodified third-party GPAI (Scenarios A, B, C) — pass-through duty plus diligence on upstream provider; (ii) PB crosses the modification threshold (Scenario D) and becomes GPAI provider in respect of modifications — limited but real duties (Annex XI internal docs + Annex XII downstream pack + copyright policy + training-data summary, all specific to the fine-tune). Binding since 2 August 2025 for new GPAI models.
eu-ai-act-art-53.applies_rationale: Pass-through duty in all GPAI-touching scenarios; full GPAI-provider duty only in Scenario D.
documented_evidence: Per-model upstream supplier file (Annex XII pass-through)
Training-data summary (where PB triggers GPAI provider role)
Annex XI internal documentation (where PB triggers GPAI provider role)
eu-ai-act-art-53.operational_evidence: Pick a model; trace the chain: upstream provider → PB pass-through → customer deployer pack.
eu-ai-act-art-53.pitfall: Confusing GPAI **model** obligations (Articles 51-55) with AI **system** obligations. Over-claiming open-source exemption (does not apply if systemic-risk).
eu-ai-act-art-53.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox documents the third-party GPAI models it integrates, preserves the downstream documentation those providers supply under Article 53 of the EU AI Act, and where it materially modifies a model, prepares its own GPAI documentation in respect of the modifications.

crosswalk.note: Transfer basis — Annex XII downstream documentation
Transfer basis (processor scope)

eu-ai-act-art-54.title: Authorised Representatives for non-EU GPAI Providers
eu-ai-act-art-54.requirement: Non-EU GPAI providers must, prior to placing a GPAI model on the EU market, appoint an authorised representative in the EU by written mandate. AR keeps the technical documentation, cooperates with the AI Office and competent authorities, can terminate if provider non-compliant.
eu-ai-act-art-54.pb_interpretation: Triggers only if PB itself becomes a GPAI provider (Scenario D — fine-tunes above threshold) AND PB is non-EU. Distinct from Article 22 (which is for high-risk-system AR). Both ARs needed if both roles trigger.
eu-ai-act-art-54.applies_rationale: Conditional on Scenario D + non-EU PB establishment.
eu-ai-act-art-54.pitfall: Forgetting that high-risk AR (Art 22) and GPAI AR (Art 54) are separate; one AR may satisfy both, by design.

eu-ai-act-art-56.title: Codes of Practice (legal status)
eu-ai-act-art-56.requirement: The AI Office facilitates codes of practice. The Commission may approve a code by implementing act. Adherence is voluntary. Non-signatories must demonstrate compliance by other adequate means.
eu-ai-act-art-56.pb_interpretation: Codes are a route to compliance, not the compliance itself. PB can choose to align with codes (GPAI CoP if Scenario D triggers; Marking and Labelling CoP for Article 50(2)/(4)). Not legally required. Trade-off: signing creates presumption of compliance but binds PB to the code's terms; non-signing keeps flexibility but puts evidence burden on 'demonstrate by other means'.
eu-ai-act-art-56.applies_rationale: Governance article; signing decisions are PB-strategic.

eu-ai-act-art-72.title: Post-Market Monitoring (high-risk)
eu-ai-act-art-72.requirement: Providers of high-risk systems must operate a post-market monitoring system, with a documented plan, that actively and systematically collects, documents, and analyses data on system performance throughout its lifetime. Evidence used to update the risk-management system, the system itself, and to enable corrective actions.
eu-ai-act-art-72.pb_interpretation: Conditional on high-risk status. Even non-high-risk products need a de-facto incident response process covering AI-specific failure modes (model degradation, prompt injection, hallucinated outputs leading to harm, data leakage). PB designs PMM as default operational discipline.
eu-ai-act-art-72.applies_rationale: Binding for high-risk; recommended as default operational discipline.
documented_evidence: PMM plan template (per Art 72(3); Commission template forthcoming under Art 96/98)
Telemetry and customer-feedback channels feeding PMM
eu-ai-act-art-72.operational_evidence: Inspect PMM plan; verify telemetry feeds reach risk register; review last-quarter PMM cycle.
eu-ai-act-art-72.pitfall: Treating PMM as a 'v1.1 feature' rather than a day-1 process.
eu-ai-act-art-72.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox operates a post-market monitoring process with documented telemetry and feedback channels feeding a living risk register, in alignment with Article 72 of the EU AI Act for any high-risk variant.

crosswalk.note: Performance monitoring — PMM extends ISMS monitoring to AI lifecycle
Learning from incidents — feeds PMM data
Monitoring, measurement, analysis and evaluation
Continual improvement

business_models.note: AI Act post-market monitoring aligns with POPIA s 19 ongoing-safeguards verification.

eu-ai-act-art-73.title: Serious-Incident Reporting (high-risk)
eu-ai-act-art-73.requirement: Providers of high-risk systems must report serious incidents to market surveillance authorities of the Member State where the incident occurred. Timing: immediately and at most 15 days after the provider becomes aware of the serious incident or of the causal link; 2 days for widespread infringement or serious malfunction of critical infrastructure; 10 days for incidents resulting in death.
eu-ai-act-art-73.pb_interpretation: Conditional on high-risk status. Decision tree with the four timing buckets is a small but critical artifact; failure of timing is visible non-compliance. PB drills the decision tree annually even outside high-risk scope.
eu-ai-act-art-73.applies_rationale: Binding for high-risk; deployer also reports under Art 26(5).
documented_evidence: Liaison list per Member State market surveillance authority
eu-ai-act-art-73.operational_evidence: Run a tabletop exercise; verify timing decisions match the four buckets (2/10/15 days, plus 'immediately').
eu-ai-act-art-73.pitfall: Confusing the timing buckets. Treating 'serious malfunction' too narrowly.
eu-ai-act-art-73.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox maintains a serious-incident decision tree and reporting playbook aligned with the timing requirements of Article 73 of the EU AI Act for any high-risk variant.

crosswalk.note: Incident management planning — Art 73 has stricter timing
Assessment and decision on information security events
Response to information security incidents
Learning from incidents
Privacy incident management — Art 73 has stricter timing for high-risk AI

business_models.note: AI Act Art. 73 serious-incident reporting aligns with POPIA s 22 breach-notification timing.

iso-42001.full_name: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 Information technology — Artificial intelligence — Management system

companion_standards.name: AI System Impact Assessment
companion_standards.role: Guidance standard (not certifiable). Provides 10-element AISIA methodology referenced from Clause 6.1.4 and Annex A.5 controls. May be promoted to a separate framework YAML — see oq-42001-42005-separate.

companion_standards.name: Requirements for AI management system certification bodies
companion_standards.role: Governs accreditation of certification bodies, not orgs. PB benchmarks audit-readiness against 42006 expectations (auditor competence, audit-time calculation, sampling). Never claim "ISO 42006 compliant" — it is for CBs.

companion_standards.name: Artificial intelligence concepts and terminology
companion_standards.role: Normatively referenced from 42001 for definitions. Aligned terminology used throughout.

companion_standards.name: Information technology — Artificial intelligence — Guidance on risk management
companion_standards.role: Reference methodology for Clause 6.1 AI risk assessment. Used to deepen Annex C-driven risk-source analysis.

pb_application.why_chosen: ISO/IEC 42001 is the foundational AI Management System (AIMS) standard for a vendor selling AI-platform technology to confidential firms. PrivateBox's on-premises architecture is a control, not a governance system; AIMS duties (policy, governance, impact assessment, lifecycle, supplier governance, transparency, oversight, incident handling, continual improvement) remain in scope regardless of deployment model. 42001 is the natural extension of PB's ISO 27001 ISMS and ISO 27701 PIMS — it shares the Annex SL high-level structure, allowing a single integrated management system rather than three parallel systems. Commercially, 42001 is the AIMS standard buyers in legal, finance and healthcare procurement processes ask about (see Anthropic, AWS, Google, Microsoft, Workday, Cognizant accredited certifications listed in source pack §14). PB's positioning is "designed in alignment with ISO/IEC 42001:2023" pre-certification, with an accredited-certification roadmap benchmarked to ISO/IEC 42006:2025 audit expectations. Never claim "ISO 42001 certified" until and unless an accredited certification body issues a certificate against a defined AIMS scope.

on_prem.rationale: AIMS scope statement (per source §2 target wording): "The design, development, packaging, distribution, support, and continual improvement of the PrivateBox on-premises single-tenant AI platform, including its model selection/evaluation pipeline, RAG and connector framework, update and telemetry services, and customer support operations. Excluded: customer-operated deployments after handover, customer fine-tuning of models inside their environment, and customer-provided data — these remain under the customer's own AIMS / governance." This scope is product-and-operations bounded, deliberately narrow enough to be defensible in a Stage-1 audit, broad enough to cover every layer the customer touches at procurement.

ai_factory.rationale: AIMS scope extends in principle to ringfenced multi-tenant deployments at a partner data centre. Several controls have divergent ownership pending partner-DC selection (see oq-partner-dc-selection): A.4.5 system/computing resources, A.6.2.5 deployment, A.10 supplier programme. Where ownership genuinely differs, requirements carry owner_by_model with the AI Factory side flagged status:pending. Until partner DC is selected, PB cannot finalise Stage-1 audit scope for the AI Factory model. AI Factory remains aspirational; no AI Factory certification claim should be made.

top_risks: Misclassifying PB's AI role (provider vs deployer) — leads to wrong AIMS duties, wrong contract language, exposure to obligations the customer should bear (Annex C.2.1, A.10.4).
AIMS scope drift in marketing claims that imply PB governs customer AI use, creating joint-controller legal exposure under POPIA/GDPR (§2 A6, §10 red-flag list).
Foreseeable-misuse coverage in AISIA records (ISO 42005 6.8.3) — one of the most commonly under-implemented elements per source §6 caveat 8; mandatory in every AISIA, not best-practice add-on.
Bias methodology that is awareness-only rather than measurable — major nonconformity per Schellman/orbit.reconn.io findings (Annex C.2.5, A.6.1.4).

Third-party / open-weight model supply chain (Llama, Mistral) — A.10.2 supplier governance cannot be excluded; "pulled-from-Hugging-Face" model adoption without due diligence is a known finding pattern.
Hidden change management — silent retrains, RAG-corpus updates, prompt-template changes that bypass change control (Clause 6.3, A.6).
Telemetry boundary leakage — customer content (vs operational metadata) crossing trust boundary creates POPIA/GDPR exposure (A.7.5, §7 telemetry scenario).
Internal audit conducted by the AIMS team itself — auditor independence is required by Clause 9.2 and emphasised by ISO/IEC 42006; failing this is a Stage-2 finding pattern.

soa_guidance.principles: The Statement of Applicability is the bridge between AIMS scope, AI risk treatment and Annex A control implementation. Auditors read it first. Per source §5 and §15, every Annex A control must appear in PB's SoA with: applicability decision (Y/Shared/Conditional/N/A), implementation status, owner, evidence pointer, and risk-based justification. SoA must reflect the published Annex A numbering — source §5 flags a known industry discrepancy (38 vs 39 vs 42 controls) tracked in oq-42001-annex-numbering. PB should add a deployment-model column showing whether each control is PB-owned, CL-owned or SH for on-prem vs AI Factory. That makes the SoA defensible in enterprise procurement and reduces audit-time per ISO/IEC 42006.
soa_guidance.na_justification_rules: Per Note 3 of Clause 6.1.3, exclusions are permitted with documented justification. Tie every exclusion to scope boundaries, architecture facts, accepted residual risk, or alternative controls. A.10.2 (suppliers) and A.7 (data) cannot be excluded by an org that uses third-party models or processes any data — these are always in scope for PB. Empty justifications are a known Schellman finding pattern.
soa_guidance.honesty_rules: The SoA must not imply controls are implemented when they are planned, customer-owned or out of scope. Show the implemented/planned state honestly. External-facing SoA summaries follow the safe-claim language in source §10 and §17 — never claim "100% covered" without showing exclusion justifications. Preserve a line back to the AI risk register and AISIA records.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: PB AIMS — covering PB's design/build/distribution/support of the platform.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer AIMS — covering customer's deployment, use, fine-tuning, customer-data governance.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Per source §2 A6, PB does NOT govern the customer's AI use. Two AIMS, one trust interface.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: PB AI Policy v1.x (top-management approved).
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer's own AI policy for their internal AI use.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: PB provides a policy-template starter; customer adapts to sector and context.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: PB AI risk methodology (product-level risks, supply chain, lifecycle).
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer-side AI risk register for their use cases.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Methodologies aligned to ISO 23894 + Annex C; cross-readable.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: AISIA at product / feature level; PB provides 42005-aligned methodology.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: AISIA at customer use-case level (sector, decision context, affected populations).
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Per A.5 and §12. PB provides Annex-E-style template; customer adapts per use case.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: SDLC for the product, integrating NIST SP 800-218A; build, train, V&V, package, deploy.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer change management for their deployment, fine-tuning, RAG corpus.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: NIST 800-218A used as overlay on the build pipeline.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: PB pre-deployment gate before shipping a build/release.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer's own go/no-go for installing into their environment.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Per A.6.2.5. PB ships, customer accepts; both retain approval records.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: Product-level KPIs, drift detectors in the product, vendor-side incident triage.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer operates the deployed system; primary monitoring of their use.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Telemetry boundary defines what PB sees (operational metadata only by default).

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: Vulnerability triage, product-side advisories, EU AI Act Art. 73 serious-incident notifications for product flaws.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Incident handling for customer's use-case-side incidents.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: 15-day serious-incident window (EU AI Act); 72-hour breach window (POPIA s.22).

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: Telemetry pipeline; data dictionary; PII-free guarantee.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer accepts/declines telemetry per opt-in.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Default-off or strictly-bounded; customer-content never in telemetry.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: Just-in-time access workflow; session recording; access reviews.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer approves each session; session is fully logged.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Standing access is forbidden — a known §7 pitfall.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: Cryptographically signed updates; SBOM; vulnerability disclosures; staged rollout.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer-controlled update windows; rollback authority.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Aligned to NIST SSDF / SLSA-equivalent supply-chain controls.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: Approved-models list; model-card review; bias/safety eval; licence due diligence.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer-side acceptance of models proposed for their deployment.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: A.10.2. "Pulled-from-Hugging-Face" model adoption without DD is forbidden.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: Ingestion framework, quality gates, sensitive-data handling controls.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer owns the corpus, content rights, end-of-life data handling.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: PB provides controls; customer attests rights and PII handling.

evidence_families.name: AIMS Scope Statement and AI Policy
examples: PrivateBox AI Policy v1.x with principles, prohibited uses, exceptions
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Foundation of the AIMS; without it, every downstream control lacks anchor.

evidence_families.name: Governance Charter and RACI
examples: RACI matrix (AI Gov Lead, Model Risk Lead, AI Security Lead, Data/RAG Steward, Support AI Liaison)
AI Governance Committee Terms of Reference + minutes
Whistleblower / concern-reporting SOP and concern log

evidence_families.name: AI Risk Methodology and Risk Register
examples: AI risk methodology document (aligned with ISO 23894 + Annex C)
AI risk register (per system in scope; treatments traceable to SoA)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Pre-emptive against the "renamed InfoSec methodology" finding pattern.

evidence_families.name: AISIA Methodology (42005-aligned) and per-system AISIA records
examples: AISIA methodology covering 10 elements per ISO 42005:2025
Harm/benefit taxonomy aligned to 42005 Annex C
Per-system AISIA records with foreseeable-misuse section completed
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Compulsory under A.5 and Clause 6.1.4; foreseeable-misuse coverage is a common gap.
evidence_families.cadence: Pre-deployment + on material change + scheduled review

evidence_families.name: AI System Inventory
examples: Inventory of every model / system / version / dataset / owner / status / deployment locations
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Without an inventory, the SoA scope is indefensible.

evidence_families.name: Data Provenance, Datasheets and RAG controls
examples: Datasheets per training/evaluation dataset (provenance, demographics, rights, labelling)
Data quality criteria and remediation records
RAG / data-ingestion controls SOP (rights, quality, sensitive-data handling)
Customer-data telemetry data dictionary + opt-in statement

evidence_families.name: Bias Assessment Methodology and reports
examples: Bias methodology with baselines + retest cadence
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: A.6.1.4 is an EU AI Act gap area; awareness statements are not methodologies.

evidence_families.name: V&V, Robustness and Adversarial Test artefacts
examples: Test plans (functional + adversarial + edge + drift + fairness + security)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: A.6.1.5; functional-only testing is a known weakness.

evidence_families.name: Lifecycle SOP and change management
examples: AI lifecycle SOP (cradle-to-grave; integrates NIST SP 800-218A)
Change-control templates including model-swap / RAG / prompt-template / environment changes
