evidence_families.name: Human Oversight design and records
examples: Human Oversight SOP (override authority + monitoring + concern channel)
Per-system oversight design (where humans-in-the-loop sit, what they can stop)
Sector-specific oversight rules (healthcare, legal, finance default to human-in-the-loop)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: A.6.2.3 + EU AI Act Art. 14; rubber-stamp oversight is an audit failure.

evidence_families.name: AI event logging, monitoring and KPIs
examples: AI Event-Logging Standard (schema, retention, integrity, customer-control)
Monitoring & KPI Dashboard Specification (drift, fairness, incidents, support tickets)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: A.6.2.4 + 9.1; logs that exist but don't link to AIMS elements fail audit.

evidence_families.name: Incident response and notification
examples: 15-day EU AI Act Art. 73 serious-incident workflow
Past incident records with cause/closure trace
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Late notification is a regulatory exposure, not just a finding.

evidence_families.name: Supplier and model-vendor due diligence
examples: Supplier / Model-Vendor DD pack including open-weight model evaluation
Per-model approval records (eval results, accepted residual risks, approver)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: A.10.2; cannot be excluded; "pulled-from-Hugging-Face syndrome" is a known finding.

evidence_families.name: Customer-facing transparency / user information
examples: Transparency / User Information Pack (capabilities, limitations, conditions of non-use, change log)
Customer Shared-Responsibility Matrix (productised version of source §7)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: A.8 + EU AI Act Art. 13/50/52; sales-driven inflation is a typical risk.

evidence_families.name: Support access SOP and Telemetry data dictionary
examples: Telemetry data dictionary + default-off / opt-in statement
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Hidden personal-data egress via telemetry is a POPIA/GDPR risk; standing support access is forbidden.

evidence_families.name: Update delivery and supply-chain attestation
examples: Update-Delivery Standard (signing, SBOM, vulnerability disclosure, staged rollout)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: A.10.2 + NIST SSDF; software supply chain is a top vector.

evidence_families.name: Internal Audit programme and Management Review
examples: Internal Audit Programme (independent of AIMS team)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: 9.2 + 9.3; same team auditing itself fails impartiality requirement (42006).
evidence_families.cadence: At least annual; more often during early maturity

evidence_families.name: Nonconformity and CAPA log
examples: NC / CAPA log entries (each with cause, action, effectiveness review, closure date)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: 10.2; an NC log that records issues but never closes them is a finding pattern.

evidence_families.name: Statement of Applicability and crosswalks
examples: SoA covering every Annex A control (applicability + status + owner + evidence pointer + justification)
Crosswalk Pack (42001 × 27001 × 27701 × NIST AI RMF × EU AI Act × POPIA × King V)
SoA with deployment-model annotations (PB / SH / CL per business model)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: 6.1.3; auditor reads SoA first.
evidence_families.cadence: At least annual + per scope change

evidence_families.name: Trust Pack language guide and external claims log
examples: Trust-Pack Language Guide (pre-approved phrasing, red-flag list)
External-claims register (where each claim is made + supporting evidence pointer)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: External overclaim creates regulatory and reputation exposure independent of audit.

evidence_families.name: Competence matrix and training records
examples: Competence matrix (AI ethics, ML risk, AI secure SDLC, data governance, model evaluation, jurisdictional law)
Awareness records (all staff, including support engineers)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: 7.2; auditor samples randomly — staff who can't describe AI policy fail.

evidence_families.name: Certification readiness pack (42006-benchmarked)
examples: CB selection record (impartiality check — separation of advisory and audit)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: 42006 expectations shape what auditors will demand; pre-mapped evidence reduces audit time.

evidence_families.name: AISIA template library (42005 Annex E-style)
examples: Sector-specific AISIA addenda (healthcare safety case, finance fairness, legal privilege)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: AISIA quality varies wildly; templated approach reduces variance and gap risk.

evidence_families.name: Connector security and integration controls
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: A.10 + complexity-of-environment risk source (Annex C); connector misconfig is a top vector.

evidence_families.name: Backup and disaster recovery for AI systems
examples: DR / failover procedures appropriate to on-prem
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Backups carry data; cross-jurisdictional storage exposure is a POPIA/GDPR risk.

scenarios.name: Pure on-prem deployment, no remote support
scenarios.description: Customer runs PrivateBox entirely within their own infrastructure with no PB telemetry, support access, or remote operations. PB's role is provider/producer; customer is sole operator.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Customer-facing obligations dominate; runbook + handover record required.
Supplier-side obligations limited to model providers and update channel.
Intended-use enforcement is largely customer-side; PB documentation is the lever.

scenarios.notes: Lowest PB operational liability; highest documentation rigour required.

scenarios.name: On-prem with vendor-managed update delivery
scenarios.description: PrivateBox controls the model/code supply chain via signed updates, SBOMs, change notifications, and rollback authority. Customer accepts or defers updates. PB role: provider with software supply chain responsibility.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Documentation of changes; update integrity required.
PB is supplier of updates; customer's supplier-DD applies to PB.
Update is a planned change to AIMS-governed system.

scenarios.notes: Cryptographic signing + SBOM are non-negotiable.

scenarios.name: On-prem with operational-metadata telemetry
scenarios.description: Telemetry pipeline returns operational metadata to PB (no customer content). Telemetry is opt-in, bounded by a published data dictionary, and PII-free by design.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Event log scope explicitly bounds what is in telemetry.
Telemetry must not carry personal information; technical evidence required.
Customer transparency on what telemetry collects and why.

scenarios.notes: Hidden payloads are the risk — data dictionary is the control.

scenarios.name: On-prem with just-in-time PB support access
scenarios.description: PB engineers gain time-bounded access to customer environment for troubleshooting. Each session is customer-approved, fully recorded, and bounded by a documented data-handling protocol. Standing access is prohibited.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Support engineer becomes part of the oversight surface.
Customer relationship terms govern session approval.
Per-session data-handling protocol when customer data is in scope.

scenarios.notes: Insider data exposure risk — session recording is the audit hook.

scenarios.name: Managed deployment-time support engagement
scenarios.description: PB engineers hands-on during the install of the platform into customer environment. Engagement is scope-bounded and time-boxed; a formal handover record terminates PB operational involvement.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Pre-deployment gate is jointly signed by PB and customer.
Pre-go-live testing performed per documented plan.

scenarios.notes: Risk is PB perceived as joint controller — handover record is the boundary.

scenarios.name: Troubleshooting requiring access to customer data
scenarios.description: Exception-only — PB engineers access customer data to diagnose an issue. Default is no access; activated case-by-case with explicit customer authorisation and data-minimisation discipline.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: PII processing context — POPIA/GDPR exposure if mishandled.
Customer authorisation per incident is contractual.
Access is logged in the event log per A.6.2.4.

scenarios.notes: Privilege/confidentiality risk in legal sector — preferred path is no access.

scenarios.name: Document ingestion and RAG corpus setup support
scenarios.description: PB advises customer on RAG corpus structure, ingestion pipeline, quality gates. Customer owns the corpus, content rights, and PII handling decisions. PB provides controls and templates only.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Data governance frame for the corpus.
Quality criteria for ingested content.
Provenance, rights, demographics for ingested data.

scenarios.notes: Customer attestation of rights and PII handling is the legal-defensibility hook.

scenarios.name: Backup support
scenarios.description: Backup infrastructure carries customer data; encryption, location, retention, and customer-managed keys must be defined.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Backup data may contain PII; encryption + key management apply.
Backup operations logged per event-log standard.

scenarios.notes: Cross-jurisdictional storage is the POPIA/GDPR exposure.

scenarios.name: Integration / connector security
scenarios.description: PB-built or PB-advised connectors that link PrivateBox to customer systems (SharePoint, Slack, document repositories, etc.). Least-privilege, documented permission scope, pen-tested.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Each connector is a third-party integration with supplier-DD implications.
Connector may surface PII into AI context; minimisation rules apply.

scenarios.notes: Over-broad credentials is the typical pitfall.

scenarios.name: Hosted update repository / model package distribution
scenarios.description: PB serves software supply chain — signed updates, model packages, SBOMs, vulnerability disclosure. Customer pulls on schedule.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: PB is the supplier; customer's supplier programme tests PB's controls.
Build / package documentation is PB's artefact.

scenarios.notes: SLSA-equivalent attestation is the bar.

scenarios.name: Customer fine-tuning / adaptation of models
scenarios.description: Customer changes model behaviour via fine-tuning or LoRA-style adaptation in their environment. PB provides safe-tuning guidance, guardrails, and post-tune evaluation templates. Outcome of fine-tuning is customer-owned.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Fine-tuning may introduce or amplify bias; post-tune eval required.
Customer obligations for fine-tuned outputs.
Intended-use scope may shift after fine-tuning.

scenarios.notes: PB blamed for customer-induced bias is the risk — guardrails + guidance are PB's controls.

scenarios.name: Customer use of third-party models within PrivateBox
scenarios.description: PB hosts the integration layer; customer chooses among approved third-party models (open-weight or proprietary). Approved-models list and addition workflow govern.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Each model is a supplier; DD pack required.
Robustness testing is per-model.

scenarios.notes: Unsanctioned models is the risk — approved-list discipline is the control.

scenarios.name: AI Factory — ringfenced multi-tenant deployment in partner DC
scenarios.description: Aspirational deployment shape. Partner DC hosts ringfenced multi-tenant infrastructure for PrivateBox. Multi-tenant isolation, partner-DC supplier governance, and physical-security distinctions all in play. Pending partner-DC selection.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Computing resources owned by partner DC; PB integrates.
Partner DC is a supplier subject to DD.
Scope statement extends to ringfenced multi-tenant deployment.

scenarios.notes: Blocked on oq-partner-dc-selection. Until partner DC chosen, this scenario is conditional.

open_questions.question: How does PrivateBox align Annex A control numbering with the published ISO/IEC 42001:2023 standard?
candidate_answers: Adopt the PECB-aligned 39-control numbering used in source pack §5
Adopt the published ISO numbering once the official PDF is licensed
Adopt a hybrid PB-internal numbering with mapping table to ISO
open_questions.notes: Source §5 explicitly flags 38 vs 39 vs 42 control discrepancies across secondary sources. Authoritative count is in the paywalled ISO PDF. Interim: this YAML uses the PECB-aligned numbering from source §5; SoA must align to published numbering before Stage 1 audit. v1 YAML carries 39 Annex A controls.

open_questions.question: Which accredited certification body will PrivateBox engage for ISO/IEC 42001 certification?
open_questions.notes: Choice is partly geographic (UK, EU, ZA, US presence) and partly impartiality (any CB used for advisory cannot then audit — see source §13). Decision affects audit-time calculation per ISO/IEC 42006 and the timing of Stage 1 readiness. Track in roadmap §16 LATER bucket.

open_questions.question: What subset of Annex A controls comprises PrivateBox's Stage-1 certification scope?
candidate_answers: Source §16 NOW phase subset (A.2 + A.3 + AIMS scope + risk methodology + AI Policy)
Full Annex A coverage (most rigorous, longer Stage 1)
Phased scope expanding by surveillance cycle
open_questions.notes: PB's commercial position favours product-scoped certification (per case studies — AWS, Google, Workday, Microsoft Service Trust Portal pattern in §14). Setting maturity_target:Cert on the Stage-1 subset commits PB publicly. Until decided, all requirements default maturity_target:AR.

open_questions.question: Should ISO/IEC 42005 be promoted to a separate framework YAML?
candidate_answers: Keep as companion only (current state); 42005's 10-element AISIA model is absorbed into Clause 6.1.4 and A.5 controls.
Author iso-42005.yaml as a separate framework so the OS can render an AISIA-specific view.
open_questions.notes: Promoting to separate framework gives the OS a dedicated AISIA-methodology view (useful for procurement when buyers ask "show me your impact-assessment process"). Current state suffices for v1; revisit when AISIA Methodology is published.

open_questions.question: When does PrivateBox add King V as a framework and activate AI-related crosswalks?
candidate_answers: Now (King V effective for financial years from 2026-01-01; PB FY2026 starts soon)
At authoring of king-v.yaml; pending crosswalks captured in PENDING-CROSSWALKS.md until then
open_questions.notes: King V is binding for SA companies effective FYs starting on/after 2026-01-01. PB's board narrative (source §17) already references King V principles. Pending crosswalks captured in frameworks/iso-42001/PENDING-CROSSWALKS.md.

open_questions.question: When does PrivateBox license the official ISO/IEC 42001:2023 PDF (and 42005, 42006)?
candidate_answers: When current PB-curated source pack reaches a known gap
open_questions.notes: Source §3 caveat — current authoring is from PB-curated authoritative summaries (NIST crosswalk, Schellman/BSI/DNV/A-LIGN guidance). Final claim language and SoA numbering require the official paywalled text. Resolve before any external Stage-1 readiness review or accredited audit.

open_questions.question: Which DC partner(s) for AI Factory deployments?
open_questions.notes: Cross-framework OQ — also blocks ISO 27001 A.7 physical controls. Until partner DC is selected, AI Factory ownership of system / computing resources (A.4.5), AIMS scope statement extension (cl-4.3), and partner-DC supplier obligations (A.10) carry status:pending. Mirrored in iso-27001.yaml.

requirements.title: Understanding the organisation and its context
requirements.requirement: Determine internal and external issues relevant to the AIMS purpose and ability to achieve intended outcomes. Determine PrivateBox's role in the AI ecosystem (provider, producer, customer, partner — for PB: provider + producer).
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor verifies the AIMS scope traces from documented internal/external context, including the PB-as-provider-and-producer role declaration.
requirements.pb_interpretation: PrivateBox must explicitly position itself as an AI system provider and component producer to confidential firms; not a deployer of AI on behalf of those firms. Customer environment is external context; PB R&D / support environment is internal context. Both must be captured. Refresh annually and on material change (new jurisdiction, new sector, new model class, regulatory change).
requirements.operational_evidence: Dated, version-controlled context register signed off by top management; traceable to AIMS scope and risk criteria.
requirements.failure_modes: Generic boilerplate that doesn't reflect PB; not refreshed; ignores POPIA/EU AI Act/King V; doesn't name PB's AI role; AIMS scope can't be derived from context.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox's AIMS is contextualised for confidential-firm AI in legal, finance and healthcare across ZA, EU, US and UK.
requirements.scope_note: Customer environment is external context input; PB R&D and support environments are internal.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned. AIMS context extends ISMS context with AI-role declaration.
HLS-aligned. AIMS context complements PIMS context with AI scope.
NIST AI RMF GOV 1.1 legal / regulatory understanding aligns with AIMS context.

requirements.title: Understanding the needs and expectations of interested parties
requirements.requirement: Identify interested parties relevant to the AIMS, their relevant requirements, and which become AIMS requirements.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor verifies stakeholder requirements are captured and traceable to AIMS controls.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Interested parties include: customers (legal/finance/healthcare CISOs, DPOs, GCs), data subjects (clients of customers), regulators (Information Regulator ZA, EU AI Office, UK ICO, US sectoral), employees, third-party model providers, support partners, investors. Maintain a stakeholder register with documented expectations and requirement-traceability matrix.
requirements.operational_evidence: Evidence that interested-party requirements map to specific Annex A controls and AIMS objectives.
requirements.failure_modes: Customer-only view; missing data subjects and regulators; no traceability to controls; static register that doesn't update on regulatory change.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox's AIMS systematically captures and addresses the requirements of customers, data subjects, regulators and supply-chain partners.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned. AIMS adds AI-specific stakeholders (regulators, data subjects in AI context).
HLS-aligned; PIMS interested-party identification extends to AI-context data subjects.
NIST AI RMF GOV 5.1 external feedback aligns with interested-parties identification.

requirements.title: Determining the scope of the AI management system
requirements.requirement: Maintain a documented AIMS scope statement covering boundaries, products, services, locations and AI roles.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor checks scope is precise, coherent and defensible — narrow enough to be defensible, broad enough to be credible.
requirements.pb_interpretation: See target wording in pb_application.scope_decisions.on_prem.rationale. Scope is product-and-operations bounded; explicitly excludes customer-operated deployments after handover, customer fine-tuning inside their environment, and customer-provided data — these remain under the customer's own AIMS. Scope statement is 1 page, top-management-signed, referenced from policy. Scope expansion to AI Factory pending oq-partner-dc-selection.
requirements.operational_evidence: Signed scope statement traceable to context register and to AIMS objectives.
requirements.failure_modes: Scope drift in marketing; scope that seems to extend over customer environments (creates legal exposure); scope mismatched with what auditor sees in operations.
requirements.external_safe_claim: Our AIMS scope covers the design, build and support of PrivateBox; customer-operated deployments are governed by the customer's own AIMS, which we enable with documentation.
requirements.scope_note: Stage-1 scope statement is the single most-read document in the audit.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; AIMS scope is parallel to ISMS scope but distinct in coverage.
HLS-aligned; AIMS scope distinct from PIMS scope.

requirements.title: AI management system
requirements.requirement: Establish, implement, maintain and continually improve the AIMS.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor expects to see a coherent AIMS architecture document showing how clauses 4–10 and Annex A connect, and how AIMS integrates with ISMS / PIMS.
requirements.pb_interpretation: PDCA cycle integrated with PB's ISO 27001 ISMS and ISO 27701 PIMS. Single management system, three labels. AIMS Manual / Overview describes process architecture and integration points.
requirements.operational_evidence: Auditor walks the AIMS architecture; cross-references should resolve.
requirements.cadence: Annual review of AIMS architecture document
requirements.failure_modes: Three parallel siloed management systems instead of one integrated; AIMS exists on paper only.
requirements.external_safe_claim: AIMS architecture aligned to ISO/IEC 42001, integrated with our existing ISMS and PIMS.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; PB runs one integrated management system surface.
HLS-aligned.

requirements.title: Leadership and commitment
requirements.requirement: Top management demonstrates leadership and commitment to the AIMS — measurable, not delegable.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor checks AIMS sponsorship is at executive level, with measurable commitment and resource allocation; AIMS sponsored only at IT manager level is a frequent Schellman finding.
requirements.pb_interpretation: CEO and Board own AI accountability. Documented in board / Exco minutes; CEO sign-off on AI Policy and AIMS scope; visible budget allocation. AI Governance Committee has executive representation. King V Principle 1 (Ethical leadership) and POPIA accountability principle apply.
documented_evidence: CEO sign-off on AI Policy and AIMS Scope Statement
Budget allocation evidence (AI Gov function, audit)
AI Governance Committee Terms of Reference
requirements.operational_evidence: Top management can describe AI Policy and AIMS in interview; resource decisions trace to AIMS objectives.
requirements.failure_modes: AIMS sponsored only at IT-manager level; no executive interview readiness; no budget trace.
requirements.external_safe_claim: AIMS commitment is documented at top-management level with measurable resourcing.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; same leadership pattern across ISMS / PIMS / AIMS.
HLS-aligned.
Deployer obligations under Art. 26 require designated humans and oversight at organisational level.
POPIA accountability principle (s 8) requires top-management oversight of personal-information processing; AIMS leadership scope covers PB-as-RP duties.
NIST AI RMF GOV 2.3 executive responsibility aligns with AIMS leadership.

requirements.title: AI Policy
requirements.requirement: Top management establishes, communicates and maintains an AI Policy framed by business strategy, ethics, risk environment and legal requirements, including a process for handling exceptions.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor tests whether the AI Policy is documented, approved, communicated, available to interested parties, and understood by random employees on interview.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Linked to A.2.2. PrivateBox AI Policy v1.x with principles, scope, prohibited uses, exceptions process, owner and version. Disseminated; staff can describe core principles. Reviewed annually and on trigger (new framework, regulatory change, material incident).
requirements.operational_evidence: Random employee interviewed by auditor can describe AI Policy core principles.
requirements.failure_modes: Boilerplate policy not tailored to PB; sales team can't describe it; exceptions handled informally.
requirements.external_safe_claim: Our AI Policy, approved by top management, governs every PrivateBox AI activity.

crosswalk.note: AI Policy parallels InfoSec Policy; cross-policy alignment required.
AI Policy aligns with PIMS Privacy Policy.
AI Policy operationalises POPIA accountability for AI-specific processing; cross-policy alignment with PB privacy notice required.
NIST AI RMF GOV 1.2 trustworthy-AI characteristics integrated into policies.

requirements.title: Roles, responsibilities and authorities
requirements.requirement: Documented assignment of roles, responsibilities and authorities for AIMS conformance and reporting.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor checks named owners exist for each AIMS function and that authority lines support reporting on AIMS performance.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Linked to A.3.2. RACI plus AI Governance Charter naming AI Governance Lead, Model Risk Lead, AI Security Lead, Data/RAG Steward, Customer Support AI Liaison. Cross-functional AI Governance Committee meets on fixed cadence. Same person may not own development AND oversight (Annex A.3.2 failure pattern).
requirements.operational_evidence: Each AI role has a named individual who can speak to their responsibility in interview.
requirements.failure_modes: Same person owns development and oversight; AI Governance Lead role exists but has no real authority.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox operates a documented cross-functional AI governance structure with named owners and committee oversight.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; AI roles overlay InfoSec roles.
HLS-aligned; Information Officer role required by POPIA also overlaps.
Deployer Art. 26.2 designated-humans obligation parallels AIMS role assignment.
NIST AI RMF GOV 2.1 roles and responsibilities documented.

requirements.title: Actions to address risks and opportunities
requirements.requirement: Determine risks and opportunities for the AIMS to achieve intended outcomes, prevent or reduce undesired effects, and continually improve.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor checks risk-and-opportunity determination is documented and feeds into the AI risk methodology and AIMS objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Risks-and-opportunities discussion is the bridge between context (4.1), interested parties (4.2) and AI objectives (6.2). Documented as input to AIMS planning.
requirements.operational_evidence: Planning record is referenced in management review and informs the AI risk register.
requirements.failure_modes: Generic risk-only treatment; no opportunity identification; not traceable to objectives.
requirements.external_safe_claim: AIMS planning addresses both AI risks and opportunities under documented criteria.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; AIMS planning parallels ISMS planning.
HLS-aligned (27701 collapses 6.1.1-6.1.3 into 6.1).

requirements.title: AI risk assessment
requirements.requirement: Maintain a documented AI risk assessment methodology with criteria, acceptance, repeatability, informed by Annex C objectives / risk sources and ISO/IEC 23894.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor verifies the methodology is AI-specific (not a renamed InfoSec methodology) and is actually used to populate an AI risk register per system in scope.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Methodology must address AI-specific risk sources (bias, fairness, robustness, foreseeable misuse, automation level, ML-specific risks) per Annex C. Per-system AI risk register references the methodology. Reuse of 27001 methodology under a new label is a known failure pattern.
documented_evidence: AI Risk Register (per system in scope)
requirements.operational_evidence: Risk register entries trace to methodology criteria; treatments link to SoA controls.
requirements.cadence: Quarterly register review; annual methodology review
requirements.failure_modes: Generic InfoSec methodology renamed; no Annex C coverage; static register.
requirements.external_safe_claim: AI-specific risk methodology consistent with ISO/IEC 42001 and ISO/IEC 23894, covering Annex C objectives and risk sources.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; AI risk methodology distinct from but integrated with InfoSec risk methodology.
AIMS risk integrates with PIMS risk for AI-with-PII contexts.
EU AI Act Art. 9 risk-management system for high-risk AI is satisfied via AIMS risk methodology.
AI risk methodology must address POPIA s 19 security safeguards as a core risk class for any AI handling personal information.
NIST AI RMF MAP 1.6 system requirements account for socio-technical risk.
NIST AI RMF MEASURE 1.1 risk metric selection.

requirements.title: AI risk treatment
requirements.requirement: Document an AI risk treatment plan with control selection, and produce a Statement of Applicability comparing selected controls to Annex A. Note 3 permits exclusions with documented justification.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor reads the SoA first; expects every Annex A control with applicability decision, owner, evidence pointer, justification.
requirements.pb_interpretation: SoA is THE bridge document. One row per Annex A control: applicability (Y / Shared / Conditional / N/A), implementation status, control owner, evidence pointer, justification. Add deployment-model column (PB / SH / CL per business model). Empty justifications are a known Schellman finding pattern.
documented_evidence: Statement of Applicability (every Annex A control)
requirements.operational_evidence: SoA is current with the deployed system inventory; treatments reference live controls.
requirements.cadence: At least annual + on scope change
requirements.failure_modes: Excluding controls without written justification → major nonconformity; SoA stale; no deployment-model annotations.
requirements.external_safe_claim: Documented Statement of Applicability covering every Annex A control of ISO/IEC 42001.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; AIMS SoA mirrors ISMS SoA pattern.
PIMS extends 27001 SoA; AIMS adds the AI-specific Annex A.
AI Act Art. 9 risk-management system implementation is the SoA equivalent for high-risk AI.

requirements.title: AI system impact assessment
requirements.requirement: Maintain a documented process for assessing AI system impacts on individuals, groups and society — deepened by ISO/IEC 42005:2025.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor expects 42005-aligned methodology covering 10 elements (process documentation, integration with mgmt system, timing/scope, roles, thresholds, conduct, harms/benefits incl. foreseeable misuse, measures, recording, approval, monitoring/review).
requirements.pb_interpretation: PB AISIA Methodology aligned to ISO/IEC 42005:2025. Annex-E-style template applied per AI system. Foreseeable-misuse coverage (42005 6.8.3) is mandatory in every AISIA, not a best-practice add-on. Sector-specific AISIA addenda for healthcare safety, finance fairness, legal privilege.
requirements.companion_note: Use ISO/IEC 42005 methodology, harm/benefit taxonomy (Annex C) and template (Annex E).
requirements.operational_evidence: Each in-scope AI system has a current AISIA with foreseeable-misuse section completed and approved.
requirements.cadence: Pre-deployment + on material change + scheduled review
requirements.failure_modes: Hand-wave methodology; one-size-fits-all template; foreseeable-misuse section omitted; no approval workflow.
requirements.external_safe_claim: AI System Impact Assessment methodology aligned with ISO/IEC 42005:2025, including foreseeable-misuse coverage.

crosswalk.note: AISIA conceptually parallel to risk assessment; ISMS does not have a direct equivalent.
PIMS DPIA is structurally adjacent; AISIA template should integrate DPIA where AI processes PII.
AI Act Art. 9 risk mgmt + Art. 27 FRIA align with AISIA — single integrated template recommended.
AISIA covers POPIA s 71 automated decision-making analysis; integrated AISIA + DPIA template recommended.
AISIA security treatment maps to POPIA s 19 safeguards.
NIST AI RMF MAP 5.1 likelihood and magnitude of impacts — AISIA equivalent.

requirements.title: AI objectives and planning to achieve them
requirements.requirement: Establish AI objectives at relevant functions, levels and processes — measurable, time-bound, communicated, monitored and updated.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor checks objectives are SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) and not aspirational platitudes.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Aspirational statements ("we will be ethical") fail. Objectives are measurable: e.g., "100% of AI systems have current AISIA before deployment", "<2% drift threshold triggers re-eval", "Bias audit per release within sector criteria". Communicated to relevant functions; monitored in KPI dashboard.
requirements.operational_evidence: Objectives traced through risk register, AISIA, monitoring; reviewed at management review.
requirements.failure_modes: Aspirational statements that cannot be measured; no KPI link; no time-boxing.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox sets and tracks measurable AI objectives at relevant functions.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned.
HLS-aligned.

requirements.title: Planning of changes
requirements.requirement: AIMS changes are performed in a planned manner.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor checks change-control procedure explicitly covers AI-specific changes (model swaps, RAG corpus changes, prompt-template changes, deployment-environment changes).
requirements.pb_interpretation: Change-control procedure must explicitly enumerate AI-specific change types — model retrains, RAG corpus updates, prompt-template changes, hyperparameter changes. Silent retrains are a known failure pattern. Each change triggers re-AISIA where material.
requirements.operational_evidence: Recent changes traceable through CR records; re-AISIA executed where triggered.
requirements.failure_modes: Silent retrains; RAG corpus changes outside CR; prompt-template changes treated as config.
requirements.external_safe_claim: AI changes — including model swaps, RAG and prompt-template updates — go through documented change control with re-AISIA where material.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; AI-specific change types are the AIMS extension.
HLS-aligned.

requirements.title: Resources
requirements.requirement: Determine and provide the resources needed for the AIMS — establishment, implementation, maintenance and continual improvement.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor checks resources (people, tooling, infrastructure, budget) are documented and traceable to AIMS activities.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Resource catalogue covers AI Gov function staffing, AISIA tooling, evaluation infrastructure, audit-readiness budget. Computing resources for AI workloads are the most distinctive resource requirement (cross-link to A.4.5).
requirements.operational_evidence: Resources allocated traceable through procurement and timesheet records.
requirements.failure_modes: AIMS gets goodwill but no budget; AI Gov is a part-time role for an already-loaded engineer.
requirements.external_safe_claim: Resources for the AIMS are determined annually and traceable through PB's planning cycle.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned.
HLS-aligned.

requirements.title: Competence
requirements.requirement: Determine necessary competence for persons whose work affects AIMS performance; ensure they are competent on the basis of education, training or experience; retain documented information as evidence.
requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor samples randomly; documents that exist but no one can find or has read fail this clause.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Competence framework covers: AI ethics, ML risk, AI-specific secure SDLC, data governance, model evaluation, jurisdictional law (POPIA, EU AI Act, GDPR, HIPAA-equivalent, FCA/SEC/JSE-relevant). Competence matrix per role; training plans tied to it; certifications recorded. EU AI Act Art. 4 (AI literacy) compliance hook.
requirements.operational_evidence: Random staff sampling shows current training status against role competence requirements.
requirements.failure_modes: Training plans exist but completion not tracked; competence matrix doesn't reflect actual job requirements; sales staff not in scope of AI training.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox maintains a documented AI competence framework and training programme covering all AIMS-relevant roles.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned.
HLS-aligned; PIMS adds privacy-specific competence overlay.
EU AI Act Art. 4 AI-literacy obligations are operationalised through the AIMS competence framework.
NIST AI RMF GOV 2.2 personnel and partners receive AI risk-management training.

requirements.title: Awareness

requirements.pb_interpretation: All staff including support engineers covered. Awareness comms reach customer-facing roles too so they don't accidentally overclaim AIMS coverage.

requirements.title: Communication

requirements.auditor_intent: Auditor checks communication procedures cover incident notification timelines (EU AI Act 15-day, POPIA 72-hour) and customer-facing change communications.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Comms procedure covers internal (AIMS performance reporting, incident escalation) and external (regulators, customers — EU AI Act Art. 73 serious-incident, POPIA s.22 breach, Art. 50 transparency).

requirements.title: Documented information

requirements.operational_evidence: Random doc lookup completes within sampling expectation; versions trace through change history.
requirements.failure_modes: Untracked SharePoint sprawl; missing retention; outdated documents in active use.

requirements.failure_modes: Dashboard exists but unmonitored; no thresholds; drift detection lagging.

requirements.title: Internal audit

requirements.pb_interpretation: Annual at minimum; more often during early maturity. Standardised inputs/outputs template; minutes distributed; decisions tracked.

requirements.failure_modes: Lunch meeting with no minutes; inputs partial; decisions not tracked.

requirements.failure_modes: Boilerplate not tailored to PB; principles unmemorable; exceptions handled informally without procedure.

requirements.title: AI system event logs
requirements.objective: Log AI system events — time, date, inputs (or hashes), outputs, anomalies — with defined retention.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Logs are CUSTOMER-OWNED by default. PrivateBox defines schema and retention guidance; only sees what telemetry contracts permit. Schema supports auditor reconstruction of events.
documented_evidence: AI Event-Logging Standard (schema + retention + integrity + customer-control)
requirements.operational_evidence: Logs in customer deployments meet the standard; retention held; integrity guarantees maintained.
requirements.failure_modes: Logs in customer environment without published spec; retention undefined; integrity unprotected; PB-side retention unbounded.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: Auditable AI event-logging standard defining schema, retention and integrity expectations, customer-controlled by default.

crosswalk.note: 27001 logging / monitoring / clock sync controls.
Record-keeping for high-risk AI under Art. 12.
AI event logs subject to POPIA s 14 retention rules; logs must not be retained longer than necessary.
Event logging supports POPIA s 19 security-safeguard verification and s 22 incident investigation.
NIST AI RMF MEASURE 2.4 production monitoring including event logs.

requirements.title: AI system deployment
requirements.objective: Document deployment plans and require pre-go-live verification — impact assessment, testing, oversight, ops docs all complete and signed.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Pre-deployment gate enforced by Change Board. Checklist: AISIA signed, testing signed, oversight in place, ops documentation complete. Verbal sign-off fails the gate.
requirements.operational_evidence: Recent deployments traced through Change Board records.
requirements.failure_modes: Verbal sign-off; checklist items skipped; deployment without current AISIA.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: Documented pre-deployment approval gate with signed verification of impact assessment, testing, oversight and operational documentation.

crosswalk.note: Separation of dev/test/production environments.
NIST AI RMF MANAGE 1.1 go / no-go decision.

requirements.title: Data for development and enhancement of AI systems
requirements.objective: Govern operational data — quality, provenance, security, transparency — used in AI development and enhancement.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Operational data governance frame. Cross-references A.4.3 (data resources). For PB: training/eval data is PB-owned; customer corpora are customer-owned but governed under PB's framework when PB advises on RAG setup.
requirements.operational_evidence: Sample dataset traces through governance framework end-to-end.
requirements.failure_modes: Provenance partial; quality criteria implicit; transparency on data sources missing.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox operates a documented data governance framework covering quality, provenance, security and transparency.

crosswalk.note: Data classification.
Data governance for high-risk AI.
Data governance frame applies POPIA processing-limitation conditions (ss 9-12) — minimisation, lawful basis, direct collection.
NIST AI RMF MAP 4.1 component risks including data.

requirements.title: Acquisition of data
requirements.objective: Document data acquisition — categories, quantities, sources, demographics, prior handling, rights (PII, copyright), labelling metadata, provenance.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Per-acquisition record. Particularly important for open-weight model training data (Common Crawl, LAION, etc. — copyright/PII hot-spots). Customer-corpus acquisitions documented when PB advises.
requirements.operational_evidence: Sample acquisition has full record; rights cleared before use.
requirements.failure_modes: Acquisition records skipped for "public" data; rights not cleared; demographics undocumented.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: Data acquisitions are documented with provenance, rights and demographic metadata.

crosswalk.note: Data governance / acquisition obligations.
Data acquisition documentation supports POPIA s 11 lawful-basis justification and s 12 direct-collection rules.

requirements.title: Quality of data for AI systems
requirements.objective: Define data quality criteria, measurement methods and remediation paths.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Quality criteria documented and measurable. Per-dataset quality reports; remediation procedures for sub-threshold data. Customer corpus advisories include quality gates.
documented_evidence: Data quality criteria per dataset class
requirements.operational_evidence: Sample dataset has current quality report; remediation actions trace.
requirements.failure_modes: Quality criteria implicit; thresholds undefined; remediation ad-hoc.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: Data quality criteria and remediation procedures are documented per dataset class.

crosswalk.note: PIMS data minimisation / quality overlap.
Data quality obligations for high-risk AI.
Data quality criteria operationalise POPIA s 16 information-quality condition.

requirements.title: Processing of personal information in AI systems
requirements.objective: Govern processing of personal information by AI systems — integration with POPIA / GDPR / UK GDPR / HIPAA-equivalent.
requirements.pb_interpretation: PB defaults to "no customer PII processing in PB environment" with technical evidence; processing remains in customer's environment. When PB telemetry or support access touches PII, it is exception-only with case-by-case authorisation.
requirements.operational_evidence: Sample telemetry confirms PII-free; sample support session confirms case-by-case authorisation for PII access.
requirements.cadence: Annual + per change to data flows
requirements.failure_modes: Telemetry leaks PII; standing support access; PII processing not flowed; DPIA missing for AI-with-PII.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: By default, PrivateBox does not process customer personal information in its environment; exceptions are case-by-case, customer-authorised and logged.

crosswalk.note: PIMS controllers + processors PII processing controls; full PIMS overlay applies.
Special categories of personal data under Art. 10(5).
PII processing in AI subject to POPIA processing-limitation conditions (ss 9-12).
PII processing in AI subject to POPIA s 19 security safeguards and s 20-21 operator confidentiality.
Special-PI handling in AI subject to POPIA ss 26-33 enhanced-control regime.
NIST AI RMF MEASURE 2.10 privacy risk examination.

requirements.title: System characteristics and documentation
requirements.objective: Document AI system characteristics — intended use, capabilities, limitations, conditions of non-use.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Customer-facing transparency pack including system cards, capabilities, limitations, conditions of non-use, change log. Sales-driven inflation is a typical risk; legal review before publication.
documented_evidence: Transparency / User Information Pack per release
requirements.operational_evidence: Recent releases have current transparency pack accessible to customers.
requirements.failure_modes: Sales-driven inflation; conditions of non-use absent; capability claims unbacked by V&V.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: Customer-facing transparency pack documenting capabilities, limitations and conditions of non-use per release.

crosswalk.note: Privacy and protection of personal information; transparency overlap.
Transparency and provision of information for high-risk AI under Art. 13.
System characteristics documentation feeds POPIA s 18 collection-notice obligations for AI deployments.
NIST AI RMF GOV 4.2 system / model card documentation.

requirements.title: External reporting / disclosure when users interact with AI
requirements.objective: Disclose when users interact with AI; align with EU AI Act Art. 50 transparency obligations.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Customer-facing UI/UX components disclose AI interaction. Transparency aligned with EU AI Act Art. 50 (deepfake / synthetic content / AI-system interaction).
requirements.operational_evidence: Customer-facing surfaces show AI-interaction disclosure.
requirements.failure_modes: Disclosure absent in some UI surfaces; deepfake/synthetic-content marking missing; legal review skipped.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox surfaces disclose AI-interaction in line with EU AI Act Art. 50 transparency obligations.

crosswalk.note: Transparency obligations for AI providers and deployers.

requirements.title: Communication of incidents
requirements.objective: Maintain incident-notification procedures with timelines aligned to applicable regulation.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Notification SOPs: EU AI Act Art. 73 (15-day serious-incident); POPIA s.22 breach notification where personal info is involved (72-hour standard pattern). Customer-facing incident notification template.
requirements.operational_evidence: Past incidents trace through notification timelines.
requirements.failure_modes: Late notification; SOP exists but not exercised; customer notification template absent.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: Documented incident-notification procedures aligned to EU AI Act Art. 73 and POPIA s.22 timelines.

crosswalk.note: Privacy notification overlap.
Serious-incident reporting obligations for high-risk AI (15 days).
Incident communication includes POPIA s 22 breach notification (operator notifies RP immediately; RP notifies Regulator + data subjects).
NIST AI RMF MANAGE 4.3 incident communication.

requirements.title: Information for interested parties
requirements.objective: Provide interested parties with information about limitations, known biases and edge cases.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Goes beyond capability claims — actively communicates limitations, known biases, edge cases. Procurement responses provide depth that sales pages don't.
requirements.operational_evidence: Procurement responses sampled show comprehensive limitation coverage.
requirements.failure_modes: Limitations omitted from public surfaces; known biases redacted; edge cases not surfaced.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: Documented limitations, known biases and edge cases are communicated to procurement and interested parties.

crosswalk.note: Transparency obligations include limitations disclosure.

requirements.title: Communication of changes
requirements.objective: Communicate material changes in AI systems to affected parties.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Customer change-notification pipeline. Material changes trigger notification with sufficient lead time for customer change-management.
requirements.operational_evidence: Recent material changes notified per SOP; customer ack records held.
requirements.failure_modes: Notification skipped for "minor" model swaps that materially change behaviour; lead time too short for customer change-management.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: Material changes to PrivateBox AI systems are communicated to customers under documented SOP with sufficient lead time.

crosswalk.note: Transparency includes change notification to deployers.

requirements.title: Processes for responsible use of AI systems
requirements.objective: Establish internal processes that guide responsible use of AI systems.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Internal Acceptable Use Policy for PrivateBox staff and contractors. Covers PB-internal use of AI tooling for development, support, customer engagement.
requirements.operational_evidence: Random staff sampling confirms knowledge of acceptable-use scope.
requirements.failure_modes: Acceptable use undefined for new tools; staff use AI for sensitive purposes outside policy.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox operates a documented internal acceptable-use policy for AI systems.

crosswalk.note: Acceptable use of information and assets.

requirements.title: Objectives for responsible use of AI systems
requirements.objective: Translate responsible-use principles into measurable objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Measurable objectives for responsible internal AI use — e.g., 100% of customer-data interactions via approved tooling; bias reports reviewed before sales deployment.
requirements.operational_evidence: Objectives traced to KPIs and reviewed at management review.
requirements.failure_modes: Objectives platitudinous; no KPI link.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: Responsible-use objectives are measurable and tracked through management review.

crosswalk.note: Acceptable use objectives.

requirements.title: Intended use of the AI system
requirements.objective: Operational safeguards prevent out-of-scope use; user training and guidance support intended use.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Critical for legal/finance/healthcare to scope what PrivateBox is NOT used for (e.g., not the sole basis for clinical, credit, or legal-advice decisions). Customer-facing intended-use register with prohibitions.
requirements.operational_evidence: Customer deployment artefacts show intended-use scope; staff training current.
requirements.failure_modes: Intended-use undefined; customer deployments drift outside scope without flag; sector-specific prohibitions absent.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: Documented intended and prohibited uses per AI system, with sector-specific guidance for high-stakes domains.

crosswalk.note: Acceptable use + asset return cover use scope.
Deployer obligations under Art. 26 + transparency under Art. 50.
Intended-use enforcement supports POPIA s 11 lawful-purpose limitation.
Intended-use scope flags AI workflows that may trigger POPIA s 71 substantial-effect analysis.
NIST AI RMF MAP 3.3 targeted application scope.

requirements.title: Allocating responsibilities (suppliers and partners)
requirements.objective: Allocate AI-related responsibilities across the supply chain — model providers, partners, upstream data sources.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Cannot be excluded from SoA. Due diligence on model providers (open-weight: Meta, Mistral, etc.; proprietary: Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.). Contractual AI requirements; ongoing oversight; model-card review. "Pulled-from-Hugging-Face" model adoption without DD is a known finding pattern.
requirements.operational_evidence: Sample model approval traces from DD through approval to deployment.
requirements.failure_modes: Open-weight models adopted without DD; supplier review absent; contractual AI clauses missing from MSAs; partner DC supplier obligations undefined.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: Documented supplier and model-vendor due-diligence programme covering open-weight and proprietary models.

crosswalk.note: Supplier relationships, ICT supply chain, monitoring of supplier services.
Responsibilities along the AI value chain under Art. 25.
Supplier due diligence covers POPIA s 20-21 operator obligations; sub-processor authorisation under s 21.
NIST AI RMF GOV 6.1 third-party AI / data / IP / supply-chain risk.
NIST 218A third-party model governance.

requirements.title: Suppliers
requirements.objective: Govern the supplier-side of AI relationships — DD, contractual obligations, ongoing oversight.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Supplier programme covering model providers, hosting partners, RAG / data acquisition vendors, open-source library providers (with appropriate scope per supplier risk).
requirements.operational_evidence: Supplier register sampled traces to current contracts and review records.
requirements.failure_modes: Open-source library providers omitted from supplier programme; reviews skipped.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: Supplier programme covers AI-relevant vendors with appropriate due-diligence depth.

crosswalk.note: Supplier relationships and contractual InfoSec.

requirements.title: Customers
requirements.objective: Govern customer-side AI relationships — intended use, prohibited use, oversight expectations, change notifications, EULA / MSA AI clauses.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Customer-facing AI clauses in MSA / EULA. Customer Shared-Responsibility Matrix (productised version of source §7). Customer enablement pack. Sales staff trained on red-flag claim list.
requirements.operational_evidence: Recent customer onboarding sampled shows AI clauses signed, matrix delivered, enablement complete.
requirements.cadence: Per customer onboarding + per material change
requirements.failure_modes: AI clauses absent from MSA; shared-responsibility matrix verbal; customer enablement skipped.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: Documented customer-facing AI obligations covering intended use, prohibited use, oversight expectations and change notifications.

crosswalk.note: Supplier relationships pattern applied to customer side.
Value-chain responsibilities + deployer obligations.
Customer-facing AI obligations include accountability flow-down for customer's POPIA RP duties.
Customer relationship terms include POPIA s 22 mutual-notification clause.

iso-42001-cl-4.1.title: Understanding the organisation and its context
iso-42001-cl-4.1.requirement: Determine internal and external issues relevant to the AIMS purpose and ability to achieve intended outcomes. Determine PrivateBox's role in the AI ecosystem (provider, producer, customer, partner — for PB: provider + producer).
iso-42001-cl-4.1.auditor_intent: Auditor verifies the AIMS scope traces from documented internal/external context, including the PB-as-provider-and-producer role declaration.
iso-42001-cl-4.1.pb_interpretation: PrivateBox must explicitly position itself as an AI system provider and component producer to confidential firms; not a deployer of AI on behalf of those firms. Customer environment is external context; PB R&D / support environment is internal context. Both must be captured. Refresh annually and on material change (new jurisdiction, new sector, new model class, regulatory change).
iso-42001-cl-4.1.operational_evidence: Dated, version-controlled context register signed off by top management; traceable to AIMS scope and risk criteria.
iso-42001-cl-4.1.failure_modes: Generic boilerplate that doesn't reflect PB; not refreshed; ignores POPIA/EU AI Act/King V; doesn't name PB's AI role; AIMS scope can't be derived from context.
iso-42001-cl-4.1.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox's AIMS is contextualised for confidential-firm AI in legal, finance and healthcare across ZA, EU, US and UK.
iso-42001-cl-4.1.scope_note: Customer environment is external context input; PB R&D and support environments are internal.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned. AIMS context extends ISMS context with AI-role declaration.
HLS-aligned. AIMS context complements PIMS context with AI scope.
NIST AI RMF GOV 1.1 legal / regulatory understanding aligns with AIMS context.

iso-42001-cl-4.2.title: Understanding the needs and expectations of interested parties
iso-42001-cl-4.2.requirement: Identify interested parties relevant to the AIMS, their relevant requirements, and which become AIMS requirements.
iso-42001-cl-4.2.auditor_intent: Auditor verifies stakeholder requirements are captured and traceable to AIMS controls.
iso-42001-cl-4.2.pb_interpretation: Interested parties include: customers (legal/finance/healthcare CISOs, DPOs, GCs), data subjects (clients of customers), regulators (Information Regulator ZA, EU AI Office, UK ICO, US sectoral), employees, third-party model providers, support partners, investors. Maintain a stakeholder register with documented expectations and requirement-traceability matrix.
iso-42001-cl-4.2.operational_evidence: Evidence that interested-party requirements map to specific Annex A controls and AIMS objectives.
iso-42001-cl-4.2.failure_modes: Customer-only view; missing data subjects and regulators; no traceability to controls; static register that doesn't update on regulatory change.
iso-42001-cl-4.2.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox's AIMS systematically captures and addresses the requirements of customers, data subjects, regulators and supply-chain partners.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned. AIMS adds AI-specific stakeholders (regulators, data subjects in AI context).
HLS-aligned; PIMS interested-party identification extends to AI-context data subjects.
NIST AI RMF GOV 5.1 external feedback aligns with interested-parties identification.

iso-42001-cl-4.3.title: Determining the scope of the AI management system
iso-42001-cl-4.3.requirement: Maintain a documented AIMS scope statement covering boundaries, products, services, locations and AI roles.
iso-42001-cl-4.3.auditor_intent: Auditor checks scope is precise, coherent and defensible — narrow enough to be defensible, broad enough to be credible.
iso-42001-cl-4.3.pb_interpretation: See target wording in pb_application.scope_decisions.on_prem.rationale. Scope is product-and-operations bounded; explicitly excludes customer-operated deployments after handover, customer fine-tuning inside their environment, and customer-provided data — these remain under the customer's own AIMS. Scope statement is 1 page, top-management-signed, referenced from policy. Scope expansion to AI Factory pending oq-partner-dc-selection.
iso-42001-cl-4.3.operational_evidence: Signed scope statement traceable to context register and to AIMS objectives.
iso-42001-cl-4.3.failure_modes: Scope drift in marketing; scope that seems to extend over customer environments (creates legal exposure); scope mismatched with what auditor sees in operations.
iso-42001-cl-4.3.external_safe_claim: Our AIMS scope covers the design, build and support of PrivateBox; customer-operated deployments are governed by the customer's own AIMS, which we enable with documentation.
iso-42001-cl-4.3.scope_note: Stage-1 scope statement is the single most-read document in the audit.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; AIMS scope is parallel to ISMS scope but distinct in coverage.
HLS-aligned; AIMS scope distinct from PIMS scope.

iso-42001-cl-4.4.title: AI management system
iso-42001-cl-4.4.requirement: Establish, implement, maintain and continually improve the AIMS.
iso-42001-cl-4.4.auditor_intent: Auditor expects to see a coherent AIMS architecture document showing how clauses 4–10 and Annex A connect, and how AIMS integrates with ISMS / PIMS.
iso-42001-cl-4.4.pb_interpretation: PDCA cycle integrated with PB's ISO 27001 ISMS and ISO 27701 PIMS. Single management system, three labels. AIMS Manual / Overview describes process architecture and integration points.
iso-42001-cl-4.4.operational_evidence: Auditor walks the AIMS architecture; cross-references should resolve.
iso-42001-cl-4.4.cadence: Annual review of AIMS architecture document
iso-42001-cl-4.4.failure_modes: Three parallel siloed management systems instead of one integrated; AIMS exists on paper only.
iso-42001-cl-4.4.external_safe_claim: AIMS architecture aligned to ISO/IEC 42001, integrated with our existing ISMS and PIMS.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; PB runs one integrated management system surface.
HLS-aligned.

iso-42001-cl-5.1.title: Leadership and commitment
iso-42001-cl-5.1.requirement: Top management demonstrates leadership and commitment to the AIMS — measurable, not delegable.
iso-42001-cl-5.1.auditor_intent: Auditor checks AIMS sponsorship is at executive level, with measurable commitment and resource allocation; AIMS sponsored only at IT manager level is a frequent Schellman finding.
iso-42001-cl-5.1.pb_interpretation: CEO and Board own AI accountability. Documented in board / Exco minutes; CEO sign-off on AI Policy and AIMS scope; visible budget allocation. AI Governance Committee has executive representation. King V Principle 1 (Ethical leadership) and POPIA accountability principle apply.
documented_evidence: CEO sign-off on AI Policy and AIMS Scope Statement
Budget allocation evidence (AI Gov function, audit)
AI Governance Committee Terms of Reference
iso-42001-cl-5.1.operational_evidence: Top management can describe AI Policy and AIMS in interview; resource decisions trace to AIMS objectives.
iso-42001-cl-5.1.failure_modes: AIMS sponsored only at IT-manager level; no executive interview readiness; no budget trace.
iso-42001-cl-5.1.external_safe_claim: AIMS commitment is documented at top-management level with measurable resourcing.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; same leadership pattern across ISMS / PIMS / AIMS.
HLS-aligned.
Deployer obligations under Art. 26 require designated humans and oversight at organisational level.
POPIA accountability principle (s 8) requires top-management oversight of personal-information processing; AIMS leadership scope covers PB-as-RP duties.
NIST AI RMF GOV 2.3 executive responsibility aligns with AIMS leadership.

iso-42001-cl-5.2.title: AI Policy
iso-42001-cl-5.2.requirement: Top management establishes, communicates and maintains an AI Policy framed by business strategy, ethics, risk environment and legal requirements, including a process for handling exceptions.
iso-42001-cl-5.2.auditor_intent: Auditor tests whether the AI Policy is documented, approved, communicated, available to interested parties, and understood by random employees on interview.
iso-42001-cl-5.2.pb_interpretation: Linked to A.2.2. PrivateBox AI Policy v1.x with principles, scope, prohibited uses, exceptions process, owner and version. Disseminated; staff can describe core principles. Reviewed annually and on trigger (new framework, regulatory change, material incident).
iso-42001-cl-5.2.operational_evidence: Random employee interviewed by auditor can describe AI Policy core principles.
iso-42001-cl-5.2.failure_modes: Boilerplate policy not tailored to PB; sales team can't describe it; exceptions handled informally.
iso-42001-cl-5.2.external_safe_claim: Our AI Policy, approved by top management, governs every PrivateBox AI activity.

crosswalk.note: AI Policy parallels InfoSec Policy; cross-policy alignment required.
AI Policy aligns with PIMS Privacy Policy.
AI Policy operationalises POPIA accountability for AI-specific processing; cross-policy alignment with PB privacy notice required.
NIST AI RMF GOV 1.2 trustworthy-AI characteristics integrated into policies.

iso-42001-cl-5.3.title: Roles, responsibilities and authorities
iso-42001-cl-5.3.requirement: Documented assignment of roles, responsibilities and authorities for AIMS conformance and reporting.
iso-42001-cl-5.3.auditor_intent: Auditor checks named owners exist for each AIMS function and that authority lines support reporting on AIMS performance.
iso-42001-cl-5.3.pb_interpretation: Linked to A.3.2. RACI plus AI Governance Charter naming AI Governance Lead, Model Risk Lead, AI Security Lead, Data/RAG Steward, Customer Support AI Liaison. Cross-functional AI Governance Committee meets on fixed cadence. Same person may not own development AND oversight (Annex A.3.2 failure pattern).
iso-42001-cl-5.3.operational_evidence: Each AI role has a named individual who can speak to their responsibility in interview.
iso-42001-cl-5.3.failure_modes: Same person owns development and oversight; AI Governance Lead role exists but has no real authority.
iso-42001-cl-5.3.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox operates a documented cross-functional AI governance structure with named owners and committee oversight.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; AI roles overlay InfoSec roles.
HLS-aligned; Information Officer role required by POPIA also overlaps.
Deployer Art. 26.2 designated-humans obligation parallels AIMS role assignment.
NIST AI RMF GOV 2.1 roles and responsibilities documented.

iso-42001-cl-6.1.1.title: Actions to address risks and opportunities
iso-42001-cl-6.1.1.requirement: Determine risks and opportunities for the AIMS to achieve intended outcomes, prevent or reduce undesired effects, and continually improve.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.1.auditor_intent: Auditor checks risk-and-opportunity determination is documented and feeds into the AI risk methodology and AIMS objectives.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.1.pb_interpretation: Risks-and-opportunities discussion is the bridge between context (4.1), interested parties (4.2) and AI objectives (6.2). Documented as input to AIMS planning.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.1.operational_evidence: Planning record is referenced in management review and informs the AI risk register.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.1.failure_modes: Generic risk-only treatment; no opportunity identification; not traceable to objectives.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.1.external_safe_claim: AIMS planning addresses both AI risks and opportunities under documented criteria.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; AIMS planning parallels ISMS planning.
HLS-aligned (27701 collapses 6.1.1-6.1.3 into 6.1).

iso-42001-cl-6.1.2.title: AI risk assessment
iso-42001-cl-6.1.2.requirement: Maintain a documented AI risk assessment methodology with criteria, acceptance, repeatability, informed by Annex C objectives / risk sources and ISO/IEC 23894.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.2.auditor_intent: Auditor verifies the methodology is AI-specific (not a renamed InfoSec methodology) and is actually used to populate an AI risk register per system in scope.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.2.pb_interpretation: Methodology must address AI-specific risk sources (bias, fairness, robustness, foreseeable misuse, automation level, ML-specific risks) per Annex C. Per-system AI risk register references the methodology. Reuse of 27001 methodology under a new label is a known failure pattern.
documented_evidence: AI Risk Register (per system in scope)
iso-42001-cl-6.1.2.operational_evidence: Risk register entries trace to methodology criteria; treatments link to SoA controls.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.2.cadence: Quarterly register review; annual methodology review
iso-42001-cl-6.1.2.failure_modes: Generic InfoSec methodology renamed; no Annex C coverage; static register.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.2.external_safe_claim: AI-specific risk methodology consistent with ISO/IEC 42001 and ISO/IEC 23894, covering Annex C objectives and risk sources.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; AI risk methodology distinct from but integrated with InfoSec risk methodology.
AIMS risk integrates with PIMS risk for AI-with-PII contexts.
EU AI Act Art. 9 risk-management system for high-risk AI is satisfied via AIMS risk methodology.
AI risk methodology must address POPIA s 19 security safeguards as a core risk class for any AI handling personal information.
NIST AI RMF MAP 1.6 system requirements account for socio-technical risk.
NIST AI RMF MEASURE 1.1 risk metric selection.

iso-42001-cl-6.1.3.title: AI risk treatment
iso-42001-cl-6.1.3.requirement: Document an AI risk treatment plan with control selection, and produce a Statement of Applicability comparing selected controls to Annex A. Note 3 permits exclusions with documented justification.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.3.auditor_intent: Auditor reads the SoA first; expects every Annex A control with applicability decision, owner, evidence pointer, justification.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.3.pb_interpretation: SoA is THE bridge document. One row per Annex A control: applicability (Y / Shared / Conditional / N/A), implementation status, control owner, evidence pointer, justification. Add deployment-model column (PB / SH / CL per business model). Empty justifications are a known Schellman finding pattern.
documented_evidence: Statement of Applicability (every Annex A control)
iso-42001-cl-6.1.3.operational_evidence: SoA is current with the deployed system inventory; treatments reference live controls.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.3.cadence: At least annual + on scope change
iso-42001-cl-6.1.3.failure_modes: Excluding controls without written justification → major nonconformity; SoA stale; no deployment-model annotations.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.3.external_safe_claim: Documented Statement of Applicability covering every Annex A control of ISO/IEC 42001.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; AIMS SoA mirrors ISMS SoA pattern.
PIMS extends 27001 SoA; AIMS adds the AI-specific Annex A.
AI Act Art. 9 risk-management system implementation is the SoA equivalent for high-risk AI.

iso-42001-cl-6.1.4.title: AI system impact assessment
iso-42001-cl-6.1.4.requirement: Maintain a documented process for assessing AI system impacts on individuals, groups and society — deepened by ISO/IEC 42005:2025.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.4.auditor_intent: Auditor expects 42005-aligned methodology covering 10 elements (process documentation, integration with mgmt system, timing/scope, roles, thresholds, conduct, harms/benefits incl. foreseeable misuse, measures, recording, approval, monitoring/review).
iso-42001-cl-6.1.4.pb_interpretation: PB AISIA Methodology aligned to ISO/IEC 42005:2025. Annex-E-style template applied per AI system. Foreseeable-misuse coverage (42005 6.8.3) is mandatory in every AISIA, not a best-practice add-on. Sector-specific AISIA addenda for healthcare safety, finance fairness, legal privilege.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.4.companion_note: Use ISO/IEC 42005 methodology, harm/benefit taxonomy (Annex C) and template (Annex E).
iso-42001-cl-6.1.4.operational_evidence: Each in-scope AI system has a current AISIA with foreseeable-misuse section completed and approved.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.4.cadence: Pre-deployment + on material change + scheduled review
iso-42001-cl-6.1.4.failure_modes: Hand-wave methodology; one-size-fits-all template; foreseeable-misuse section omitted; no approval workflow.
iso-42001-cl-6.1.4.external_safe_claim: AI System Impact Assessment methodology aligned with ISO/IEC 42005:2025, including foreseeable-misuse coverage.

crosswalk.note: AISIA conceptually parallel to risk assessment; ISMS does not have a direct equivalent.
PIMS DPIA is structurally adjacent; AISIA template should integrate DPIA where AI processes PII.
AI Act Art. 9 risk mgmt + Art. 27 FRIA align with AISIA — single integrated template recommended.
AISIA covers POPIA s 71 automated decision-making analysis; integrated AISIA + DPIA template recommended.
AISIA security treatment maps to POPIA s 19 safeguards.
NIST AI RMF MAP 5.1 likelihood and magnitude of impacts — AISIA equivalent.

iso-42001-cl-6.2.title: AI objectives and planning to achieve them
iso-42001-cl-6.2.requirement: Establish AI objectives at relevant functions, levels and processes — measurable, time-bound, communicated, monitored and updated.
iso-42001-cl-6.2.auditor_intent: Auditor checks objectives are SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) and not aspirational platitudes.
iso-42001-cl-6.2.pb_interpretation: Aspirational statements ("we will be ethical") fail. Objectives are measurable: e.g., "100% of AI systems have current AISIA before deployment", "<2% drift threshold triggers re-eval", "Bias audit per release within sector criteria". Communicated to relevant functions; monitored in KPI dashboard.
iso-42001-cl-6.2.operational_evidence: Objectives traced through risk register, AISIA, monitoring; reviewed at management review.
iso-42001-cl-6.2.failure_modes: Aspirational statements that cannot be measured; no KPI link; no time-boxing.
iso-42001-cl-6.2.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox sets and tracks measurable AI objectives at relevant functions.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned.
HLS-aligned.

iso-42001-cl-6.3.title: Planning of changes
iso-42001-cl-6.3.requirement: AIMS changes are performed in a planned manner.
iso-42001-cl-6.3.auditor_intent: Auditor checks change-control procedure explicitly covers AI-specific changes (model swaps, RAG corpus changes, prompt-template changes, deployment-environment changes).
iso-42001-cl-6.3.pb_interpretation: Change-control procedure must explicitly enumerate AI-specific change types — model retrains, RAG corpus updates, prompt-template changes, hyperparameter changes. Silent retrains are a known failure pattern. Each change triggers re-AISIA where material.
iso-42001-cl-6.3.operational_evidence: Recent changes traceable through CR records; re-AISIA executed where triggered.
iso-42001-cl-6.3.failure_modes: Silent retrains; RAG corpus changes outside CR; prompt-template changes treated as config.
iso-42001-cl-6.3.external_safe_claim: AI changes — including model swaps, RAG and prompt-template updates — go through documented change control with re-AISIA where material.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned; AI-specific change types are the AIMS extension.
HLS-aligned.

iso-42001-cl-7.1.title: Resources
iso-42001-cl-7.1.requirement: Determine and provide the resources needed for the AIMS — establishment, implementation, maintenance and continual improvement.
iso-42001-cl-7.1.auditor_intent: Auditor checks resources (people, tooling, infrastructure, budget) are documented and traceable to AIMS activities.
iso-42001-cl-7.1.pb_interpretation: Resource catalogue covers AI Gov function staffing, AISIA tooling, evaluation infrastructure, audit-readiness budget. Computing resources for AI workloads are the most distinctive resource requirement (cross-link to A.4.5).
iso-42001-cl-7.1.operational_evidence: Resources allocated traceable through procurement and timesheet records.
iso-42001-cl-7.1.failure_modes: AIMS gets goodwill but no budget; AI Gov is a part-time role for an already-loaded engineer.
iso-42001-cl-7.1.external_safe_claim: Resources for the AIMS are determined annually and traceable through PB's planning cycle.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned.
HLS-aligned.

iso-42001-cl-7.2.title: Competence
iso-42001-cl-7.2.requirement: Determine necessary competence for persons whose work affects AIMS performance; ensure they are competent on the basis of education, training or experience; retain documented information as evidence.
iso-42001-cl-7.2.auditor_intent: Auditor samples randomly; documents that exist but no one can find or has read fail this clause.
iso-42001-cl-7.2.pb_interpretation: Competence framework covers: AI ethics, ML risk, AI-specific secure SDLC, data governance, model evaluation, jurisdictional law (POPIA, EU AI Act, GDPR, HIPAA-equivalent, FCA/SEC/JSE-relevant). Competence matrix per role; training plans tied to it; certifications recorded. EU AI Act Art. 4 (AI literacy) compliance hook.
iso-42001-cl-7.2.operational_evidence: Random staff sampling shows current training status against role competence requirements.
iso-42001-cl-7.2.failure_modes: Training plans exist but completion not tracked; competence matrix doesn't reflect actual job requirements; sales staff not in scope of AI training.
iso-42001-cl-7.2.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox maintains a documented AI competence framework and training programme covering all AIMS-relevant roles.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned.
HLS-aligned; PIMS adds privacy-specific competence overlay.
EU AI Act Art. 4 AI-literacy obligations are operationalised through the AIMS competence framework.
NIST AI RMF GOV 2.2 personnel and partners receive AI risk-management training.

iso-42001-cl-7.3.title: Awareness
iso-42001-cl-7.3.requirement: Persons doing work under PB's control are aware of the AI Policy, their contribution to AIMS effectiveness, and the implications of non-conformance.
iso-42001-cl-7.3.auditor_intent: Auditor interviews random staff (including support engineers); they should describe the AI Policy, their role, and consequences of non-conformance.
iso-42001-cl-7.3.pb_interpretation: All staff including support engineers covered. Awareness comms reach customer-facing roles too so they don't accidentally overclaim AIMS coverage.
iso-42001-cl-7.3.operational_evidence: Random employee interview confirms awareness; sales/marketing can describe red-flag list.
iso-42001-cl-7.3.failure_modes: Sales/Marketing not in awareness scope; awareness exists for engineers only.
iso-42001-cl-7.3.external_safe_claim: All PrivateBox staff complete AI Policy awareness training; sales and customer-facing roles cover red-flag claim language.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned.
HLS-aligned.
AI literacy under Art. 4 reaches the awareness clause.

iso-42001-cl-7.4.title: Communication
iso-42001-cl-7.4.requirement: Determine internal and external communications relevant to the AIMS — what, when, with whom, how and who communicates.
iso-42001-cl-7.4.auditor_intent: Auditor checks communication procedures cover incident notification timelines (EU AI Act 15-day, POPIA 72-hour) and customer-facing change communications.
iso-42001-cl-7.4.pb_interpretation: Comms procedure covers internal (AIMS performance reporting, incident escalation) and external (regulators, customers — EU AI Act Art. 73 serious-incident, POPIA s.22 breach, Art. 50 transparency).
documented_evidence: Incident notification SOPs (EU AI Act Art. 73, POPIA s.22)
iso-42001-cl-7.4.operational_evidence: Past incidents trace through comms records; cadence held.
iso-42001-cl-7.4.failure_modes: No comms matrix; incident timelines not pre-baked into SOPs.
iso-42001-cl-7.4.external_safe_claim: AIMS communications procedures cover internal performance reporting and external regulatory / customer obligations including 15-day serious-incident notifications.

crosswalk.note: HLS-aligned.
HLS-aligned; PIMS communication includes data-subject notification.
Communication procedure includes POPIA s 22 breach notification timing.

iso-42001-cl-7.5.title: Documented information
iso-42001-cl-7.5.requirement: Maintain documented information required by the standard and the AIMS — version-controlled, dated, owner-attributed, retention-defined.
iso-42001-cl-7.5.auditor_intent: Auditor samples; documents that exist but cannot be located or are out of date fail this clause.
