scenarios.name: Pure on-prem deployment, no remote support, no telemetry
scenarios.description: Customer runs PrivateBox entirely within its own infrastructure with no PB telemetry, support access or remote operations. PB's POPIA exposure is limited to its own corporate records (no customer personal information processed by PB).

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Customer carries full RP accountability; PB documents non-processing posture.
PB IO governs its own records; customer IO governs deployment.

scenarios.notes: Lowest PB POPIA exposure; documentation rigour the primary control.

scenarios.name: On-prem with signed offline / pull-only updates
scenarios.description: PB delivers updates with cryptographic signing and SBOM; customer pulls on schedule. PB does not receive customer personal information through the update channel.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Update integrity is a security safeguard control PB owns.
Update repository hosting location may be cross-border; disclosed in transfer register.

scenarios.notes: Update channel itself rarely processes PI but supply chain integrity is in scope.

scenarios.name: On-prem with remote admin
scenarios.description: PB engineers can perform remote administration of customer environment. Engineering personnel can affect means of processing and may see personal information during admin tasks.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Just-in-time access; session logging; MFA; operator confidentiality.
IO involvement on incidents.

scenarios.notes: Operator scenario; DPA / operator addendum mandatory.

scenarios.name: On-prem with operational telemetry
scenarios.description: Telemetry pipeline returns operational metadata to PB. May contain identifiers, user IDs, device data or usage traces depending on design. PB may be operator OR independent responsible party for the telemetry stream.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Minimisation — telemetry must collect only what's needed.
Telemetry must be in privacy notice / data dictionary.
Telemetry endpoint location is cross-border concern.

scenarios.notes: Hidden monitoring becomes notice + transfer problem; opt-in + data dictionary controls.

scenarios.name: Support-access troubleshooting using live customer prompts or documents
scenarios.description: PB engineers gain time-bounded access to customer environment for troubleshooting. May see live prompts, documents, or customer data.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: ss 20-21 operator confidentiality + s 22 immediate notification on incident.
Local redaction; support-bundle expiry; minimisation.

scenarios.notes: Strongest operator scenario; per-session authorisation + recording mandatory.

scenarios.name: Managed RAG / document-ingestion assistance
scenarios.description: PB advises customer on RAG corpus structure, ingestion pipeline. May stage / inspect / transform documents during implementation. Customer owns content rights and PI decisions.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Staging restrictions; secure deletion of staging copies.
No reuse of staged documents for analytics or model training.

scenarios.notes: SOW + DPA + secure deletion confirmation. "Implementation services" can become ungoverned processing.

scenarios.name: Hosted update repository / model package distribution
scenarios.description: PB serves software supply chain — signed updates, model packages, SBOMs, vulnerability disclosure. Repository may collect subscriber / admin metadata.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Repository metadata minimisation.
Repository security baseline; abuse monitoring.

scenarios.notes: Vendor-operated; PB is RP for its own repository operations.

scenarios.name: Vendor-run backup support involving customer data copy
scenarios.description: PB provides backup infrastructure that stores customer personal information.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Backup encryption; customer-managed keys; restore authority.
Backup location may be cross-border; s 72 applies.
Backup retention bound by customer's primary retention policy.

scenarios.notes: Strong DPA + sub-processor terms; cross-border-aware deployment.

scenarios.name: Offshore support engineer accesses incident data
scenarios.description: Foreign engineer accesses customer environment / incident data during support. Transfer of personal information across jurisdiction.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: s 72 transfer rules; adequacy / binding agreement / consent required.
Operator + cross-border-aware support.
Foreign transfer of special / children PI may need prior authorisation.

scenarios.notes: "Temporary" support is still a transfer; regional support routing is the control.

scenarios.name: PrivateBox website, CRM, bids, billing, staff data (PB's own corporate processing)
scenarios.description: PrivateBox's own corporate processing of personal information — customer prospects, account contacts, billing data, employees, marketing.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: PB-as-RP accountability for own processing.
PB privacy notice + PAIA manual.
PB B2B marketing consent + objection workflow.
PB IO governs this processing.

scenarios.notes: Easy to overlook while focused on product; PB is fully accountable as RP.

open_questions.question: Is PrivateBox's Information Officer designated and registered with the Information Regulator?
candidate_answers: Designated and registered (current state should be verified)
open_questions.decision_owner: Top Management + AI Governance Lead (potentially same as IO)
open_questions.notes: Section 55 requires designation; Section 56 + Regulation 4 set out the duties. Registration with the Information Regulator is a public filing — buyers verify it. Active operating cadence (training, awareness, framework review) is the additional bar.

open_questions.question: Does PrivateBox publish a current PAIA Manual and access-request forms?
open_questions.notes: PAIA Manual is hygiene-factor evidence buyers expect within 5 minutes of asking. Form-based request handling links to POPIA s 23-25 rights workflow.

open_questions.question: Which countries are approved for PrivateBox support engineer access to customer environments?
candidate_answers: SA + named approved countries with safeguards
Per-deal scoping with no default approved list
open_questions.notes: Section 72 transfer controls require adequacy / binding agreement / consent. Default geography decision must precede customer engagements; per-deal scoping creates audit complexity.

open_questions.question: What is PrivateBox's documented sub-operator / sub-processor chain and customer-notice approach?
candidate_answers: Maintain a public sub-processor list with email-on-change notifications
Maintain an NDA-gated sub-processor list with per-customer notification
Per-deal disclosure with no maintained list
open_questions.notes: Section 21 requires sub-operator authorisation by RP. Public sub-processor lists (Workday, Microsoft, Anthropic patterns) are procurement-friendly but require maintenance discipline.

open_questions.question: What is PrivateBox's POPIA-aligned posture for B2B direct marketing?
candidate_answers: Strict opt-in for all electronic marketing including B2B
Soft opt-in (existing customer exception) per s 69(3)
Per-channel decision (email vs SMS vs phone)
open_questions.notes: Section 69 has electronic-marketing rules with limited customer exception. B2B is in scope. Posture decision affects CRM workflow, opt-in records, suppression list maintenance.

open_questions.question: Does PrivateBox publish a documented "special data deployment profile" for healthcare, pharmacy, education customers?
candidate_answers: Yes — per-sector profile with restricted-control configurations
Refer to customer scoping engagement only
open_questions.notes: Sections 26-33 (special PI) + 34-35 (children). Healthcare, pharmacy and education are PB target sectors. Generic templates are a finding pattern in these deployments.

open_questions.question: Does PrivateBox align to or contribute to a sectoral Code of Conduct under POPIA Chapter 7?
candidate_answers: Track and align where one exists (e.g., financial services Code)
open_questions.notes: POPIA Chapter 7 (ss 60-68) provides for sectoral Codes of Conduct. Aligning to a Code can shape sector-specific obligations. Current SA Code landscape is sparse; tracking is sufficient for v1.

open_questions.question: Has PrivateBox screened its current customer use cases for prior-authorisation triggers?
open_questions.notes: Sections 57-58 trigger prior authorisation for specific high-risk processing (unique identifier linking, criminal-behaviour processing for third parties, credit reporting, certain foreign transfers). Pre-sales screening is the intervention.

requirements.title: Accountability
requirements.requirement: The responsible party must ensure that the conditions set out in Chapter 3, and all the measures that give effect to those conditions, are complied with at the time of determining the purpose and means of processing, and during the processing itself.
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor checks that privacy controls exist from design through operation, not only in policy documents — privacy-by-design rather than privacy-by-brochure.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Embed compliance into product governance. Privacy review gate in SDLC; release checklist; exception approval; risk register. Customer accountable for its purposes; PrivateBox accountable for product and service-design controls and for its own corporate processing as RP.
requirements.operational_evidence: Sample releases checked for privacy sign-off; risk register reviewed at management cadence.
requirements.failure_modes: Treating accountability as "customer problem"; policy with no approvals, no cadence, no owner.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox implements controls designed to support POPIA-aligned processing from deployment through operations.

requirements.title: Processing limitation
requirements.requirement: Processing must be lawful, in a reasonable manner that does not infringe the privacy of the data subject, adequate, relevant and not excessive (minimality). Processing must have a justification (consent, contract performance, legal obligation, legitimate interests, etc.). Personal information must be collected directly from the data subject unless an exception applies.
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor verifies collection is necessary, justified and minimised; logs / debug bundles / telemetry reflect minimisation; indirect collection has documented basis.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Default-minimise data captured by the product and vendor tooling. Disable unnecessary prompt retention by default; minimise telemetry fields; restrict support file collection; define lawful purposes for any vendor-side data received. Indirect-collection sources (connectors, imports, admin syncs) tagged and explicit.
requirements.operational_evidence: Default configurations show minimisation; sample telemetry payload confirms PII-free; sample support bundle confirms scoping.
requirements.failure_modes: "Debug everything" culture; undefined support collections; collecting "just in case" data; silent background imports.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox is designed to minimise vendor-side handling of customer personal information.

requirements.title: Purpose specification
requirements.requirement: Personal information must be collected for a specific, explicitly defined and lawful purpose. Records must not be retained longer than necessary for the purpose, subject to limited statutory exceptions. On expiry of retention, records must be destroyed or de-identified in a manner that prevents reconstruction.
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor checks every dataset, log, cache, backup and exported report has a defined purpose and retention rule, and that destruction is verified.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Define retention by data class; auto-deletion workflows; decommission wipe standard; backup retention logic; local cache expiry. Customer owns primary in-environment retention; PrivateBox owns vendor-side copies and guides customer retention defaults.
documented_evidence: Retention schedule per data class (logs, backups, support bundles, repositories)
requirements.operational_evidence: Sample expired data shows deletion proof; backup retention configuration matches schedule.
requirements.failure_modes: Forgotten support bundles; stale backups; persistent logs; backups outliving production.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox supports customer-defined retention and secure deletion processes for local deployments.

requirements.title: Further processing limitation
requirements.requirement: Further processing of personal information must be in accordance with, or compatible with, the purpose for which it was collected. Compatibility is assessed against the relationship between purposes, the nature of the information, the consequences of further processing, the manner of collection, and any contractual rights and obligations.
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor scrutinises whether vendor reuses customer data for analytics, model improvement, support training or demos without explicit lawful basis and customer agreement.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Ban unapproved secondary use. Contract clause forbidding model training on customer data without explicit agreement; separate support data from product improvement data; approval workflow for any reuse. "De-identified if possible" is not a blanket excuse.
documented_evidence: DPA / operator agreement with reuse-prohibition clause
Separation of support data from product improvement data
requirements.operational_evidence: Sample vendor-side dataset traces show no customer data beyond support case scope.
requirements.failure_modes: Using support data for product training; "operational analytics" becoming secondary use without review; treating "anonymised" as automatic permission.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox does not assume any right to repurpose customer data beyond agreed services.

requirements.title: Information quality
requirements.requirement: The responsible party must take reasonably practicable steps to ensure that personal information is complete, accurate, not misleading and updated where necessary, having regard to the purpose for which it is collected or processed.
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor checks that ingested records, identity data, tickets, logs and customer- maintained knowledge bases have refresh and correction processes.
requirements.pb_interpretation: For PrivateBox, this is mainly about imported records, identity data, tickets, logs and customer- maintained knowledge bases. Knowledge-base refresh process; metadata versioning; ticket correction process; customer guidance on stale or duplicated data.
requirements.operational_evidence: Refresh logs; change history; source metadata visible in samples.
requirements.failure_modes: "Garbage in, garbage out" with no accountability; static knowledge bases that quietly age.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox supports controlled ingestion and versioned content updates to improve information quality.

requirements.title: Openness
requirements.requirement: The responsible party must maintain the documentation of all processing operations under its responsibility (s 17) and must take reasonably practicable steps to ensure the data subject is aware of specified information at or as soon as reasonably practicable after collection (s 18 — purpose, source, voluntariness, consequences, law, recipients, transfer plans, rights).
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor checks both internal records (RoPA-equivalent) and outward-facing notices are maintained and reflect actual processing.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Maintain documented processing inventory and notice set. Record all processing operations; maintain PrivateBox privacy notice; create customer deployment notice template; keep version control. Customer notices end-user processing; PrivateBox notices its own website, sales, support, telemetry and staff processing.
requirements.operational_evidence: Published notices match actual data flows; version history shows refresh cadence.
requirements.failure_modes: Template notices that do not match reality; generic notices that omit telemetry or support flows; inventory and notices drift apart over time.
requirements.external_safe_claim: We document our processing and provide customers with deployment guidance to support POPIA transparency duties.

requirements.title: Security safeguards
requirements.requirement: Secure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information by taking appropriate, reasonable technical and organisational measures (s 19). Operators must process personal information only with the responsible party's knowledge or authorisation, treat the information as confidential and be contractually bound to maintain s 19 security (s 20-21). Notify the Regulator and affected data subjects of security compromises as soon as reasonably possible; operators notify the responsible party immediately (s 22).
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor scrutinises both prevention (controls) and response (notification timeliness). Dis-Chem enforcement notice and Department of Basic Education infringement notice are visible indicators of Regulator priorities.
requirements.pb_interpretation: This is the operational centre of POPIA for PrivateBox. Produce a complete security control baseline. MFA; strong password rules; PAM; audit logs; encryption where applicable; signing of updates; secure config baseline; vulnerability management; secure support access; regular review. Operator scenario: mandatory DPA with confidentiality + immediate-notification clauses; sub-processor flow-down. Section 22 dual-track: PB-as-operator notifies customer-as-RP immediately; customer has statutory notification duty to Regulator + data subjects.
documented_evidence: Access control matrix; PAM / MFA configuration
requirements.operational_evidence: Pen-test reports; config reviews; access reviews; tabletop exercise records; past incidents trace through notification timelines.
requirements.cadence: Annual + per architecture change + per incident
requirements.failure_modes: Stopping at "customer hosts it"; ad-hoc Slack/WhatsApp support and screen photos; weak admin controls (Dis-Chem pattern); shared admin passwords; "friendly support" with no operator contract; undeclared subprocessors; waiting for forensic certainty before s 22 notification.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox implements security and incident controls consistent with POPIA's safeguard requirements, with clear customer and vendor responsibilities.

requirements.title: Data subject participation
requirements.requirement: A data subject has the right to request confirmation of whether personal information about them is held (s 23), to request access to that information (s 23-25 in the manner provided by PAIA), and to request correction or deletion of inaccurate or non-compliant information (s 24).
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor / buyer tests routing — does the right request get to the right party (vendor records vs deployment records), and does the response complete within reasonable time?
requirements.pb_interpretation: Build rights-routing and support workflows. Rights matrix; customer-facing routing clause; admin tools to search / export / delete logs or support bundles; identity verification steps. Customer answers customer-data requests; PrivateBox answers requests about its own records and supports customer retrieval where contractually required.
documented_evidence: Rights routing matrix (vendor vs deployment data)
requirements.operational_evidence: Sample rights request traces from intake to response; routing decisions documented.
requirements.failure_modes: Telling data subjects to contact "whoever" with no routing logic; no distinction between vendor records and customer records; no identity verification.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox provides documented request-routing and support processes to help customers address POPIA rights requests.

requirements.title: Special personal information
requirements.requirement: Processing of special personal information — religious or philosophical beliefs, race or ethnic origin, trade union membership, political persuasion, health or sex life, biometric information, criminal behaviour — is generally prohibited unless a POPIA exception applies (consent, public interest, legal obligation, etc.).
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor scrutinises that special-PI processing has documented lawful basis, enhanced safeguards, and segregated controls. PrivateBox is sold into pharmacy / healthcare / HR contexts — high-likelihood deployment area.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Treat special PI as an enhanced control class. Special-data classification scheme; stricter access controls; safe defaults for logs; no vendor training use; incident severity upgrade; contractual flags. Customer usually decides lawful basis; PrivateBox supports restricted access, segregation, auditability and no unauthorised reuse.
requirements.operational_evidence: Pharmacy / healthcare / HR deployments show enhanced-control configurations and sign-off.
requirements.cadence: Per deal screening + annual profile review
requirements.failure_modes: Assuming health data is "safe" merely because hosted locally; treating "student data" as ordinary data; voice / image AI processed without recognising biometric category.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox supports higher-control deployments for environments handling special personal information.

requirements.title: Children's personal information
requirements.requirement: Processing of personal information of children is generally prohibited unless a statutory exception applies (e.g., consent of competent person, necessity for legal proceedings, public interest). Re-use of legacy collection requires authorisation.
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor scrutinises whether child-data scenarios have explicit lawful basis and enhanced controls. Education and family-services deployments are key triggers.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Add a children-data decision gate. Pre-sales screening for child-data use; parental / competent- person routing guidance; stricter data handling and support rules. No child-data deployment without explicit approval.
requirements.operational_evidence: Education / youth-service deals show completed child-data screening and contract limiters.
requirements.failure_modes: Treating "student data" as ordinary data; using family or school data without recognising that children are included.
requirements.external_safe_claim: Where clients intend to process children's information, PrivateBox applies additional scoping and control measures.

requirements.title: Information Officer designation, duties and registration
requirements.requirement: The head of a private body is the Information Officer for POPIA purposes (s 55) and may delegate in writing. The Information Officer's duties include encouraging compliance, dealing with requests, cooperating with the Regulator, providing the Regulator with information, and additional duties set out in Regulation 4 (compliance framework, PIIA, PAIA Manual maintenance, internal measures, awareness). Registration of the Information Officer with the Information Regulator is required.
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator registration is publicly verifiable. Operating cadence (training, awareness, framework review, deputies) is the additional bar. Buyers verify both within minutes of asking.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Make privacy governance visible and functioning. Register IO; designate deputies where needed; define RACI; maintain framework; annual review; internal awareness training. PrivateBox IO governs its own organisation; customer IO governs theirs; cross-engagement on incidents.
requirements.operational_evidence: Public registration verifiable on Regulator portal; current training records; framework recently reviewed.
requirements.failure_modes: Having a policy owner but no registered or active IO; named IO with no operating system around them; framework drafted but not used.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox maintains formal privacy governance and information officer responsibilities for its own operations.

requirements.title: Prior authorisation triggers and procedure
requirements.requirement: Prior authorisation by the Information Regulator is required for certain high-risk processing activities, including: processing unique identifiers for purposes other than originally specified in conjunction with another responsible party's processing; processing information on criminal behaviour or unlawful or objectionable conduct on behalf of third parties; processing for credit reporting; transferring special personal information or personal information of children to a third country that does not provide an adequate level of protection.
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor / buyer checks PrivateBox can identify prior-authorisation triggers in scoping and does not blithely assume "not our issue."
requirements.pb_interpretation: Make prior-authorisation screening mandatory for high-risk deals. Pre-sales legal questionnaire; unique-identifier linking review; foreign support review; special-data transfer review. Customer generally applies as RP; PrivateBox identifies triggers and supports evidence.
requirements.operational_evidence: Sample regulated-sector opportunity shows completed screening and decision record.
requirements.failure_modes: Missing trigger because nobody mapped optional support flows; treating "matching" as purely technical when unique-identifier linking is a statutory trigger.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox highlights scenarios that may require additional POPIA authorisation or customer legal review.

requirements.title: Direct marketing by means of unsolicited electronic communications
requirements.requirement: Processing of personal information for the purpose of direct marketing by means of any form of electronic communication is prohibited unless the data subject has given consent or is a customer of the responsible party (subject to limited conditions). Each communication must include details of the sender and a means to opt out.
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor checks consent records, opt-out workflow and suppression list maintenance. Form 4 (objection form) readiness is the additional indicator.
requirements.pb_interpretation: PrivateBox's own marketing operations are in scope. Obtain proper marketing consent; maintain opt-out; suppression list; Form 4 readiness. B2B is in scope (POPIA does not exempt B2B from s 69).
requirements.operational_evidence: Sample CRM records show consent capture date and source; suppression list reconciles to opt-outs.
requirements.failure_modes: Treating "POPIA does not affect B2B marketing" as a defence; informal opt-out without suppression list; missing consent date / source.
requirements.external_safe_claim: Our own marketing practices are governed by consent and objection handling controls.

requirements.title: Automated decision-making
requirements.requirement: A data subject may not be subject to a decision which results in legal consequences for them, or which affects them substantially, that is based solely on the basis of automated processing of personal information intended to provide a profile of them — subject to specific exceptions (necessary for entering into / performance of a contract subject to safeguards; required by law). Where exception applies, suitable measures must safeguard the data subject's legitimate interests, including the opportunity to make representations.
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor / buyer scrutinises whether AI workflows make automated decisions that materially affect individuals. Healthcare diagnosis, credit scoring, hiring recommendations, legal advice are key triggers.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Critical for legal / finance / healthcare sector deployments. Disable "fully autonomous adjudication" without human review support; document all such workflows; provide human-in-the-loop by default for consequential outputs. PB designs controls; customer owns final business decision. Section 71 ↔ EU AI Act Art. 22 (automated processing) + Art. 14 (human oversight) — single integrated approach recommended.
documented_evidence: Human-in-the-loop design records per use case
Customer-facing scope disclosure on automated workflows
Representation / appeal pathway for affected data subjects
requirements.operational_evidence: Sample customer deployment shows human review point; customer ack of automated-decision scope.
requirements.failure_modes: Saying "AI recommendation" and "automated decision" are obviously different without analysis; workflow that makes the AI determinative in practice while marketed as advisory.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox is designed to support customer-controlled decision processes with human-review points for consequential workflows.

requirements.title: Trans-border flows of personal information
requirements.requirement: A responsible party may not transfer personal information about a data subject to a third party in a foreign country unless one of the following applies: the recipient is subject to law / binding corporate rules / binding agreement that provides adequate protection; the data subject consents to the transfer; the transfer is necessary for performance of a contract; the transfer is for the benefit of the data subject; or the transfer is necessary for legal proceedings.
requirements.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor / buyer scrutinises offshore support, cloud telemetry, hosted services. Even "temporary support access" by a foreign engineer is a transfer.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Default local deployment strongly reduces transfer risk. Where transfers occur (offshore support, sub-processors, telemetry endpoints), maintain transfer register with adequacy / binding agreement / consent record per transfer. Cross-border-aware support routing; regional sub-processor selection; customer notice template for cross-border disclosures.
documented_evidence: Adequacy / binding agreement / consent record per transfer
requirements.operational_evidence: Each transfer has documented basis; sub-processor list reconciles to live deployments; support routing matches approved-country list.
requirements.failure_modes: Assuming remote support access is not a transfer; "temporary" support by foreign engineer treated as ad-hoc rather than transfer; cloud telemetry routed through default offshore endpoint without disclosure.
requirements.external_safe_claim: Cross-border exposures, where present, are separately scoped and governed; the default local deployment minimises section 72 exposure.

requirements.title: Codes of conduct, enforcement and penalties
requirements.requirement: POPIA empowers the Information Regulator to issue, monitor and enforce. Chapter 7 provides for sectoral Codes of Conduct that bind responsible parties in the sector. Chapter 10 sets out enforcement powers (assessments, enforcement notices, civil remedies). Chapter 11 sets out offences, penalties, and administrative fines (up to R10 million or 10 years imprisonment for serious offences).
requirements.auditor_intent: Visible Regulator enforcement (Dis-Chem, Department of Basic Education) signals priorities and sets the bar for evidence quality. "Good intentions" does not satisfy enforcement.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Build for inspectability. Keep complete evidence pack; maintain assessment-ready documentation; monitor sector codes; keep incident and rights logs. Marketing claims must lag evidence maturity, not lead it.
requirements.operational_evidence: Procurement and legal review can be answered from one evidence pack; mock assessment held annually.
requirements.cadence: Annual mock assessment + continuous evidence maintenance
requirements.failure_modes: Marketing claims ahead of evidence maturity; treating Regulator enforcement as theoretical; sector codes ignored.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox maps its controls to POPIA requirements and maintains evidence to support customer due diligence and Regulator engagement.

popia-cond-1.title: Accountability
popia-cond-1.requirement: The responsible party must ensure that the conditions set out in Chapter 3, and all the measures that give effect to those conditions, are complied with at the time of determining the purpose and means of processing, and during the processing itself.
popia-cond-1.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor checks that privacy controls exist from design through operation, not only in policy documents — privacy-by-design rather than privacy-by-brochure.
popia-cond-1.pb_interpretation: Embed compliance into product governance. Privacy review gate in SDLC; release checklist; exception approval; risk register. Customer accountable for its purposes; PrivateBox accountable for product and service-design controls and for its own corporate processing as RP.
popia-cond-1.operational_evidence: Sample releases checked for privacy sign-off; risk register reviewed at management cadence.
popia-cond-1.failure_modes: Treating accountability as "customer problem"; policy with no approvals, no cadence, no owner.
popia-cond-1.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox implements controls designed to support POPIA-aligned processing from deployment through operations.

popia-cond-2.title: Processing limitation
popia-cond-2.requirement: Processing must be lawful, in a reasonable manner that does not infringe the privacy of the data subject, adequate, relevant and not excessive (minimality). Processing must have a justification (consent, contract performance, legal obligation, legitimate interests, etc.). Personal information must be collected directly from the data subject unless an exception applies.
popia-cond-2.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor verifies collection is necessary, justified and minimised; logs / debug bundles / telemetry reflect minimisation; indirect collection has documented basis.
popia-cond-2.pb_interpretation: Default-minimise data captured by the product and vendor tooling. Disable unnecessary prompt retention by default; minimise telemetry fields; restrict support file collection; define lawful purposes for any vendor-side data received. Indirect-collection sources (connectors, imports, admin syncs) tagged and explicit.
popia-cond-2.operational_evidence: Default configurations show minimisation; sample telemetry payload confirms PII-free; sample support bundle confirms scoping.
popia-cond-2.failure_modes: "Debug everything" culture; undefined support collections; collecting "just in case" data; silent background imports.
popia-cond-2.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox is designed to minimise vendor-side handling of customer personal information.

popia-cond-3.title: Purpose specification
popia-cond-3.requirement: Personal information must be collected for a specific, explicitly defined and lawful purpose. Records must not be retained longer than necessary for the purpose, subject to limited statutory exceptions. On expiry of retention, records must be destroyed or de-identified in a manner that prevents reconstruction.
popia-cond-3.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor checks every dataset, log, cache, backup and exported report has a defined purpose and retention rule, and that destruction is verified.
popia-cond-3.pb_interpretation: Define retention by data class; auto-deletion workflows; decommission wipe standard; backup retention logic; local cache expiry. Customer owns primary in-environment retention; PrivateBox owns vendor-side copies and guides customer retention defaults.
documented_evidence: Retention schedule per data class (logs, backups, support bundles, repositories)
popia-cond-3.operational_evidence: Sample expired data shows deletion proof; backup retention configuration matches schedule.
popia-cond-3.failure_modes: Forgotten support bundles; stale backups; persistent logs; backups outliving production.
popia-cond-3.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox supports customer-defined retention and secure deletion processes for local deployments.

popia-cond-4.title: Further processing limitation
popia-cond-4.requirement: Further processing of personal information must be in accordance with, or compatible with, the purpose for which it was collected. Compatibility is assessed against the relationship between purposes, the nature of the information, the consequences of further processing, the manner of collection, and any contractual rights and obligations.
popia-cond-4.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor scrutinises whether vendor reuses customer data for analytics, model improvement, support training or demos without explicit lawful basis and customer agreement.
popia-cond-4.pb_interpretation: Ban unapproved secondary use. Contract clause forbidding model training on customer data without explicit agreement; separate support data from product improvement data; approval workflow for any reuse. "De-identified if possible" is not a blanket excuse.
documented_evidence: DPA / operator agreement with reuse-prohibition clause
Separation of support data from product improvement data
popia-cond-4.operational_evidence: Sample vendor-side dataset traces show no customer data beyond support case scope.
popia-cond-4.failure_modes: Using support data for product training; "operational analytics" becoming secondary use without review; treating "anonymised" as automatic permission.
popia-cond-4.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox does not assume any right to repurpose customer data beyond agreed services.

popia-cond-5.title: Information quality
popia-cond-5.requirement: The responsible party must take reasonably practicable steps to ensure that personal information is complete, accurate, not misleading and updated where necessary, having regard to the purpose for which it is collected or processed.
popia-cond-5.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor checks that ingested records, identity data, tickets, logs and customer- maintained knowledge bases have refresh and correction processes.
popia-cond-5.pb_interpretation: For PrivateBox, this is mainly about imported records, identity data, tickets, logs and customer- maintained knowledge bases. Knowledge-base refresh process; metadata versioning; ticket correction process; customer guidance on stale or duplicated data.
popia-cond-5.operational_evidence: Refresh logs; change history; source metadata visible in samples.
popia-cond-5.failure_modes: "Garbage in, garbage out" with no accountability; static knowledge bases that quietly age.
popia-cond-5.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox supports controlled ingestion and versioned content updates to improve information quality.

popia-cond-6.title: Openness
popia-cond-6.requirement: The responsible party must maintain the documentation of all processing operations under its responsibility (s 17) and must take reasonably practicable steps to ensure the data subject is aware of specified information at or as soon as reasonably practicable after collection (s 18 — purpose, source, voluntariness, consequences, law, recipients, transfer plans, rights).
popia-cond-6.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor checks both internal records (RoPA-equivalent) and outward-facing notices are maintained and reflect actual processing.
popia-cond-6.pb_interpretation: Maintain documented processing inventory and notice set. Record all processing operations; maintain PrivateBox privacy notice; create customer deployment notice template; keep version control. Customer notices end-user processing; PrivateBox notices its own website, sales, support, telemetry and staff processing.
popia-cond-6.operational_evidence: Published notices match actual data flows; version history shows refresh cadence.
popia-cond-6.failure_modes: Template notices that do not match reality; generic notices that omit telemetry or support flows; inventory and notices drift apart over time.
popia-cond-6.external_safe_claim: We document our processing and provide customers with deployment guidance to support POPIA transparency duties.

popia-cond-7.title: Security safeguards
popia-cond-7.requirement: Secure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information by taking appropriate, reasonable technical and organisational measures (s 19). Operators must process personal information only with the responsible party's knowledge or authorisation, treat the information as confidential and be contractually bound to maintain s 19 security (s 20-21). Notify the Regulator and affected data subjects of security compromises as soon as reasonably possible; operators notify the responsible party immediately (s 22).
popia-cond-7.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor scrutinises both prevention (controls) and response (notification timeliness). Dis-Chem enforcement notice and Department of Basic Education infringement notice are visible indicators of Regulator priorities.
popia-cond-7.pb_interpretation: This is the operational centre of POPIA for PrivateBox. Produce a complete security control baseline. MFA; strong password rules; PAM; audit logs; encryption where applicable; signing of updates; secure config baseline; vulnerability management; secure support access; regular review. Operator scenario: mandatory DPA with confidentiality + immediate-notification clauses; sub-processor flow-down. Section 22 dual-track: PB-as-operator notifies customer-as-RP immediately; customer has statutory notification duty to Regulator + data subjects.
documented_evidence: Access control matrix; PAM / MFA configuration
popia-cond-7.operational_evidence: Pen-test reports; config reviews; access reviews; tabletop exercise records; past incidents trace through notification timelines.
popia-cond-7.cadence: Annual + per architecture change + per incident
popia-cond-7.failure_modes: Stopping at "customer hosts it"; ad-hoc Slack/WhatsApp support and screen photos; weak admin controls (Dis-Chem pattern); shared admin passwords; "friendly support" with no operator contract; undeclared subprocessors; waiting for forensic certainty before s 22 notification.
popia-cond-7.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox implements security and incident controls consistent with POPIA's safeguard requirements, with clear customer and vendor responsibilities.

popia-cond-8.title: Data subject participation
popia-cond-8.requirement: A data subject has the right to request confirmation of whether personal information about them is held (s 23), to request access to that information (s 23-25 in the manner provided by PAIA), and to request correction or deletion of inaccurate or non-compliant information (s 24).
popia-cond-8.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor / buyer tests routing — does the right request get to the right party (vendor records vs deployment records), and does the response complete within reasonable time?
popia-cond-8.pb_interpretation: Build rights-routing and support workflows. Rights matrix; customer-facing routing clause; admin tools to search / export / delete logs or support bundles; identity verification steps. Customer answers customer-data requests; PrivateBox answers requests about its own records and supports customer retrieval where contractually required.
documented_evidence: Rights routing matrix (vendor vs deployment data)
popia-cond-8.operational_evidence: Sample rights request traces from intake to response; routing decisions documented.
popia-cond-8.failure_modes: Telling data subjects to contact "whoever" with no routing logic; no distinction between vendor records and customer records; no identity verification.
popia-cond-8.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox provides documented request-routing and support processes to help customers address POPIA rights requests.

popia-s-special-pi.title: Special personal information
popia-s-special-pi.requirement: Processing of special personal information — religious or philosophical beliefs, race or ethnic origin, trade union membership, political persuasion, health or sex life, biometric information, criminal behaviour — is generally prohibited unless a POPIA exception applies (consent, public interest, legal obligation, etc.).
popia-s-special-pi.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor scrutinises that special-PI processing has documented lawful basis, enhanced safeguards, and segregated controls. PrivateBox is sold into pharmacy / healthcare / HR contexts — high-likelihood deployment area.
popia-s-special-pi.pb_interpretation: Treat special PI as an enhanced control class. Special-data classification scheme; stricter access controls; safe defaults for logs; no vendor training use; incident severity upgrade; contractual flags. Customer usually decides lawful basis; PrivateBox supports restricted access, segregation, auditability and no unauthorised reuse.
popia-s-special-pi.operational_evidence: Pharmacy / healthcare / HR deployments show enhanced-control configurations and sign-off.
popia-s-special-pi.cadence: Per deal screening + annual profile review
popia-s-special-pi.failure_modes: Assuming health data is "safe" merely because hosted locally; treating "student data" as ordinary data; voice / image AI processed without recognising biometric category.
popia-s-special-pi.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox supports higher-control deployments for environments handling special personal information.

popia-s-children.title: Children's personal information
popia-s-children.requirement: Processing of personal information of children is generally prohibited unless a statutory exception applies (e.g., consent of competent person, necessity for legal proceedings, public interest). Re-use of legacy collection requires authorisation.
popia-s-children.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor scrutinises whether child-data scenarios have explicit lawful basis and enhanced controls. Education and family-services deployments are key triggers.
popia-s-children.pb_interpretation: Add a children-data decision gate. Pre-sales screening for child-data use; parental / competent- person routing guidance; stricter data handling and support rules. No child-data deployment without explicit approval.
popia-s-children.operational_evidence: Education / youth-service deals show completed child-data screening and contract limiters.
popia-s-children.failure_modes: Treating "student data" as ordinary data; using family or school data without recognising that children are included.
popia-s-children.external_safe_claim: Where clients intend to process children's information, PrivateBox applies additional scoping and control measures.

popia-s-information-officer.title: Information Officer designation, duties and registration
popia-s-information-officer.requirement: The head of a private body is the Information Officer for POPIA purposes (s 55) and may delegate in writing. The Information Officer's duties include encouraging compliance, dealing with requests, cooperating with the Regulator, providing the Regulator with information, and additional duties set out in Regulation 4 (compliance framework, PIIA, PAIA Manual maintenance, internal measures, awareness). Registration of the Information Officer with the Information Regulator is required.
popia-s-information-officer.auditor_intent: Regulator registration is publicly verifiable. Operating cadence (training, awareness, framework review, deputies) is the additional bar. Buyers verify both within minutes of asking.
popia-s-information-officer.pb_interpretation: Make privacy governance visible and functioning. Register IO; designate deputies where needed; define RACI; maintain framework; annual review; internal awareness training. PrivateBox IO governs its own organisation; customer IO governs theirs; cross-engagement on incidents.
popia-s-information-officer.operational_evidence: Public registration verifiable on Regulator portal; current training records; framework recently reviewed.
popia-s-information-officer.failure_modes: Having a policy owner but no registered or active IO; named IO with no operating system around them; framework drafted but not used.
popia-s-information-officer.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox maintains formal privacy governance and information officer responsibilities for its own operations.

popia-s-prior-auth.title: Prior authorisation triggers and procedure
popia-s-prior-auth.requirement: Prior authorisation by the Information Regulator is required for certain high-risk processing activities, including: processing unique identifiers for purposes other than originally specified in conjunction with another responsible party's processing; processing information on criminal behaviour or unlawful or objectionable conduct on behalf of third parties; processing for credit reporting; transferring special personal information or personal information of children to a third country that does not provide an adequate level of protection.
popia-s-prior-auth.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor / buyer checks PrivateBox can identify prior-authorisation triggers in scoping and does not blithely assume "not our issue."
popia-s-prior-auth.pb_interpretation: Make prior-authorisation screening mandatory for high-risk deals. Pre-sales legal questionnaire; unique-identifier linking review; foreign support review; special-data transfer review. Customer generally applies as RP; PrivateBox identifies triggers and supports evidence.
popia-s-prior-auth.operational_evidence: Sample regulated-sector opportunity shows completed screening and decision record.
popia-s-prior-auth.failure_modes: Missing trigger because nobody mapped optional support flows; treating "matching" as purely technical when unique-identifier linking is a statutory trigger.
popia-s-prior-auth.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox highlights scenarios that may require additional POPIA authorisation or customer legal review.

popia-s-direct-marketing.requirement: Processing of personal information for the purpose of direct marketing by means of any form of electronic communication is prohibited unless the data subject has given consent or is a customer of the responsible party (subject to limited conditions). Each communication must include details of the sender and a means to opt out.
popia-s-direct-marketing.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor checks consent records, opt-out workflow and suppression list maintenance. Form 4 (objection form) readiness is the additional indicator.
popia-s-direct-marketing.pb_interpretation: PrivateBox's own marketing operations are in scope. Obtain proper marketing consent; maintain opt-out; suppression list; Form 4 readiness. B2B is in scope (POPIA does not exempt B2B from s 69).
popia-s-direct-marketing.operational_evidence: Sample CRM records show consent capture date and source; suppression list reconciles to opt-outs.
popia-s-direct-marketing.failure_modes: Treating "POPIA does not affect B2B marketing" as a defence; informal opt-out without suppression list; missing consent date / source.
popia-s-direct-marketing.external_safe_claim: Our own marketing practices are governed by consent and objection handling controls.

Where exception applies, suitable measures must safeguard the data subject's legitimate interests, including the opportunity to make representations.
popia-s-automated-decisions.auditor_intent: Regulator / auditor / buyer scrutinises whether AI workflows make automated decisions that materially affect individuals. Healthcare diagnosis, credit scoring, hiring recommendations, legal advice are key triggers.
popia-s-automated-decisions.pb_interpretation: Critical for legal / finance / healthcare sector deployments. Disable "fully autonomous adjudication" without human review support; document all such workflows; provide human-in-the-loop by default for consequential outputs. PB designs controls; customer owns final business decision. Section 71 ↔ EU AI Act Art. 22 (automated processing) + Art. 14 (human oversight) — single integrated approach recommended.
documented_evidence: Human-in-the-loop design records per use case
Customer-facing scope disclosure on automated workflows
Representation / appeal pathway for affected data subjects

popia-s-automated-decisions.failure_modes: Saying "AI recommendation" and "automated decision" are obviously different without analysis; workflow that makes the AI determinative in practice while marketed as advisory.

popia-s-cross-border.title: Trans-border flows of personal information
popia-s-cross-border.requirement: A responsible party may not transfer personal information about a data subject to a third party in a foreign country unless one of the following applies: the recipient is subject to law / binding corporate rules / binding agreement that provides adequate protection; the data subject consents to the transfer; the transfer is necessary for performance of a contract; the transfer is for the benefit of the data subject; or the transfer is necessary for legal proceedings.

Chapter 11 sets out offences, penalties, and administrative fines (up to R10 million or 10 years imprisonment for serious offences).

king-v.full_name: King V Code on Corporate Governance for South Africa, 2025
king-v.issuer: Institute of Directors in South Africa (IoDSA) / King Committee

effective_dates.milestone: King V Code launched by IoDSA / King Committee
King V effective for financial years commencing on or after this date
First mandatory King V Disclosure Framework reports (for FY2026)

companion_standards.name: King V Foundational Concepts
companion_standards.role: Foundational concepts paper (IoDSA, 2025). Defines the four governance outcomes and integrated- thinking / double-materiality framing. Read alongside the Code.

companion_standards.name: King V Glossary
companion_standards.role: Glossary of King V terminology. Required reading to apply terminology precisely (e.g., "governing body" vs "board"; "Ubuntu-Botho"; "Concluding Statement").

companion_standards.name: King V Disclosure Framework and Disclosure Template
companion_standards.role: Mandatory companion for any organisation claiming application of King V. Sets the structure for annual disclosure including narratives per principle, recommended-practice deviation register with compensating measures, and the concluding statement on the four outcomes.

companion_standards.name: Companies Act 71 of 2008 (South Africa) and Companies Amendment Act 16 of 2024
companion_standards.role: Statutory underpinning. Sections 7, 30, 66, 72, 75–76 and Regulation 43 set out fiduciary duties, statutory audit triggers (PI Score), and the social and ethics committee. Amendment Act 16 of 2024 introduces new remuneration provisions (not yet in force at time of authoring).

pb_application.why_chosen: King V is the natural board-level overlay above PrivateBox's parallel work on ISO 27001, ISO 27701, POPIA, ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF, NIST SSDF, and the EU AI Act. PB is a South African private (Pty) Ltd, founder-led, scaling, not JSE-listed, but sells into JSE-listed and regulated enterprise clients (banks, insurers, law firms, healthcare, professional services). Those clients' procurement diligence expects evidence of governance maturity calibrated to the SA corporate-governance code of record. King V (binding for FYs commencing on or after 2026-01-01) supersedes King IV in its entirety and adds an explicit AI-governance flagship in Principle 10 Practice 109c covering ethics, human centricity, accountability, transparency, explainability, security, privacy, fairness, trustworthiness and human oversight. Commercially, King V lets PB consolidate cyber, privacy, AI, supplier and customer-trust governance under one board-evidenced narrative — one set of artefacts, one disclosure document, multiple framework labels. Apply-and-explain is the reporting model; certification is not a thing in King V.

on_prem.rationale: King V applies to PrivateBox as a South African organisation regardless of deployment model. Proportionality means recommended practices are scaled to PB's stage and complexity; the thirteen principles themselves apply universally. Compensating measures are documented where full enterprise practices are not adopted (e.g., combined committees, co-sourced internal audit, cosec-as-a-service).

ai_factory.rationale: AI Factory ringfenced multi-tenant deployment in a partner DC introduces additional board-level oversight questions: partner-DC supplier relationship governance (P10 108f third-party assurance), elevated AI risk profile (P8 + P10 109c), and cross-jurisdictional regulator exposure (P9). Until partner DC is selected (oq-partner-dc-selection), some King V P6 committee work plans, P8 risk treatments, and P10 supplier assurance arrangements are provisional.

top_risks: Founder/CEO dual-hatting as Chair without a Lead Independent NED designated (Practice 48 prohibits the former; Practice 47 requires the compensating measure). Visible governance gap in procurement diligence.

Independence-by-assertion — NED labelled "independent" without holistic assessment against the nine factors of Practice 42 (significant funder, securities-significance, former-executive within 3 yrs, former-audit-team within 3 yrs, significant supplier/customer, related-party, >9 yrs tenure, performance-contingent remuneration). Webber Wentzel and ENSafrica both flag this as a common King IV-to-King V transition gap.
King V Disclosure Framework absent from website. Mandatory for any organisation claiming application of King V. Failure to publish weakens every external claim about King V alignment.
AI governance under Practice 109c not operationalised through System Cards, eval/red-team records, and human-override SOPs. "Human in the loop" as decoration is a known failure pattern.
Combined committees lack at least one independent NED member. King V requires independent NED on audit, risk and social-and-ethics committees; failure is a Practice 66 / Practice 75 gap.
Whistleblower channel routed to the implicated party rather than an independent provider (Practice 13d failure pattern).
Conflating ISO 27001 certification or SOC 2 attestation with "King V compliance" in marketing. These are complementary but not substitutable; equating them is a red-flag claim per source §16.
PI Score crossing Companies Act thresholds without architecture pre-built — statutory S&E committee and statutory audit suddenly required without preparation.
Going-concern, solvency, liquidity reporting absent from CFO board pack — Practice 22 obligation.

disclosure_framework.primary_artefact: King V Governance Disclosure (annual; board-approved; published on PrivateBox website). Accompanies an integrated Trust & Governance Pack covering ISO 27001/27701, POPIA, ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF and EU AI Act posture for procurement diligence.
disclosure_framework.proportionality_rules: Principles apply universally; recommended practices are scaled to PrivateBox's size, business- model complexity, ownership structure and economic/social/environmental impact. Compensating measures are documented where full enterprise practices are not adopted (e.g., combined Audit/Risk/Technology Committee; combined Social, Ethics and Remuneration Committee; co-sourced internal audit; cosec-as-a-service; AI Oversight Working Group at management level instead of a dedicated board AI committee). Proportionality is not licence to dilute the principles themselves.
disclosure_framework.jse_listing_overlap: PrivateBox is not JSE-listed. Its customers (banks, insurers, listed enterprises) are subject to JSE Listings Requirements which incorporate King-style governance expectations. PB's King V alignment is therefore a downstream procurement / supplier-diligence signal rather than a direct listing obligation.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: All King V principles, practices and disclosure obligations belong to PrivateBox as the organisation.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer governs its own organisation under whatever governance code applies to them.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: King V is PB-organisation-bound; customer governance is its own domain.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: PB Board-approved AI Governance Policy; AI System Cards; Human Oversight SOP for PB-built features.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer's deployment-side AI governance for their use cases.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Practice 109c values (ethics, human centricity, accountability, transparency, explainability, security, privacy, fairness, trustworthiness) propagate to both governance domains.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: PB-side supplier programme covering model providers, sub-processors, hosting partners.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer-side supplier programme covering PrivateBox as their vendor.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Visible bidirectional supplier diligence — PB carries DD on its suppliers; customers carry DD on PB.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: PB's King V Governance Disclosure (annual website publication); Trust & Governance Pack (NDA-gated).
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer determines what to include in its own disclosures about PB as supplier.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: PB's disclosure aims to be procurement-ready so customer disclosure inherits substantive evidence.

evidence_families.name: Board Charter and governance anchors
examples: Board Charter (King V-mapped; reserved matters; conflicts framework)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Anchors Principles 1, 5, 6, 7. First document procurement / investor diligence requests.

evidence_families.name: Board, committee and director evaluation
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Practice 8 cadence; lack of follow-up is a known failure pattern.
evidence_families.cadence: At least every 2 years; externally facilitated at maturity

evidence_families.name: Skills matrix and independence assessment
examples: Board Skills Matrix (cyber, AI, privacy, financial, sector)
Per-NED Independence Assessment Memo (against Practice 42 nine factors)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Practice 42 holistic independence assessment is a common King V gap.

evidence_families.name: Committee terms of reference and minutes
examples: Combined Audit / Risk / Technology Committee TOR
Combined Social, Ethics and Remuneration Committee TOR
Nominations TOR (or section in Board Charter while combined)
Quarterly minutes; chair reports to board

evidence_families.name: Code of Ethics, AI Ethics Statement, Whistleblowing
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Principle 2; ethics treated as a poster on the wall is a common failure.

evidence_families.name: Strategy, KPIs and integrated thinking
examples: Integrated-thinking review of the six capitals (financial, intellectual, human, social, natural, manufactured)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Principle 3; "strategy = sales targets" is a known failure pattern.

evidence_families.name: External reports and King V Disclosure Framework
examples: King V Governance Disclosure (annual; published on website)
Integrated Trust & Governance Pack (NDA-gated for procurement diligence)
Board approval minutes for each external report

evidence_families.name: CEO Mandate and Delegation of Authority Framework
examples: Delegation of Authority Framework (monetary, contractual, hiring, sub-processor, security exception, AI deployment thresholds)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Principle 7; "founder retains undocumented decision-making power" is a known failure.

evidence_families.name: Risk policy, register and appetite
examples: Enterprise Risk Register (with cyber, AI, supplier, key-person, BCM, regulatory, commercial sub-registers)
BCM / DR plan + last test report

examples: Obligations Register (POPIA, Cybercrimes, ECTA, Companies Act, EU AI Act, sector pass-through)
Adopted-standards register (ISO 27001, ISO 27701, ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF, NIST SSDF, King V)

evidence_families.name: Technology, information, data and AI governance policies
examples: AI Governance Policy + AI Ethics Statement
AI System Cards per production model
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Principle 10 flagship — Practice 109c AI governance is the highest-leverage King V update.

examples: Working-group charter (reports into Audit / Risk / Tech committee)

examples: Fair-and-responsible test between executive and overall employee remuneration
Non-binding advisory votes (if S&E committee triggered)

evidence_families.name: Combined assurance map and internal audit charter
examples: Combined Assurance Map (per significant risk × assurance provider × cadence)
External IA quality review (Practice 131f — every 5 years)
Statutory audit (when triggered by PI Score)

examples: Stakeholder Map (clients, end-users, regulators, sub-processors, model providers, employees, investors, community)
AI Transparency Disclosure pack for clients

evidence_families.cadence: Annual framework review + continuous engagement log

scenarios.name: Pre-PI-Score threshold (combined committees, founder-led)
scenarios.description: PB is below Public Interest Score thresholds for statutory audit and Reg 43 social-and-ethics committee. Combined Audit/Risk/Technology and combined Social/Ethics/Remuneration committees are proportionate. Founder/CEO leads; at least one independent NED present.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Composition target — at least one independent NED, scaling toward majority NED.
Combined committee architecture proportionate at this stage.
If founder is Chair, Lead Independent NED must be designated.

scenarios.notes: Starting posture; build statutory architecture pre-emptively.

scenarios.name: PI Score crossing Reg 43 threshold (statutory S&E committee mandatory)
scenarios.description: Public Interest Score crosses the threshold requiring a statutory Social and Ethics Committee under Companies Act s 72(4) and Regulation 43. Combined committee must be reconstituted.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Combined S&E/Remuneration committee splits into separate statutory S&E.
Statutory S&E committee composition and Reg 43 reporting cadence triggers.

scenarios.notes: Architecture should be pre-built to make this transition incremental, not disruptive.

scenarios.name: First Independent NED appointed
scenarios.description: First independent non-executive director joins the board. Independence assessment against the nine factors of Practice 42 documented. Lead Independent designation may transfer or be retired depending on Chair status.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Composition rebalanced toward NED majority target.
Independence rationale documented in writing.
Chair / Lead Independent status reassessed.

scenarios.notes: Procurement-visible milestone; signals governance maturity to enterprise buyers.

scenarios.name: First material AI incident
scenarios.description: Material AI-related incident (significant model failure, harm to data subjects, regulator concern, customer-impacting outage). Triggers escalation from AI Oversight Working Group to Audit/Risk/Technology Committee, board notification, and external disclosure consideration.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: AI governance under Practice 109c — incident triggers comprehensive review.
Risk governance — incident updates risk register and treatment plan.
AI Oversight escalation to committee; post-incident System Card update.

scenarios.notes: Tests the human-oversight and accountability machinery; visible to regulators and customers.

scenarios.name: First EU enterprise customer onboarded
scenarios.description: First EU-based enterprise customer activates EU AI Act extraterritorial reach. Triggers EU AI Act high-risk assessment, deployer/provider role analysis, and elevated disclosure obligations.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Compliance universe expands to include EU AI Act.
AI governance must satisfy EU AI Act Articles 9, 10, 13, 14, 15, 26.

scenarios.notes: Regulatory horizon-scanning signal; documented in obligations register.

scenarios.name: Founder transition / CEO succession activates
scenarios.description: Founder-CEO succession plan activates — planned transition, departure, or incapacity. Tests Practice 79 succession plan and Practice 80 chair-led CEO performance review machinery.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Delegation framework and succession plan tested.
DoA framework holds without founder personal authority.

scenarios.notes: Key-person risk realised; institutional memory and documented authority become critical.

scenarios.name: Series B / institutional investment round
scenarios.description: Institutional investment round (Series B+ or equivalent) — investor diligence demands board independence, externally facilitated evaluation, formal committee architecture, and integrated disclosure.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Majority NED / most independent target accelerated.
External reporting matures — investor-facing King V Disclosure becomes mandatory in diligence pack.
Externally facilitated board evaluation expected by investor.

scenarios.notes: Trigger for "Later" phase of source pack §14 roadmap.

open_questions.question: When does PrivateBox appoint its first independent NED, and what is the target trajectory for board independence?
candidate_answers: Within 6 months (with Lead Independent designation in interim)
Tied to Series B / institutional investment milestone
open_questions.notes: First independent NED is the foundational governance signal. Until appointed, Lead Independent NED designation is the compensating measure if founder is Chair. Source §14 "Now (0-90 days)" recommends immediate appointment.

open_questions.question: At what stage does PrivateBox split combined committees into separate Audit, Risk, Technology, S&E, and Remuneration committees?
candidate_answers: At PI Score threshold (statutory S&E split mandatory)
Phased — Audit first, then Risk/Technology, then S&E and Remuneration
open_questions.notes: Combined committees are proportionate at PB's current stage but architecture must pre-build the split so the PI Score crossing is incremental, not disruptive.

open_questions.question: Cosec-as-a-service vs in-house company secretary?
candidate_answers: Hybrid — outsourced filings, in-house governance support
open_questions.notes: Source §14 "Now" recommends cosec-as-a-service. Practice 84-90 governance support function must be arms-length from management.

open_questions.question: How is the AI Oversight Working Group constituted and what does it formally report into?
candidate_answers: Cross-functional management group reporting into Audit/Risk/Technology committee
AI oversight responsibility absorbed into existing CTO mandate
open_questions.notes: Source pack §7 recommends cross-functional management working group reporting into Audit/Risk/Tech committee — proportionate substitute for a full AI committee. Practice 109c human oversight requirements must trace through the working group's records.

open_questions.question: When does PrivateBox publish its first King V Governance Disclosure?
candidate_answers: v0.5 draft now (early-adoption signal in Trust Pack)
Full v1.0 within 12 months (with FY2026 reporting cycle)
Wait for FY2026 reports issued in 2027 (mandatory cycle)
open_questions.notes: Source §1 recommends early-adoption signal in Trust Pack from now. First mandatory cycle is FY2026 reports issued in 2027. Publishing earlier is procurement-friendly.

open_questions.question: What is PrivateBox's current Public Interest Score and projected threshold-crossing date?
candidate_answers: Approaching Reg 43 statutory S&E threshold (projected within 12 months)
open_questions.notes: PI Score is calculated per Companies Act formula (staff, turnover, beneficial interests, third-party liabilities). Knowing current value and trajectory enables architecture-ahead-of- requirement planning.

open_questions.question: How does PrivateBox staff internal audit — co-sourced, outsourced, or build in-house?
open_questions.notes: Practice 131c permits co-sourced for a scaling firm. External quality review every 5 years (Practice 131f) applies regardless of arrangement.

open_questions.question: Is the founder-CEO succession plan documented and refreshed?
open_questions.notes: Practice 79 explicit. Investor diligence pre-Series B will demand evidence. Key-person risk materialises if not documented.

requirements.title: Leadership
requirements.requirement: The governing body leads ethically and effectively as the focal point of corporate governance in the organisation, embodying tone-from-the-top through integrity, competence, responsibility, accountability, fairness and transparency.
requirements.auditor_intent: Adviser / procurement / investor reviewer checks that founder-led culture is balanced with collective board accountability and that the board operates under a written charter.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Founder-led culture must be balanced with collective board accountability. Board operates under a written charter (Practice 4) reviewed annually. Each director cultivates the six characteristics (Practice 1) with declared interests at least annually and at every meeting (Practices 39-40). Performance evaluation at least every two years (Practices 6-9).
requirements.operational_evidence: Directors can describe the charter and how it was last reviewed; evaluation actions tracked.
requirements.failure_modes: Founder/CEO acting as both Chair and CEO without Lead Independent designation; charter copied from a template and not lived.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox's governing body operates under a board charter and director conduct expectations aligned with King V Principle 1.

crosswalk.note: ISMS leadership maps to King V leadership at organisational level.
PIMS leadership analogue.
AIMS leadership analogue.
POPIA accountability principle is the statutory underpinning King V Principle 1 instantiates.

requirements.title: Ethics
requirements.requirement: The governing body governs the ethics of the organisation in a way that enables an ethical culture and responsible corporate citizenship.
requirements.auditor_intent: Procurement / investor reviewer tests that ethics is governed (board-approved code, training cadence, whistleblower channel, sanctions log) rather than assumed.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Critical because clients trust PrivateBox not to misuse data. Board approves ethics policy / code of conduct (Practice 12), AI ethics statement (linked to Principle 10 AI values), anti-corruption / anti-fraud / anti-money-laundering programmes (Practice 12c), protected whistleblowing channel (Practice 13d). Ethics built into hiring, performance, supplier onboarding.
requirements.operational_evidence: Trace a reported ethics matter from intake to closure; periodic assurance on ethics effectiveness (Practice 14).
requirements.failure_modes: Ethics conflated with compliance; whistleblower channel that routes to the implicated party; "ethics" treated as a poster on the wall.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox's ethics framework, anti-corruption controls and protected whistleblowing process are governed by the board in accordance with King V Principle 2.

crosswalk.note: Management responsibilities; ethics overlay.
AI Policy operationalises the AI-specific portion of King V ethics.
POPIA accountability principle reaches ethics governance.

requirements.group_name: Strategy, performance and sustainable value creation
requirements.title: Strategy, performance and sustainable value creation
requirements.requirement: The governing body ensures that the organisation's purpose, strategy and business model support performance that creates sustainable value within the organisation's economic, social and environmental context.
requirements.auditor_intent: Reviewer expects board-approved strategy with SMART KPIs, integrated-thinking framing across the six capitals, and explicit negative-impact mitigation.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Articulate purpose, business model, strategy and KPIs. Tie product roadmap to sustainable client outcomes — data sovereignty, on-prem assurance, lower regulatory risk for clients. Apply integrated thinking to financial, intellectual, human, social/relationship, natural (energy footprint), manufactured capitals.
requirements.operational_evidence: Board minute approving strategy and budget; quarterly KPI review; founder/CEO performance review against strategic targets.
requirements.failure_modes: Performance reduced to ARR; no link to systems value; strategy not refreshed; sustainability ignored on assumption that "we're a small tech company".
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox's strategy is approved and overseen by the governing body using integrated thinking aligned with King V Principle 3.

crosswalk.note: ISMS objectives integrated with strategy.
AIMS objectives integrated with strategy.

requirements.title: Reporting
requirements.requirement: The governing body ensures that external reports enable stakeholders to make informed assessments of how the organisation creates, preserves and erodes value, applying double-materiality concepts and integrity controls over reports.
requirements.auditor_intent: Procurement / investor reviewer expects King V Disclosure Framework published on website with a concluding statement on the four outcomes; integrated narrative across strategy / risk / AI / security / privacy.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Even as a private company, the trust pack, security disclosures, AI factsheets and governance section function as external reports. Board approves the suite (Practice 25b). Annual integrated report or proportionate equivalent. King V Disclosure Framework published on the website (Practice 27c). Double-materiality applied (Practice 26).
documented_evidence: Board approval minutes for each external report
requirements.operational_evidence: Each external report has a board approval minute and accessible publication record; concluding statement on outcomes signed off.
requirements.failure_modes: Marketing copy mistaken for governance disclosure; King V Disclosure Framework absent (mandatory failure); concluding statement copied without evidence trail.
requirements.external_safe_claim: PrivateBox publishes a governance disclosure structured in accordance with the King V Disclosure Framework.

crosswalk.note: Privacy / external disclosure overlap.
PIMS controllers transparency / notice obligation overlap.
POPIA s 17-18 openness condition; external disclosure obligations.

requirements.title: Composition of the governing body
requirements.requirement: The governing body ensures that its composition is balanced with respect to the mix of competencies, diversity and independence appropriate to the organisation's purpose and context.
requirements.auditor_intent: Reviewer expects majority NED, most independent, diverse, with independence holistically assessed against Practice 42 nine factors.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Founder-led — must consciously over-correct for independence. Target at least one independent NED at minimum, scaling to majority pre-IPO.
