king-v-rp-98.objective: The governing body maintains an obligations register linking each obligation to a control owner, covering binding laws, regulator obligations of clients that pass through, adopted non-binding standards, and regulatory horizon scanning.
king-v-rp-98.pb_interpretation: Obligations register: POPIA, Cybercrimes Act, ECTA, Companies Act, sector pass-through, EU AI Act extra-territorial reach. Adopted standards: ISO 27001, ISO 27701, ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF, NIST SSDF, EU AI Act voluntary GPAI code, King V. Horizon-scanning log.
king-v-rp-98.operational_evidence: Regulatory changes trace from horizon scan to register update to control adjustment.
king-v-rp-98.failure_modes: Register stale; horizon scanning informal; King V (voluntary) confused with binding obligations.
king-v-rp-98.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox maintains a documented compliance universe covering binding obligations, sector pass- through, and adopted non-binding standards in alignment with King V Practice 98.

crosswalk.note: Legal / regulatory / contractual compliance under ISMS.

king-v-rp-103.title: Data and information lifecycle
king-v-rp-103.objective: The governing body approves data and information policies covering acquisition, creation, use, dissemination, disposal, ethical/responsible management, legal compliance, classification of sensitive data, confidentiality, integrity, availability, privacy, data quality, and third-party / cross-border data risk management.
king-v-rp-103.pb_interpretation: Data classification scheme; data flow map; retention schedule; disposal SOP; cross-border / sub-processor register. Telemetry data dictionary; PII handling default-off. Linked to ISMS A.5.9-5.13/A.5.34, PIMS 6.x/7.x, POPIA s 13-22, EU AI Act Art. 10.
king-v-rp-103.operational_evidence: Recent data classification decisions traced; retention reviewed; sub-processor list current.
king-v-rp-103.failure_modes: Telemetry data treated as "metadata not personal" without analysis; retention undocumented.
king-v-rp-103.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox governs data and information through board-approved policies covering lifecycle, classification, privacy and third-party risk in alignment with King V Practice 103.

crosswalk.note: Data classification.
PIMS data accuracy / quality.
AIMS data for AI systems.
POPIA processing limitation + purpose specification + retention.

king-v-rp-108.title: Technology governance
king-v-rp-108.objective: The governing body approves technology policies covering legal compliance, resilience / disaster recovery / testing, benefit realisation vs investment, responsible disposal, effective cybersecurity, and outsourced / third-party tech assurance.
king-v-rp-108.pb_interpretation: Cybersecurity policy + cyber dashboard + IR plan linked to ISO 27001 and NIST CSF. Sub-processor register with SOC 2 / ISO 27001 / DPA evidence. Foundation-model provider terms reviewed.
king-v-rp-108.operational_evidence: Cyber dashboard reviewed quarterly; sub-processor reviews current; resilience tested annually.
king-v-rp-108.failure_modes: CISO reporting only annually; cyber treated as IT; foundation-model provider treated as untouchable.
king-v-rp-108.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox's technology governance — including cybersecurity, resilience, and third-party assurance — is board-overseen in alignment with King V Practice 108.

crosswalk.note: Secure SDLC + secure architecture + secure coding.
AIMS responsible design and development processes.

king-v-rp-109.title: AI governance (the King V flagship)
king-v-rp-109.objective: The governing body ensures that AI systems adhere to ethics, human centricity, accountability, transparency, explainability, security, privacy, fairness and trustworthiness; that clear accountability is established for AI decisions, actions, outputs and outcomes; and that human oversight and override mechanisms commensurate with risk are implemented.
king-v-rp-109.pb_interpretation: Defining battleground for PrivateBox. AI inventory; AI risk classification (low / limited / high / prohibited per EU AI Act); AI System Cards; pre-deployment review; model eval / red-team; human-override SOP; training-data provenance; output traceability; bias monitoring; AI incident process; user disclosure when AI is in use. AI Oversight Working Group reports into Audit/Risk/ Tech committee.
documented_evidence: AI System Cards per production model
AI Oversight Working Group charter + minutes
king-v-rp-109.operational_evidence: Board pack includes AI risk dashboard, model changes, incidents; AI System Cards trace to production; human-override tested.
king-v-rp-109.cadence: Annual policy review + per AI release / change
king-v-rp-109.failure_modes: AI deployed before policy in place; "human in the loop" as decoration; AI ethics treated as marketing.
king-v-rp-109.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox's AI governance is overseen by the board through a board-approved AI policy that operationalises the King V Practice 109c values, requires human oversight commensurate with risk, and is supported by AI System Cards for all production models.

crosswalk.note: AIMS AISIA + impact assessment + bias methodology + human oversight operationalise Practice 109c.
POPIA s 71 automated decision-making is the binding underpinning for human-override requirement.
EU AI Act risk-mgmt + transparency + human oversight + accuracy/robustness align with Practice 109c values.

king-v-rp-110.title: Periodic assurance on technology effectiveness, compliance and ethics
king-v-rp-110.objective: The governing body ensures that periodic assurance is obtained over technology effectiveness, compliance with legal and regulatory requirements, and adherence to organisational ethics in technology use.
king-v-rp-110.pb_interpretation: Assurance providers: ISO 27001 surveillance audits, ISO 27701 audits, NIST AI RMF self- assessment, pen tests, model red-teams, internal audit. Combined assurance map covers technology risk.
king-v-rp-110.operational_evidence: Assurance providers operate at cadence; findings closed.
king-v-rp-110.failure_modes: Gaps in coverage of AI risk; assurance providers unmapped.
king-v-rp-110.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox obtains periodic assurance over technology effectiveness, compliance and ethics from a documented set of assurance providers in alignment with King V Practice 110.

crosswalk.note: ISMS internal audit feeds technology assurance.
AIMS internal audit feeds technology assurance.

king-v-rp-114.title: Fair-and-responsible remuneration with malus/clawback
king-v-rp-114.objective: The remuneration policy operationalises a fair-and-responsible test between executive and overall employee remuneration; variable pay arrangements include malus and clawback provisions; non- binding advisory votes are held where applicable.
king-v-rp-114.pb_interpretation: Fair-and-responsible analysis recalculated annually. Malus / clawback specifically covers AI-misuse-linked variable pay. NED fees policy submitted to shareholders where applicable.
documented_evidence: Remuneration Policy with malus / clawback clauses
king-v-rp-114.operational_evidence: Variable pay traces through documented policy; malus / clawback enforceable.
king-v-rp-114.failure_modes: Founder-set salaries with no committee discipline; absence of malus / clawback; missing fair-and- responsible analysis.
king-v-rp-114.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox's remuneration policy includes fair-and-responsible analysis and malus / clawback for variable pay in alignment with King V Practice 114.

king-v-rp-121.title: Combined Assurance Map
king-v-rp-121.objective: The governing body approves an explicit Combined Assurance Map identifying assurance providers per significant risk, level of assurance, and cadence.
king-v-rp-121.pb_interpretation: Map covers ISO 27001/27701 surveillance, NIST AI RMF self-assessment, SOC 2 Type II (when pursued), penetration testing, internal financial controls review, statutory audit (when triggered), product-level red-team / model-evaluation reports.
king-v-rp-121.operational_evidence: Map reconciles to live engagements; gaps highlighted and remediated.
king-v-rp-121.failure_modes: Gaps in coverage; audit fatigue; remediation not tracked.
king-v-rp-121.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox operates a documented Combined Assurance Map covering significant risks across line management, specialist functions, internal audit, and external assurance in alignment with King V Practice 121.

crosswalk.note: ISMS audit execution mapped via combined assurance.
AIMS audit execution mapped via combined assurance.

king-v-rp-129.title: Internal Audit Charter
king-v-rp-129.objective: The governing body approves an Internal Audit Charter setting out the role of internal audit, its place in combined assurance, scope, authority, independence, and reporting line. For a scaling firm, co-sourced or outsourced arrangements with a named accountable executive are acceptable (Practice 131c).
king-v-rp-129.pb_interpretation: Co-sourced internal audit acceptable. Named accountable executive (e.g., COO or CFO with cosec support). Risk-based plan reviewed annually. External independent quality review at least once every 5 years (Practice 131f).
king-v-rp-129.operational_evidence: IA plan executed; findings tracked through closure; quality review completed within cadence.
king-v-rp-129.failure_modes: Same team auditing itself; external quality review skipped at 5-year mark.
king-v-rp-129.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox operates an Internal Audit function under a board-approved charter, co-sourced where proportionate, with external quality review at the King V Practice 131f cadence.

crosswalk.note: ISMS internal audit programme feeds King V internal audit.
AIMS internal audit programme feeds King V internal audit.

king-v-rp-134.title: Stakeholder Engagement Framework
king-v-rp-134.objective: The governing body approves a Stakeholder Engagement Framework setting out methodology, risk / opportunity analysis from stakeholder interests, dispute-resolution mechanisms, and periodic assurance on stakeholder management.
king-v-rp-134.pb_interpretation: PrivateBox Stakeholder Engagement Framework covers clients (especially regulated-sector procurement and CISO/DPO functions), client end-users (data subjects), regulators (Information Regulator, sector regulators), sub-processors and model providers, employees, founders/investors, community/society.
king-v-rp-134.operational_evidence: Framework reviewed annually; engagement log current; stakeholder feedback feeds strategy.
king-v-rp-134.cadence: Annual framework review + continuous engagement log
king-v-rp-134.failure_modes: Founder-only stakeholder relationships with no institutional memory; no dispute resolution.
king-v-rp-134.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox operates a documented Stakeholder Engagement Framework covering clients, regulators, employees, suppliers and community in alignment with King V Practice 134.

crosswalk.note: ISMS interested-parties identification feeds stakeholder framework.
AIMS interested-parties identification feeds stakeholder framework.

soc-2.full_name: AICPA SOC 2 (System and Organization Controls 2) — Trust Services Criteria

pb_application.why_chosen: SOC 2 is the dominant US / global enterprise procurement attestation. Legal, finance and healthcare buyers — particularly US-headquartered or US-investor-backed — demand a SOC 2 Type II report before signing material contracts. Unlike ISO 27001 (certification) or POPIA (statutory law), SOC 2 is a CPA-issued attestation evaluated against PrivateBox's own stated service commitments and system requirements. The Trust Services Criteria are organisation-neutral principles; the audit assesses whether PrivateBox's controls — as designed (Type I) and as operated (Type II) — meet its own commitments. For a customer-hosted AI vendor, the real audit question is "what system is in scope, what promises does PrivateBox make, and which controls are owned by PrivateBox, the customer, or carved-out subservice organisations?" PB's position: Security category (CC1-CC9) is the mandatory baseline; Confidentiality is the natural next addition given the legal/finance/healthcare confidentiality stake; Availability and Privacy are added only where service commitments genuinely warrant them. PB never claims "SOC 2 certified" — SOC 2 reports are issued, not certified. PB publishes reports under NDA via a Trust Center model (Workday / Microsoft / Anthropic pattern).

on_prem.rationale: SOC 2 system scope is the set of components PrivateBox actually operates or owns. For on-prem deployments, PrivateBox's in-scope system covers product development, build pipelines, repositories, code-signing, update delivery infrastructure, support tooling, telemetry relays (where used), customer-facing documentation, and corporate functions (HR, finance, legal). Customer-hosted runtime components are CUEC-controlled (Complementary User Entity Controls) — the customer assumes responsibility for local IAM, host hardening, local backups, physical security, lane controls for remote support, SIEM review, restore testing. This split is described explicitly in the system description per DC1-DC9.

ai_factory.rationale: AI Factory ringfenced multi-tenant deployment in a partner DC adds the partner DC as a subservice organisation. Carve-out vs inclusive treatment is an open question (oq-soc-2-subservice-treatment). Until partner DC is selected and a SOC 2 report is available from that provider, the scope and treatment are provisional.

top_risks: Marketing language outrunning SOC 2 commitments — "fully secure", "100% available", "AI is always accurate" — none of which can be attested. Marketing alignment to commitments register is the control.
Implicit broader categories — claiming SOC 2 without specifying which Trust Services Criteria are in scope (e.g., implying Availability or Privacy when only Security is attested).
Complementary user entity controls (CUECs) not communicated to customers in the admin guide / deployment prerequisites — leading to gaps that the auditor cannot test and the customer cannot operate.
Subservice organisations not declared (code signing, CI/CD, support tooling, telemetry relays, ticketing, identity providers). Carve-out vs inclusive treatment must be explicit.

System description out of sync with what auditors observe — e.g., model swaps, connector additions, support tooling changes that occurred during a Type II period without being captured in DC9 (significant changes).
Type II evidence collapses for "policy-only" controls — procedures written but not actually followed in practice. CC5.3 is the bellwether.
Remediation tracker without closure proof — open findings ageing without owner / due date / closure evidence. CC4.2 audit-readiness indicator.
Vendor due diligence absent for AI-specific suppliers (foundation models, code signing, telemetry relays) — CC9.2 high-impact gap.

soa_guidance.principles: Unlike ISO 27001, SOC 2 has no Statement of Applicability concept. The functional equivalent is the criteria-to-control mapping in the system description (DC5 + CC5.1) which traces each in-scope criterion to specific PrivateBox controls. Best practice: maintain a SOC 2 Control Universe document with columns for criterion / control title / control description / control owner / control frequency / type I evidence / type II evidence / observation period / supporting SOPs.
soa_guidance.na_justification_rules: Non-applicable criteria must be explained in the system description per DC8. Common exclusions: full Privacy category (P1-P8) where PrivateBox does not collect personal information in its in-scope system; selected Processing Integrity sub-criteria where PrivateBox makes no bounded reliability commitments; specific Availability criteria where the customer owns runtime capacity. Exclusions must connect to scope and service role, not "we don't want to" or "our policy forbids that event".
soa_guidance.honesty_rules: Service commitments and system requirements (DC2) must match real operations. Avoid vague promises like "fully secure by default" or "always available". State precisely what PrivateBox operates and what it does NOT operate. External claims register tracks every public-facing SOC 2 statement and its supporting evidence.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: System description per DC1-DC9; service commitments register; system requirements register.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer's own internal documentation of its use of PrivateBox.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Customer responsibilities (CUECs) explicitly enumerated in the system description.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: All controls within PrivateBox's in-scope system (development, build, signing, distribution, support, telemetry).
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Local IAM, host hardening, physical security, local backups, restore testing, SIEM review (CUECs).
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Auditor cannot test CUECs; report describes them so customer's own audit can verify.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: Vendor due diligence; subservice register; assessment of subservice SOC 2 reports.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer's own assessment of PrivateBox as a subservice in customer's SOC 2.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Carve-out vs inclusive treatment declared per subservice in DC7.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: Continuous evidence collection across observation period; remediation tracker; control execution logs.
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: N/A for SOC 2 attestation period (customer is report consumer, not control operator for PB's system).
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Trust Center / NDA-gated distribution; customer ingests report as part of own vendor risk programme.

shared_responsibility_model.pb_owned: Marketing / sales language alignment with commitments register; red-flag claim list (no "certified").
shared_responsibility_model.cl_owned: Customer's own marketing about its use of PrivateBox.
shared_responsibility_model.shared_note: Customer-facing security overview reflects actual SOC 2 scope and report status.

evidence_families.name: Governance, oversight, and ethical conduct
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: CC1.1-CC1.5 foundation; ethical tone and accountability anchor every later criterion.
evidence_families.cadence: Annual review + per hire / org change

evidence_families.name: Information and communication
examples: Customer-facing security overview / admin guide / support terms
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: CC2.1-CC2.3; shared-responsibility clarity tested via external comms artefacts.

evidence_families.name: Risk assessment, fraud risk, change risk
examples: Risk register (AI, support, deployment, customer-hosting risks)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: CC3.1-CC3.4; living risk process tied to service commitments.
evidence_families.cadence: Quarterly register review + annual methodology + on material change

evidence_families.name: Monitoring activities and remediation tracker
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: CC4.1-CC4.2; Type II depends on recurring monitoring and timely remediation.

evidence_families.name: Control activities and technology general controls (SDLC)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: CC5.1-CC5.3; high-weight audit area for software vendors.

evidence_families.name: Logical access security (provisioning, RBAC, least privilege)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: CC6.1-CC6.3; most buyer diligence lands on access controls.

evidence_families.name: Physical access and asset disposal
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: CC6.4-CC6.5; CUEC-shared in on-prem deployments.

evidence_families.name: Boundary protection, transmission, malware prevention
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: CC6.6-CC6.8; major shared-responsibility area in customer-hosted deployments.

evidence_families.name: Vulnerability management and operational monitoring
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: CC7.1-CC7.2; telemetry-off customers need explicit caveats.

evidence_families.name: Security event evaluation, IR execution, recovery
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: CC7.3-CC7.5; zero-events periods still need event listings and rationale.

evidence_families.name: Change management for code, models, prompts, configs
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: CC8.1; model updates and prompt/config changes must be treated as controlled changes.

evidence_families.name: Business continuity, DR, and vendor risk management
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: CC9.1-CC9.2; vendor governance for AI-specific suppliers is a recurring gap.

evidence_families.name: Availability — capacity, backups, recovery (conditional)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: A1.1-A1.3; only when Availability is in scope and service commitments warrant.

evidence_families.name: Confidentiality — classification, retention, disposal (conditional)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: C1.1-C1.2; often added even when Privacy is not scoped, for legal / finance / healthcare buyers.

evidence_families.name: Processing Integrity — specs, input / processing / output / storage (conditional)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: PI1.1-PI1.5; only scope where bounded reliability commitments exist.

evidence_families.name: Privacy — notice, consent, collection, use, access, disclosure (conditional)
examples: Vendor privacy clauses + breach notice templates
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: P1-P8; often customer-owned in customer-hosted deployments; exclusions explained per DC8.

evidence_families.name: Type I and Type II evidence packs
examples: Type II evidence (continuous collection across observation period)
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: Distinct evidentiary models; Type I asks "designed?", Type II asks "operated?".

evidence_families.name: System description and service commitments
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: The system description is the foundation; everything else is tested against it.

evidence_families.name: Subservice organisation governance
examples: Carve-out / inclusive treatment decisions per subservice
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: DC7; auditor reviews how PrivateBox governs its own subservice organisations.

evidence_families.name: Customer responsibility matrix / CUEC documentation
evidence_families.why_auditor_cares: DC6; substantial in on-prem scope; auditor cannot test CUECs but verifies they are documented.

description_criteria.title: Types of services provided
description_criteria.pb_interpretation: PrivateBox is a customer-hosted AI software / appliance with optional remote support, telemetry, updates, and maintenance. The system description distinguishes default local deployment from any managed or remote components in scope.
description_criteria.on_prem_note: Default local deployment must be separated from optional managed / support features so the auditor understands which components PrivateBox actually operates.
description_criteria.evidence: Service catalogue; product architecture summary; deployment reference architecture.

description_criteria.title: Principal service commitments and system requirements
description_criteria.requirement: State principal service commitments and system requirements.
description_criteria.pb_interpretation: SOC 2 is evaluated against service commitments and system requirements, not generic security claims. PrivateBox states exactly what it does and does not operate in customer environments. Commitments cover security, support response, update delivery, confidentiality, and any bounded processing or availability commitments — narrowly and precisely.
description_criteria.on_prem_note: Avoid vague promises like "fully secure by default" or "always available". State what PrivateBox does NOT operate in customer environments.
description_criteria.evidence: Contract templates; SLA / SOW language; security commitments register.

description_criteria.title: Infrastructure, software, people, procedures, and data
description_criteria.requirement: Describe infrastructure, software, people, procedures, and data.
description_criteria.pb_interpretation: Infrastructure covers PB dev / support / update infrastructure plus any customer-hosted runtime components in scope. Software covers the core AI service, models, connectors, admin portals, support tools, update services, logging / monitoring tools. People covers engineering, security, support, deployment, customer success, incident coordinators. Procedures cover provisioning, support access, release, patch advisory, incident handling, export handling, RMA, backup / restore. Data covers customer documents, prompts, logs, telemetry, model artefacts, admin data, support bundles, audit records.
description_criteria.on_prem_note: Customer-hosted servers, models, connectors, update tools, and support channels must be explicitly addressed if relevant; identify which runtime infrastructure is customer-owned and whether included, carved out, or a CUEC dependency.
description_criteria.evidence: Architecture diagram; software inventory / SBOM; org chart; SOP library; data-flow diagrams.

description_criteria.title: Significant system incidents
description_criteria.pb_interpretation: Material incidents affecting commitments must be disclosed in the system description. Include incidents in PrivateBox-managed components and clearly allocate customer-site incidents by responsibility. Zero-incident periods still need event listings and rationale.
description_criteria.on_prem_note: Separate PrivateBox-owned incidents from customer-local incidents beyond PrivateBox visibility or responsibility.

description_criteria.title: Applicable trust services criteria and related controls
description_criteria.requirement: Describe applicable criteria and related controls.
description_criteria.pb_interpretation: The narrative ties stated controls to the chosen trust services criteria. If PrivateBox claims alignment to Security only, the description does not imply broader categories unless they are actually scoped. Criteria-to-control mapping is the functional equivalent of an SoA.
description_criteria.on_prem_note: Do not imply Availability / Privacy if these are really customer-owned or out of scope.
description_criteria.evidence: Scope memo; criteria-to-control mapping; SOC 2 Control Universe document.

description_criteria.title: Complementary user entity controls (CUECs)
description_criteria.requirement: Describe complementary user entity controls assumed in design.
description_criteria.pb_interpretation: Substantial for PrivateBox in on-prem scope. CUECs cover local IAM, host hardening, local backups, physical security, lane controls for remote support, SIEM review, restore testing. Customer responsibility matrix is the productised artefact.
description_criteria.on_prem_note: Likely the most material section for an on-prem AI vendor — most operational controls have CUEC dependencies.
description_criteria.evidence: Customer responsibility matrix; admin guide; deployment prerequisites.

description_criteria.title: Subservice organisations and carve-out / inclusive treatment
description_criteria.requirement: Describe subservice organisations and carve-out or inclusive treatment.
description_criteria.pb_interpretation: Third-party services relied on by the in-scope system must be described. Examples for PB include update delivery services, support portals, remote-access tooling, ticketing, telemetry relays, code-signing services, CI/CD providers. State whether inclusive or carve-out model is used per subservice. Subservice SOC 2 reports ingested where available.
description_criteria.on_prem_note: Carve-out vs inclusive treatment is a scoping decision that affects the audit scope and report structure. Default for PB is carve-out for most subservices; inclusive only where PB has significant operational control.
description_criteria.evidence: Vendor register; contracts; SOC reports; architecture diagrams.

description_criteria.title: Nonrelevant criteria
description_criteria.pb_interpretation: Critical for Privacy and some Processing Integrity criteria in a customer-controlled environment. Exclusion rationale must connect to scope and service role — not to "we don't want to" or "our policy forbids that event".
description_criteria.on_prem_note: Especially important when Privacy is excluded from scope despite PrivateBox being sold into sectors that process personal information — explain that PrivateBox's in-scope system does not collect personal information directly from data subjects.

description_criteria.title: Significant system changes during the Type II period
description_criteria.requirement: Describe significant system changes during the Type II period.
description_criteria.pb_interpretation: Type II mandatory if changes occurred. Material candidates include version shifts, model swaps, new connectors, remote-support changes, telemetry changes, organisational changes. In a rapidly-changing AI product company, this section is non-trivial.
description_criteria.on_prem_note: Track model and prompt-template changes as system changes; they materially affect controls around AI behaviour and customer experience.
description_criteria.evidence: Change log; release history; architecture review minutes.

scenarios.name: Type I attestation — point-in-time design
scenarios.description: SOC 2 Type I report addresses whether the system description is presented in accordance with the description criteria and whether controls were suitably designed as of a point in time. No operating-effectiveness evidence required.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: All CC criteria in scope by design.
Risk-to-control mapping is the primary Type I artefact.

scenarios.notes: Suitable as first-time SOC 2 milestone; ~6-month preparation cycle. Type II follow-up typical.

scenarios.name: Type II attestation — 3-month observation period (first-time)
scenarios.description: First-time Type II report covering a short observation period (typically 3 months). Establishes operating-effectiveness baseline; report acceptable for procurement but with explicit period caveat.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Monitoring activities must be evidenced over the period.
Procedure execution evidence for the period.

scenarios.notes: Public examples show 3-month and 11-month and 12-month periods in use.

scenarios.name: Type II attestation — 11-12 month observation period (mature)
scenarios.description: Mature Type II report covering full annual cycle. Procurement-preferred. Subsequent reports may "bridge" between periods.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Annual monitoring cycle traced through evidence.
Change management evidence across releases.

scenarios.notes: Standard for procurement diligence at scale; bridge letters between periods.

scenarios.name: Subservice organisation — carve-out treatment
scenarios.description: PrivateBox identifies the subservice (e.g., update delivery, ticketing, code signing) in the system description but the auditor does not test the subservice's controls. The customer is expected to obtain assurance over the subservice (typically via that subservice's own SOC 2 report).

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Vendor due diligence covers carved-out subservices.

scenarios.notes: Default treatment for most PrivateBox subservices; reduces audit scope.

scenarios.name: Subservice organisation — inclusive treatment
scenarios.description: PrivateBox treats the subservice as part of its own system; the auditor tests subservice controls or relies on subservice management's representations. Typical only where PrivateBox has significant operational control over the subservice.

triggered_requirements.scenario_specific_note: Subservice subject to PB-led vendor governance.

scenarios.notes: Rare for PrivateBox; only for tightly-integrated subservices where audit visibility is high.

open_questions.question: Does PrivateBox pursue Type I first, then Type II, or go straight to Type II?
candidate_answers: Type I first (3-6 months), then Type II (covering 3-12 months)
Straight to Type II (3-month initial period, then 12-month subsequent periods)
Bridge approach (Type I plus simultaneous Type II observation)
open_questions.notes: Type I provides faster market signal but requires Type II follow-up for procurement credibility. Many vendors do Type I → Type II in sequence over 9-12 months.

open_questions.question: Which CPA firm performs the SOC 2 attestation?
candidate_answers: EY / KPMG / PwC / Deloitte (Big 4; high enterprise prestige)
open_questions.notes: Service auditor choice affects cost, audit-time, prestige signal, and depth of process. Impartiality: any firm used for advisory cannot then perform the attestation engagement.

open_questions.question: Which additional Trust Services categories beyond Security are in scope?
candidate_answers: Security + Confidentiality (procurement-friendly for legal / finance / healthcare)
open_questions.notes: Each additional category adds audit cost and effort. Confidentiality is the natural next addition; Privacy is often customer-owned in customer-hosted deployments and may be excluded with DC8 rationale.

open_questions.question: What observation period does PrivateBox commit to for its first Type II report?
open_questions.decision_owner: Top Management + Audit / Risk / Tech committee
open_questions.notes: Source pack notes public reports range from 3-month to 12-month observation periods. Shorter periods get a report faster but with explicit period caveat.

open_questions.question: What is PrivateBox's default subservice organisation treatment — carve-out or inclusive?
candidate_answers: Carve-out by default (reduces audit scope; customer obtains subservice assurance)
open_questions.notes: Carve-out is standard for most subservices. Inclusive is rare and only where PB has significant operational control.

open_questions.question: How does PrivateBox distribute SOC 2 reports to customers?
candidate_answers: Trust Center / portal under NDA (Workday / Microsoft / Anthropic pattern)
Per-customer access via legal review per request
open_questions.notes: Trust Center model is procurement-friendly and scales. NDA pattern matters for SOC 2 distribution by AICPA distribution restrictions.

open_questions.question: How does PrivateBox disclose SOC 2 readiness pre-first-attestation?

candidate_answers.Aligned-to: PB has implemented controls aligned to SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria; first audit in progress

candidate_answers.In progress: PB is preparing for first SOC 2 attestation; readiness review complete

open_questions.notes: Source pack flags marketing language as a top risk. Pre-attestation phrasing must avoid implying a report has been issued.

requirements.title: Integrity and ethical values
requirements.requirement: The entity demonstrates a commitment to integrity and ethical values through standards of conduct that are communicated, with deviations addressed.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Board-approved code of conduct; security expectations for staff and contractors; disciplinary process. Small-company governance acceptable if oversight is real and evidenced.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Approved policies; signed acknowledgements; issue log demonstrating the disciplinary path exists and has been used.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Onboarding attestations across the period; annual refresh records; event-driven enforcement records.
requirements.failure_modes: Missing attestations; inconsistent enforcement; unapproved policy; no contractor scope.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox maintains documented ethical and security conduct standards aligned to SOC 2 CC1.1.

crosswalk.note: ISMS management responsibilities; ethical-conduct overlay.
King V Principle 2 ethics + Practice 12 code of ethics.

requirements.title: Oversight independence
requirements.requirement: The board of directors (or equivalent oversight body) demonstrates independence from management and exercises oversight of the development and performance of internal control.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Oversight body or formal leadership forum with security agenda. Founder-led firms still need demonstrable oversight even if not a public-company board. King V independent NED appointment strengthens this.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Charter; meeting cadence schedule.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Minutes across the period showing actual challenge and follow-up; quarterly or more frequent meetings.
requirements.failure_modes: Security oversight only informal; meetings held but no challenge or action.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox has documented oversight of its control environment.

crosswalk.note: ISMS leadership and commitment.
King V Principle 1 leadership.

requirements.title: Structure, authority and responsibility
requirements.requirement: Management, with board oversight, establishes structures, reporting lines, and appropriate authorities and responsibilities in pursuit of objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Org chart and responsibility matrix covering product, ops, security, support. Include remote support authority and emergency powers explicitly.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Org chart and role descriptions; RACI covering support and security.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Approved org updates across the period; delegated approvals samples.
requirements.cadence: As changed; at least annual review
requirements.failure_modes: Ambiguous authority; no owner list; outdated org chart; approval by wrong person.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox defines security and operational responsibilities clearly.

crosswalk.note: ISMS roles, responsibilities and authorities.
King V composition + Delegation of Authority Framework.

requirements.title: Competence and staffing
requirements.requirement: The entity demonstrates a commitment to attract, develop and retain competent individuals in alignment with objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Formal hiring criteria; onboarding checklist; annual security education. Local-hosted deployments still require competent support and deployment staff.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Hiring checklist; training plan; role-based training mapping.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Training logs across the period; onboarding records sampled by hire; annual training completion.
requirements.failure_modes: No role-based training; missed training; incomplete onboarding.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox aligns staffing and training to secure service delivery.

crosswalk.note: ISMS competence.
AIMS competence; AI-specific skills.

requirements.title: Accountability
requirements.requirement: The entity holds individuals accountable for their internal control responsibilities in pursuit of objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Security duties tied to performance reviews and policy attestations. If duties are informal, evidence usually weakens quickly.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Attestation flow; review process documentation.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Reviews completed across the period; sanctions log; policy attestations refreshed.
requirements.failure_modes: Policies without enforcement path; reviews omitted; no action on breaches.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox assigns accountability for control execution.

crosswalk.note: ISMS roles and accountability.

requirements.title: Quality information
requirements.requirement: The entity obtains or generates and uses relevant, quality information to support the functioning of internal control.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Key control data sources defined: asset lists, user lists, release records, incidents. For customer-hosted estates, distinguish PB-known data from customer-only data.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Inventory governance documentation; data owners named.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Current inventories maintained across the period; reconciliations.
requirements.failure_modes: Stale or conflicting inventories; local estates create inventory gaps.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox maintains quality operational and security information.

crosswalk.note: ISMS asset inventory + data classification.

requirements.title: Internal communication
requirements.requirement: The entity internally communicates information, including objectives and responsibilities for internal control, necessary to support the functioning of internal control.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Formal channels for policy publication and change communication. Evidence should show both publication and receipt.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Policy portal / publication record; change-notification process.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Acknowledgements across the period; change notices; meeting notes.
requirements.failure_modes: "Available on request" only; messages posted but not acknowledged.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox communicates security and operational duties internally.

crosswalk.note: ISMS communication.

requirements.title: External communication
requirements.requirement: The entity communicates with external parties regarding matters affecting the functioning of internal control.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Critical for shared-responsibility clarity. Customer-facing documentation for local responsibilities, support paths, and incident routing. Vague promises and missing customer duties are the central failure modes.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Security overview; support terms; shared-responsibility matrix template.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Customer documentation versions across the period; contract clauses; notifications sent.
requirements.failure_modes: Vague promises; missing customer duties; customer docs inconsistent with support reality.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox provides clear security and responsibility information to customers.

crosswalk.note: ISMS privacy / external disclosure overlap.
PIMS notice to data subjects.
POPIA openness condition (collection notice).

requirements.title: Objectives and commitments
requirements.requirement: The entity specifies objectives with sufficient clarity to enable the identification and assessment of risks relating to objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Contractual and operational commitments written precisely. Service commitments register; scope statement. Avoid vague promises like "fully secure by default".
requirements.type_i_evidence: Service commitments register; system requirements register.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Current commitments across the period; contract-to-control mapping.
requirements.failure_modes: Marketing claims exceed control reality; sales language outruns documented scope.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox documents service commitments and system requirements.

crosswalk.note: ISMS objectives.

requirements.title: Risk identification and analysis
requirements.requirement: The entity identifies risks to the achievement of its objectives across the entity and analyses risks as a basis for determining how risks should be managed.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Risk register including AI, support, deployment, and customer-hosting risks. Include prompt injection, data leakage, unsafe connector use, remote-support misuse, update tampering.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Risk methodology; current register.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Risk reviews across the period; treatment updates; closures.
requirements.failure_modes: One-off risk workshop only; stale register; actions never close.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox operates a documented risk-management process.

crosswalk.note: ISMS risk assessment.
AIMS AI risk methodology.
King V Principle 8 risk.

requirements.title: Fraud risk
requirements.requirement: The entity considers the potential for fraud in assessing risks to the achievement of objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Insider misuse, credential abuse, falsified approvals, audit-log tampering. For AI tooling, include prompt abuse and fabricated output workflows where relied upon.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Fraud scenarios in risk register; abuse cases.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Fraud-risk review notes across the period; investigation records (if any).
requirements.failure_modes: Fraud considered "not applicable"; AI misuse omitted from risk design.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox considers misuse and fraud risks in its control design.

requirements.title: Change risk
requirements.requirement: The entity identifies and assesses changes that could significantly impact the system of internal control.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Model changes, new connectors, new support tooling, new data flows trigger re-assessment. Many readiness programmes fail here after fast product iteration.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Change-risk trigger list; architecture / risk review gate documentation.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Change review packs across the period; reassessment records for material changes.
requirements.failure_modes: Changes shipped without reassessment; model / connector changes treated as routine.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox reassesses risk when its system changes materially.

crosswalk.note: ISMS planning of changes.
AIMS planning of changes including AI-specific change types.

requirements.title: Ongoing and separate monitoring
requirements.requirement: The entity selects, develops, and performs ongoing and / or separate evaluations to ascertain whether the components of internal control are present and functioning.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Combine management reviews, internal reviews, vulnerability testing, periodic independent assessments. Monitoring should cover product, support environment, and customer-facing documentation.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Monitoring calendar; internal review plan.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Review minutes across the period; pen-test results; internal audit notes; samples often shown monthly or periodic.
requirements.failure_modes: Monitoring cadence slips; monitoring undefined or ad hoc.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox performs ongoing and periodic control monitoring.

crosswalk.note: ISMS monitoring, measurement, analysis and evaluation.
King V periodic assurance over technology.

requirements.title: Deficiency communication and remediation
requirements.requirement: The entity evaluates and communicates internal control deficiencies in a timely manner to those parties responsible for taking corrective action, including senior management and the board as appropriate.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Formal remediation tracker with severity, owner, due date, and closure evidence. Auditor confidence rises sharply when closure evidence is clean.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Issue tracker workflow; severity rules and closure validation pattern.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Remediation tickets across the period; ageing reports; closure approvals.
requirements.failure_modes: Findings vanish into chats / email; open issues without owner or closure proof.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox tracks and remediates control deficiencies.

crosswalk.note: ISMS nonconformity and corrective action.

requirements.title: Select and design control activities
requirements.requirement: The entity selects and develops control activities that contribute to the mitigation of risks to the achievement of objectives to acceptable levels.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Risk-to-control mapping across product, support, and corporate environments. Good place to align security, support, and AI quality governance in one matrix.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Control matrix; risk-control linkage and owner assignment.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Evidence across mapped controls demonstrating execution.
requirements.failure_modes: Matrix exists but controls not performed; controls listed but not risk-linked.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox maps controls to assessed risks.

requirements.title: Technology general controls
requirements.requirement: The entity selects and develops general control activities over technology to support the achievement of objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Build pipelines, repositories, secrets, environments, release tooling. Usually a high-weight audit area for software vendors.
requirements.type_i_evidence: SDLC; secrets management; repo protections.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Pipeline approvals across the period; environment separation evidence; secret rotations.
requirements.failure_modes: Shared credentials; no branch protections; repo bypasses; unreviewed changes; stale secrets.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox operates technology controls over the systems that support its service.

crosswalk.note: ISMS secure SDLC + secure architecture + secure coding + security testing.
AIMS responsible design and development.

requirements.title: Policies and procedures
requirements.requirement: The entity deploys control activities through policies that establish what is expected and procedures that put policies into action.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Convert policy to step-by-step SOPs for provisioning, support, change, incident, and release. If only policies exist, Type II evidence usually collapses.
documented_evidence: SOPs with version history + review records
requirements.type_i_evidence: SOP set; periodic review records.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: SOP-linked tickets / execution logs across the period; staff don't rely on memory.
requirements.failure_modes: Policy-only programme; staff rely on memory or chats.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox operationalises documented policies through repeatable procedures.

requirements.title: Access security architecture
requirements.requirement: The entity implements logical access security software, infrastructure, and architectures over protected information assets to protect them from security events to meet the entity's objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Inventory product nodes, model artefacts, vectors, connectors, admin APIs, support tools. Require MFA and segmentation where feasible. Customer-hosted systems may implement part via CUECs.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Inventory; auth standard; network diagrams; segmentation design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: MFA state samples across the period; inventory reconciliations; config samples; stale-key reviews.
requirements.failure_modes: Unknown assets; MFA gaps; stale keys.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox designs access protections for product and support components.

crosswalk.note: ISMS access control + privileged access + secure authentication.

requirements.title: User provisioning and deprovisioning
requirements.requirement: Prior to issuing system credentials and granting system access, the entity registers and authorises new internal and external users whose access is administered by the entity.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Ticketed approvals; named accounts; prompt deprovisioning of local and support access. Distinguish customer-local admins from PB remote-support users.
requirements.type_i_evidence: JML workflow; ticket form.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Access tickets across the period; terminations sampled; deprovisioning timeliness.
requirements.failure_modes: Shared accounts; no removal step; late deprovisioning; missing approval.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox operates controlled access onboarding and offboarding processes.

crosswalk.note: ISMS identity management + authentication information + access rights.

requirements.title: Role-based access and least privilege
requirements.requirement: The entity authorises, modifies, or removes access to data, software, functions, and other protected information assets based on roles, responsibilities, or the system design and changes, giving consideration to the concepts of least privilege and segregation of duties.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Role matrix for support, engineering, deployment, and customer admins; review privileged access. Support break-glass access should be rare, approved, and logged.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Role matrix; admin model; privileged review design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Access review records across the period; admin lists; SoD checks.
requirements.failure_modes: Flat admin rights; no SoD; privilege creep.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox aligns system access to roles and least-privilege principles.

crosswalk.note: ISMS privileged access rights.

requirements.title: Physical access restrictions
requirements.requirement: The entity restricts physical access to facilities and protected information assets to authorised personnel to meet the entity's objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: PB sites: badge / visitor / media controls. Customer-hosted nodes: minimum customer responsibilities defined as CUECs. In pure customer-hosted scope, much execution may sit with the customer.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Physical security standard; visitor controls; deployment prerequisites.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Badge logs and visitor logs across the period; periodic review.
requirements.failure_modes: No physical-scope decision; no review of old badges; visitor gaps.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox defines and operates physical security controls relevant to its scope.

crosswalk.note: ISMS physical entry controls + securing offices.

requirements.title: Secure asset disposal
requirements.requirement: The entity discontinues logical and physical protections over physical assets only after the ability to read or recover data and software from those assets has been diminished and is no longer required to meet the entity's objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Secure wipe / dekey procedures for appliances, RMA devices, removable media, and support exports. Especially important for field hardware and removable support media.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Disposal / RMA procedure; crypto-erase or wipe validation.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Wipe / dekey evidence across the period; disposal records.
requirements.failure_modes: Disposal undefined for field hardware; missing sanitisation proof.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox uses secure disposal and sanitisation processes.

crosswalk.note: ISMS secure disposal or re-use of equipment.

requirements.title: Boundary protection
requirements.requirement: The entity implements logical access security measures to protect against threats from sources outside its system boundaries.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Disable remote access by default; require customer approval, MFA, session logging, and network restrictions. Major shared-responsibility area for on-prem customers.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Network baseline; remote-support policy; default-off remote support and MFA design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Firewall samples; tunnel approvals; session logs; permanent-exception reviews.
requirements.failure_modes: Permanent always-on support tunnel; permanent exceptions; undocumented remote sessions.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox uses controlled boundary protection for remote access and administration.

crosswalk.note: ISMS network security + segregation + network services.

requirements.title: Secure transmission and removal
requirements.requirement: The entity restricts the transmission, movement, and removal of information to authorised internal and external users and processes, and protects it during transmission, movement, or removal to meet the entity's objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Encrypt exports, support bundles, backups, and updates; sign packages; document offline- transfer methods. Air-gapped deployments need explicit offline controls.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Transfer standard; encryption requirements; signed updates and offline media procedures.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Signed releases across the period; export logs; encryption-evidence samples.
requirements.failure_modes: Ad hoc file transfer methods; informal transfers; unsigned packages.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox uses protected mechanisms for transmitting and removing data.

crosswalk.note: ISMS information transfer + cryptography.

requirements.title: Malicious software prevention
requirements.requirement: The entity implements controls to prevent or detect and act upon the introduction of unauthorised or malicious software to meet the entity's objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Scan build artefacts, endpoints, support uploads, imported packages; restrict installs. In customer environments, some execution may be customer-owned.
requirements.type_i_evidence: EDR / AV standard; scan process; build / package scanning design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Scan logs across the period; EDR status; agent activity.
requirements.failure_modes: Reliance on customer AV only; agents inactive; packages unscanned.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox applies malware and unauthorised-software controls across relevant environments.

crosswalk.note: ISMS protection against malware.

requirements.title: Vulnerability and configuration monitoring
requirements.requirement: To meet its objectives, the entity uses detection and monitoring procedures to identify changes to configurations that result in the introduction of new vulnerabilities, and susceptibilities to newly discovered vulnerabilities.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Maintain hardening baselines; run infra, container, dependency, and configuration scans; issue advisories to customers. Telemetry-off customers may need to self-run some controls with PB guidance.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Hardening baseline; scan schedule; FIM and remediation SLA design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Scan reports across the period; remediation tickets; FIM alert handling.
requirements.failure_modes: Scans run but no response design; findings linger.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox monitors vulnerabilities and configuration drift relevant to its system.

crosswalk.note: ISMS management of technical vulnerabilities + configuration management.

requirements.title: System and anomaly monitoring
requirements.requirement: The entity monitors system components and the operation of those components for anomalies that are indicative of malicious acts, natural disasters, and errors affecting the entity's ability to meet its objectives; anomalies are analysed to determine whether they represent security events.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Define minimum log set, SIEM integration points, alert catalogue, support-event logging. If customer disables telemetry, PB should clearly describe what it cannot observe.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Logging standard; alert matrix; minimum log list.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Alert reviews across the period; dashboards; investigations.
requirements.failure_modes: "We log everything" with no specifics; alert fatigue; no closed-loop review.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox defines monitoring requirements and operates its own in-scope monitoring controls.

crosswalk.note: ISMS logging + monitoring + clock synchronisation.
AIMS AI event logs.

requirements.title: Security event evaluation
requirements.requirement: The entity evaluates security events to determine whether they could or have resulted in a failure of the entity to meet its objectives (security incidents) and, if so, takes actions to prevent or address such failures.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Severity matrix and decision tree; include customer-impact and AI-misuse analysis. Customer- originated events still need to be routed into PB support / PSIRT decisions.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Triage workflow; severity matrix; impact-assessment design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Triage tickets across the period; severity records; escalation logs.
requirements.failure_modes: No event-to-incident decision rule; events never escalated; severity inconsistent.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox evaluates security events through a documented triage process.

crosswalk.note: ISMS incident management planning + assessment.

requirements.title: Incident response execution
requirements.requirement: The entity responds to identified security incidents by executing a defined incident-response program to understand, contain, remediate, and communicate security incidents, as appropriate.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Joint-ready playbooks for product, support, customer-site, and subservice incidents. Include joint-response roles and evidence boundaries for customer-hosted nodes.
requirements.type_i_evidence: IR plan; playbooks; contact trees; tabletop plan.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Incident files across the period; tabletop records; notifications.
requirements.failure_modes: Incident plan too generic; plan exists but not followed.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox maintains a documented incident-response programme.

crosswalk.note: ISMS incident response + learning from incidents.
POPIA security safeguards including s 22 breach notification.

requirements.title: Recovery from incidents
requirements.requirement: The entity identifies, develops, and implements activities to recover from identified security incidents.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Restoration procedures, post-incident reviews, hardening follow-up. If customer environment is primary recovery domain, PB still documents its own obligations and dependencies.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Recovery runbooks; PIR process; root-cause and prevention loop design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: PIRs across the period; recovery evidence; change records linked to incidents.
requirements.failure_modes: No structured post-incident review; no root cause or recurrence-prevention action.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox uses recovery and lessons-learned processes after incidents.

crosswalk.note: ISMS learning from information security incidents.

requirements.title: Change management
requirements.requirement: The entity authorises, designs, develops or acquires, configures, documents, tests, approves, and implements changes to infrastructure, data, software, and procedures to meet its objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Cover code, models, prompts, connectors, configs, installers, documentation, and support scripts. Model updates and prompt / config changes must be treated as controlled changes.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Change policy; release workflow; rollback design; emergency change path.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: PR approvals across the period; tickets; test evidence; release notes; rollback drills.
requirements.failure_modes: Informal hotfixes; emergency changes undocumented; weak rollback.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox manages system changes through documented approval and testing practices.

crosswalk.note: ISMS change management.
AIMS planning of changes + documentation of AI system development.

requirements.title: Business disruption mitigation
requirements.requirement: The entity identifies, selects, and develops risk mitigation activities for risks arising from potential business disruptions.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Continuity plans for support, releases, credential recovery, repos, and critical teams. Define customer recovery assumptions. Availability execution may sit mainly with the customer, but PB still owns assumptions and support recovery.
requirements.type_i_evidence: BCP / DR plan; dependency analysis; BIA and tested recovery assumptions.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: BCP reviews across the period; test results; dependency updates.
requirements.suggested_observation_period: 6-12 months; at least one test cycle preferred
requirements.failure_modes: Plan stops at "call support"; plan stale; no exercise.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox maintains continuity and disruption-response planning.

crosswalk.note: ISMS information security during disruption + ICT readiness for business continuity.

requirements.title: Vendor and business partner risk
requirements.requirement: The entity assesses and manages risks associated with vendors and business partners.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Govern cloud relays, support tooling, telemetry tools, open-source and model suppliers, code- signing services. Separate PB vendors from customer-selected local infrastructure vendors.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Vendor process; contract requirements; tiering; review cadence design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Due diligence records across the period; annual reviews; issue logs.
requirements.failure_modes: No definition of critical vendor; critical vendor not assessed; contract gaps.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox manages security risk in relevant supplier relationships.

crosswalk.note: ISMS supplier relationships, contracts, ICT supply chain, monitoring of supplier services.
AIMS supplier responsibility allocation.
POPIA operator duties (ss 20-21).

requirements.title: Capacity monitoring
requirements.requirement: The entity maintains, monitors, and evaluates current processing capacity and use of system components (infrastructure, data, and software) to manage capacity demand and to enable the implementation of additional capacity to help meet its objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Scope only if PB makes capacity or throughput commitments. In many local deployments, customer owns runtime capacity.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Capacity procedure; forecasting and thresholds.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Utilisation trends across the period; threshold actions; capacity reviews.
requirements.failure_modes: Availability promise with no metrics; capacity reviews skipped.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox maintains capacity-planning controls where availability commitments apply.

crosswalk.note: ISMS capacity management.

requirements.title: Environmental protection, backups, and recovery infrastructure
requirements.requirement: The entity authorises, designs, develops or acquires, implements, operates, approves, maintains, and monitors environmental protections, software, data backup processes, and recovery infrastructure to meet its objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Define required local backup, environmental, and restore architecture. Often a major customer- owned area for on-prem installations.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Backup standard; restore standard; environmental controls; recovery-infra design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Backup logs across the period; failure alerts; restore evidence.
requirements.failure_modes: Backups exist but restores unproven.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox documents and operates recoverability controls relevant to scope.

crosswalk.note: ISMS information backup.

requirements.title: Recovery-plan testing
requirements.requirement: The entity tests recovery plan procedures supporting system recovery to meet its objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Run restore tests for PB-managed elements; document customer test expectations. Valuable differentiator for PB maturity.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Test schedule; scenario-based restore exercises design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Restore test outputs across the period; exercise reports.
requirements.cadence: Periodic; usually annual or more often
requirements.failure_modes: Never-tested DR; tests not executed; failures not fixed.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox tests relevant recovery procedures and documents expected customer tests.

crosswalk.note: ISMS ICT readiness for business continuity.

requirements.title: Confidential information identification and retention
requirements.requirement: The entity identifies and maintains confidential information to meet the entity's objectives related to confidentiality.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Classify customer documents, support bundles, model artefacts, and secrets. Define retention rules. Often worth scoping even without full Privacy.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Classification policy; retention schedule; support-data and secrets coverage.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Labelling samples across the period; retention application records.
requirements.failure_modes: Classification too generic; data stored in wrong class; no retention enforcement.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox classifies and protects confidential information according to policy.

crosswalk.note: ISMS classification of information + labelling.
POPIA purpose specification and retention.

requirements.title: Confidential information disposal
requirements.requirement: The entity disposes of confidential information to meet the entity's objectives related to confidentiality.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Extend disposal rules to support exports, logs, snapshots, update artefacts, retired devices. Keep customer deletion responsibilities explicit where data stays entirely local.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Disposal process; automated purge plus evidence design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Purge records across the period; deletion tickets.
requirements.failure_modes: No destruction trigger; retention exceeded; deletion not evidenced.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox uses secure disposal for confidential information.

crosswalk.note: ISMS information deletion.
POPIA retention destruction.

requirements.title: Processing specifications and definitions
requirements.requirement: The entity obtains or generates, uses, and communicates relevant, quality information regarding the objectives related to processing, including definitions of data processed and product and service specifications, to support the use of products and services.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Only scope if PB makes bounded processing commitments. Do not scope generic model truthfulness here.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Specs; interfaces; acceptance criteria.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Approved specs across the period; test updates.
requirements.failure_modes: Vague correctness claims; commitments change without control update.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox defines objective processing specifications where relevant.

requirements.title: Input controls
requirements.requirement: The entity implements policies and procedures over system inputs, including controls over completeness, accuracy, and timeliness, to result in products, services, and reporting to meet the entity's objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Apply to imports, connectors, user uploads, batch jobs, support actions where PB makes commitments. Shared when customers feed local data directly.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Validation rules; exception workflow design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Validation logs across the period; rejected-input records.
requirements.failure_modes: Inputs accepted without validation; bad inputs bypass checks.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox applies controlled input-validation measures where processing-integrity commitments apply.

requirements.title: Processing controls
requirements.requirement: The entity implements policies and procedures over system processing to result in products, services, and reporting to meet the entity's objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Job controls, exception handling, reconciliations, regression tests for deterministic workflows. Most suitable for deterministic pipelines, not open-ended answer quality.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Workflow definitions; reconciliation or regression-test design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Job logs across the period; reconciliations; exception tickets.
requirements.failure_modes: Black-box reliance without controls; silent failures; no exception follow-up.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox uses controlled processing procedures for scoped workflows.

crosswalk.note: AIMS V&V and robustness for AI-system processing.

requirements.title: Output controls
requirements.requirement: The entity implements policies and procedures to deliver output, including its completeness, accuracy, and timeliness, to meet the entity's objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Define output recipients, export approvals, audit reports, error corrections. Good fit for audit exports, workflow actions, or decision-support packages.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Output distribution procedure; intended-recipient controls.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Delivery logs across the period; review checks; export approvals.
requirements.failure_modes: No control over exports or reports; wrong recipient; incomplete export.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox protects and controls output distribution where such commitments are made.

requirements.title: Storage controls for items in process
requirements.requirement: The entity implements policies and procedures to store inputs, items in processing, and outputs completely, accurately, and timely to meet the entity's objectives.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Control staging areas, vector stores, caches, output archives, audit records. Often overlaps with Confidentiality and Availability.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Storage / archival standard; integrity checks design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Archival logs across the period; integrity checks.
requirements.failure_modes: Temporary stores unmanaged; staging data unmanaged.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox protects and manages stored processing artefacts where relevant.

requirements.title: Privacy notice
requirements.requirement: The entity provides notice to data subjects about its privacy practices to meet the entity's objectives related to privacy. The notice is updated and communicated to data subjects in a timely manner for changes to the entity's privacy practices.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Scope only if PB's in-scope system collects personal information from data subjects or acts as controller / processor in a way that requires notice. Customer-only data collection may make this not relevant for the system scope.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Privacy notice; change-control workflow.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Notice versions across the period; publication evidence.
requirements.failure_modes: Privacy scoped with no notice basis; privacy practices change silently.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox documents privacy practices for in-scope personal information handling where applicable.

crosswalk.note: PIMS controllers privacy notice.
POPIA openness / collection notice.

requirements.title: Choice and consent
requirements.requirement: The entity communicates choices available regarding the collection, use, retention, disclosure, and disposal of personal information to data subjects and obtains and documents consent or authority for the collection, use, retention, disclosure, and disposal of personal information.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Scope only where PB collects or uses personal information directly for support, telemetry, or account services. Often mostly customer-owned in local deployments.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Consent process; recorded lawful-basis logic.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Consent logs across the period; lawful-basis records.
requirements.failure_modes: Consent implied but undocumented; missing consent trail.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox documents consent or lawful-basis handling for in-scope personal information where applicable.

crosswalk.note: POPIA processing limitation including s 11 consent / justification.

requirements.title: Collection limitation
requirements.requirement: Personal information is collected consistent with the entity's objectives related to privacy.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Minimise support, telemetry, and admin-personal-data collection. This may be out of scope if customers, not PB, collect end-user data.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Data minimisation standard; telemetry / support-data field reviews.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Field inventories across the period; telemetry reviews.
requirements.failure_modes: Over-collection "just in case"; PI fields creep over time.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox applies data-minimisation principles to in-scope personal information.

crosswalk.note: POPIA minimisation principle.

requirements.title: Use, retention, and disposal of personal information
requirements.requirement: The entity limits the use of personal information to the purposes identified in the entity's objectives related to privacy; the entity retains personal information consistent with the entity's objectives related to privacy; the entity securely disposes of personal information.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Define precise uses and retention for support tickets, telemetry, logs, account data, exports. Strong overlap with Confidentiality and records management.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Privacy retention / deletion process; purpose-based restrictions and purge workflow.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Purpose checks across the period; retention reports; purge evidence.
requirements.failure_modes: Retention undefined; data kept too long; unauthorised use.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox restricts use and retention of in-scope personal information.

crosswalk.note: POPIA purpose specification + retention.

requirements.title: Access and amendment rights
requirements.requirement: The entity provides data subjects with access to their personal information for review and, upon request, provides physical or electronic copies of that information; the entity corrects, amends, or appends personal information based on information provided by data subjects and communicates such information to third parties.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Only scope if PB is responsible for responding to those requests for in-scope PI. Frequently customer-owned for customer data.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Request workflow; identity verification, denial logic design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Request log across the period; responses.
requirements.failure_modes: No ownership for responses; no identity verification; overdue responses.
requirements.external_safe_phrasing: PrivateBox maintains a documented process for in-scope personal-information access or correction requests where applicable.

crosswalk.note: POPIA data subject participation.

requirements.title: Third-party disclosure, breach notice, and accounting
requirements.requirement: The entity discloses personal information to third parties consistent with the entity's objectives related to privacy and notifies data subjects of incidents involving personal information consistent with the entity's objectives related to privacy.
requirements.pb_interpretation: Privacy terms into vendor contracts; breach notification decision paths and records. Particularly relevant if PB uses telemetry, support processors, or identity services.
requirements.type_i_evidence: Vendor clauses; breach notice process; disclosure log design.
requirements.type_ii_evidence: Contracts across the period; incident records; notices.
requirements.failure_modes: Privacy obligations absent from third-party contracts; processor not notified; no record of disclosure.
